Vet ClinPath — Complete Study Hub v10

8 body-system tabs · Liver & Biochem · GI/Pancreas/Muscle/Minerals · Hematology · Hemostasis · Kidney & UA · Electrolytes/Acid-Base · Endocrine · Toxicology · Cases · 30-Q Quiz · Study Qs

🫀 Liver & Biochemistry: Seven sub-topics — start with Overview for the critical framework (enzyme tests ask WHERE, function tests ask HOW MUCH), then use the remaining sub-tabs by topic. The sub-tabs follow lecture order: Proteins → Enzymes → the 4 categories of disease → Function tests → PSS → Bilirubin.
The critical rule: enzyme tests tell you WHERE the problem is. Liver FUNCTION tests tell you HOW MUCH liver is left. Always read both.

Two types of liver tests — completely different questions

Enzyme tests — damage/obstruction

ALT ↑ AST ↑ SDH ↑
→ hepatocellular damage
ALP ↑ GGT ↑
→ cholestasis

Function tests — what liver can't do

Clearance: bilirubin, bile acids, ammonia
Synthesis: albumin, BUN, glucose, cholesterol, clotting factors
⚠️ Enzymes are NOT a reflection of liver function! Massive ALT with normal albumin/BUN = liver is damaged but still working. Minimal ALT with low albumin/BUN = end-stage failure.

4 liver disease categories and how they connect

1
Hepatocellular injury → ALT ↑, AST ↑, SDH ↑ → secondary intrahepatic cholestasis (ALP/GGT/bili ↑)
2
Cholestasis → ALP ↑, GGT ↑, bilirubin ↑, bile acids ↑ → bile acid detergent effect → secondary hepatocyte damage (ALT ↑)
3
Functional mass loss (>70% gone) → albumin ↓, BUN ↓, glucose ↓, cholesterol ↓, bilirubin ↑, bile acids ↑, ammonia ↑
4
Portal shunting → bile acids ↑↑, ammonia ↑ → hepatic atrophy → functional loss

Toxicology GI toxicants — key concepts NEW

Iron Poisoning

Phase I: GI
Phase III: Liver failure

Diagnosis: ↑ serum Fe, ↑ transferrin saturation, ↑ ferritin. Treatment: deferoxamine chelation.

Urea Toxicosis

Ruminants only
↑ Rumen NH₃ → hyperammonemia

Treatment: cold water + vinegar → traps NH₃ as NH₄⁺ (can't cross GIT). "Sudden death" near urea source.

Salt Poisoning

Hypernatremia >145 mmol/L
Pigs: eosinophilic cuffs

INDIRECT (most common): salt → water deprivation → sudden rehydration → cerebral edema. Treat SLOWLY.

Exocrine pancreas — the two disorders NEW

Pancreatitis — too much enzyme activity

AMS ↑LPS ↑PLI ↑TLI ↑

Enzymes activate inside pancreas → autodigestion. Ripple effects: liver damage, cholestasis, electrolyte derangements, DIC.

EPI — too little enzyme secretion

TLI ↓↓PLI ↓

Loss of >90% exocrine mass → maldigestion → weight loss, steatorrhea, ↓ cobalamin.

Species quick reference

SpeciesHepatocellularCholestasisMusclePancreas
DogALTALPAST, CKcPLI, cTLI; AMS/LPS unreliable alone
CatALTGGT (more sensitive than ALP)AST, CKfPLI, fTLI; avoid AMS/LPS
Large animalsSDHGGTALT, CKeTLI (horse); AMS/LPS unreliable

Key rule: evaluate albumin and globulin separately

Total protein alone hides the story. Albumin is made exclusively by the liver. Globulins come from the immune system and acute phase response. They can move in opposite directions, making TP look "normal."

Albumin = negative acute phase protein → decreases with inflammation. Globulins = positive acute phase proteins → increase with inflammation.

Hypoproteinemia — what's low and why

PatternMechanismTop differentials
↓ Albumin + ↓ GlobulinLosing all proteins togetherBlood loss, protein-losing enteropathy (PLE), severe burns/exudative lesions
↓ Albumin onlyLiver can't make it OR kidney losing it selectivelyLiver failure (only site of synthesis), glomerular disease, inflammation (negative APP)
↓ Globulin onlyImmune system failureFailure of passive transfer (neonates), immunodeficiency (inherited or acquired)

Hyperproteinemia — what's high and why

PatternTop cause
↑ Albumin + ↑ GlobulinDehydration (concentrating everything)
↑ Globulin onlyInflammation (acute phase proteins α/β), chronic antigen stimulation (γ-globulins), lymphoma/myeloma
HyperfibrinogenemiaDehydration and/or inflammation (plasma protein only — serum protein not affected)

A:G ratio (reference ~0.9–1.8)

↑ A:G ratio
  • ↑ albumin (dehydration)
  • ↓ globulin (immunodeficiency)
↓ A:G ratio
  • ↓ albumin (liver failure, glomerular loss)
  • ↑ globulin (chronic inflammation)
  • Classic: chronic inflammation in cattle
"Normal" A:G can be misleading — both could be elevated (dehydration) or both low (PLE). Always look at individual values.

Half-life — shortest to longest (dog)

CK
<few hrs
→
SDH
<12 hrs
→
AST
<24 hrs (dog)
1.5 hrs (cat)
7–8 days (horse)
→
ALT
60 hrs (dog)
→
ALP
72 hrs (dog)
→
GGT
3 days (dog)

All 5 liver enzymes at a glance

EnzymeTypeSource↑ causesKey nuances
ALTleakageLiver >> muscle (SA); Muscle only (LA)Hepatocellular damage · steroids/anticonvulsants (dog) · hyperthyroidism (cat) · muscle damageNOT useful in large animals. >3–5× URL = hepatocellular
ASTleakageLiver + muscle (non-specific)Hepatocellular damage · muscle damage · steroids · hemolysisPair with CK and ALT to find source.
SDHleakageLiver (highly specific ALL species)Hepatocellular damage (all species)Half-life <12 hrs. Labile. Enzyme of choice in large animals.
ALPinductionL-ALP (liver), B-ALP (bone), C-ALP (dogs only!)Cholestasis · bone lysis/turnover · corticosteroids (dogs) · hepatic lipidosis (cats)Feline: ALP > GGT = lipidosis; GGT > ALP = cholestasis.
GGTinductionLiver (hepatocytes + biliary). Renal → urine only. Mammary → milk only.Cholestasis · biliary hyperplasia · drug therapy · toxins (LA) · colostrumMore sensitive for cholestasis in cats and large animals.

AST — always pair with CK and ALT

AST ↑CKALTInterpretation
Yes↑↑Normal or mild ↑Muscle injury (primary source)
YesNormal↑↑Liver injury (primary source)
YesNormalNormalPrior muscle injury (CK cleared) OR very early/acute liver
These 4 categories cause each other. Hepatocellular damage → cholestasis. Cholestasis → hepatocyte damage. Always read the full panel.

1. Hepatocellular injury

ALT ↑↑AST ↑SDH ↑

Hepatocyte membrane damage → cytoplasmic enzymes leak.

Causes: hypoxia · toxins · lipidosis · DM · hyperthyroidism · neoplasia · inflammatory hepatitis · drugs

Secondary: intrahepatic cholestasis → ALP ↑, GGT ↑, bili ↑

2. Cholestasis

ALP ↑↑GGT ↑Bili ↑Bile acids ↑

Intrahepatic: lipidosis · DM · Cushing's · choleliths · neoplasia · hepatitis · pancreatitis

Extrahepatic: bile duct obstruction · pancreatitis · choleliths · neoplasia · flukes

Secondary: bile acid detergent effect → ALT ↑, AST ↑

3. Decreased functional mass

Albumin ↓BUN ↓Glu ↓Chol ↓
Bili ↑Bile acids ↑Ammonia ↑

Needs >70% hepatocyte loss. Enzymes may be paradoxically LOW in end-stage.

4. Portal shunting

Bile acids ↑↑Ammonia ↑

Congenital: young patient, single vessel, small smooth liver, usually NOT icteric, microcytic RBCs.

Acquired: multiple vessels, from cirrhosis, nodular liver, more likely icteric.

Liver function tests assess what the liver actually DOES — separate from enzyme elevations.

Bile acids — best functional test for PSS/hepatic insufficiency

Synthesized from cholesterol in liver. Recycled via enterohepatic circulation. Postprandial sample challenges the liver most.

Reason bile acids ↑Mechanism
Portosystemic shuntPortal blood bypasses liver → bile acids not extracted
Functional mass lossFewer hepatocytes to reabsorb bile acids
CholestasisReflux of bile acids back into blood
GI disease / malabsorption↓ bile acid absorption → BAs ↓ — can MASK hepatic insufficiency!
⚠️ If patient already has hyperbilirubinemia from cholestasis, bile acids testing is redundant. Not useful in horses (no gallbladder).

Ammonia — PSS and hepatic failure marker

⚠️ Sample handling critical: heparin or EDTA plasma, chilled on ice, processed within 30 min. Do NOT perform tolerance test if fasting ammonia is already elevated — CNS toxicity risk!
Ammonia ↑ — reasonExample
Decreased clearance — PSSPortal blood bypasses liver
Decreased clearance — hepatic mass loss<30% functional mass remaining
Increased production — cattleUrea toxicosis (excess dietary urea → ammonia in rumen)
Increased production — horsesIntestinal disease → abnormal bacterial flora

Synthetic products — what a failing liver can't make

ProductFinding with liver failureKey nuance
Albumin↓Liver is the ONLY site. Long half-life (7–10 days) → NOT low in acute liver disease.
BUN↓Can't convert ammonia → urea → medullary washout → PU/PD
Glucose↓↓ glycogen stores, ↓ gluconeogenesis, ↓ insulin clearance.
Cholesterol↓ usuallyBUT cholestasis causes ↑ cholesterol — opposing effects may cancel!
Clotting factorsPT/PTT prolongedCholestasis → ↓ bile → ↓ vitamin K absorption → ↓ factors II, VII, IX, X
Congenital vs. acquired PSS is a high-yield distinction.

Congenital PSS

  • Single large vessel (intrahepatic or extrahepatic)
  • Young patient (often <1 year)
  • Small liver, smooth edges
  • Neurologic signs: head pressing, ataxia, seizures
  • Microcytic RBCs
  • Ammonium biurate crystals in urine
Bile acids ↑↑Ammonia ↑
Albumin ↓BUN ↓Glucose ↓

Usually NOT icteric. Liver enzymes minimally elevated.

Acquired PSS

  • Multiple small tortuous vessels (always extrahepatic)
  • Older patient
  • Small nodular liver (cirrhosis)
  • Portal hypertension → shunts form secondarily
  • Ammonium biurate crystals in urine
Bile acids ↑↑Ammonia ↑
Bilirubin ↑ALT ↑AST ↑

More likely icteric. Liver enzymes more elevated.

Bilirubin metabolism — step by step

1
RBC → macrophage → Hgb → heme → biliverdin → UCB (unconjugated). Binds albumin. NOT water-soluble.
2
UCB-albumin → hepatocyte → glucuronidation → CB (conjugated). Water-soluble. CAN cross glomerulus → bilirubinuria.
3
⚠️ Rate-limiting step: secretion of CB into bile canaliculi. Bilirubinuria often appears BEFORE hyperbilirubinemia.
4
No bile reaching gut = acholic (gray) feces = extrahepatic obstruction clue.

3 categories of hyperbilirubinemia

TypeWhere problem isMain causesDominant bili typeKey panel clue
Pre-hepaticToo much UCB producedHemolysisUCB (indirect)Regenerative anemia, spherocytes
HepaticLiver can't uptake/conjugate/excreteHepatocyte loss, anorexia (horses!), intrahepatic cholestasisMixed↑ALT/AST + ↑ALP/GGT
Post-hepaticBile duct obstructed after conjugationCholeliths, pancreatitis, neoplasia, flukesCB (direct)↑ALP/GGT ↑↑ + acholic feces
In horses and cattle: UCB always dominates. Anorexia alone can cause UCB up to 5–10 mg/dL — physiologic!
🔬 GI, Pancreas, Muscle & Minerals: Exocrine pancreas enzymes (amylase/lipase/PLI/TLI) and pancreatitis lab findings; muscle enzyme patterns (CK, AST, myoglobin); and calcium/phosphorus/magnesium regulation with full hypo/hypercalcemia differential tables.
🔬 Lecture 4: Exocrine pancreas. Two conditions: pancreatitis (too much enzyme activity) and EPI (too little). AMS and LPS are non-specific leakage enzymes. PLI and TLI are the clinically reliable species-specific tests.

The 4 pancreatic enzyme tests

TestTypePancreas-specific?Cleared byBest used for
AMSleakageNOKidneys, liverNon-specific screening; avoid in cats
LPSleakageNOKidneysNon-specific; elevated by steroids; avoid in cats
PLIimmunoassayYES ✓NOT affected by GFRTest of choice for pancreatitis
TLIimmunoassayYES ✓Kidneys (↓ GFR → ↑ TLI)Test of choice for EPI
⚠️ AMS and LPS: ≥3–4× URL supports pancreatitis. Normal values do NOT rule it out. Glucocorticoids can raise lipase up to 5× URL without true pancreatitis.

Pancreatitis vs EPI — rapid comparison

ParameterPancreatitisEPI
PLI↑↑↓
TLI↑ (inconsistent)↓↓ (diagnostic)
AMS / LPS↑ ≥3–4× URLNormal/Low
Calcium↓ (saponification)Normal
Cobalamin (B12)Normal↓ (malabsorption)
Diagnostic test of choicePLITLI
💪 Lecture 5 — Muscle: CK is the enzyme of choice for skeletal muscle damage. ALT means muscle in large animals but liver in small animals.

CK — Creatine Kinase

FeatureDetail
TypeLeakage enzyme (cytoplasmic)
SourcesSkeletal muscle, cardiac muscle, brain
Half-life2–4 hours (very short)
Peak after injury6–12 hours
Returns to RI1–2 days with single injury event

The 3-pattern rule

CKASTALTInterpretation
↑↑↑Normal/mildCurrent muscle damage
Normal↑NormalPrevious muscle injury (CK cleared)
Normal↑↑Hepatocellular injury
⚠️ Large animals: ALT ↑ = muscle first. Small animals: ALT ↑ = liver first. Always check species!
🦴 Lecture 6 — Ca, P, Mg: Always evaluate Ca and P together (Ca × P product). Measure ionized calcium (iCa) when total Ca is abnormal. PTH, Vitamin D, and Calcitonin are master regulators.

Hormone Regulation Summary

HormoneBoneKidneyNet effect
PTH↑ Ca + PO₄ mobilization↑ Ca reabsorption, ↓ PO₄↑ Ca ↓ PO₄
Calcitonin↓ osteoclast activity↓ Ca + PO₄ resorption↓ Ca ↓ PO₄
Vitamin D↑ Ca + PO₄ mobilization↑ Ca resorption + ↑ intestinal Ca + PO₄↑ Ca ↑ PO₄

Hypercalcemia — GOSHDARNIT mnemonic

LetterConditionKey findings
GGranulomatous diseaseMacrophages produce active Vit D; ↑Ca, ↑P
OOsteolytic diseaseBone tumors releasing Ca
SSpuriousLipemia, dehydration — repeat on fresh sample
HHyperparathyroidism (primary)↑Ca, ↓P, ↑PTH; parathyroid neoplasia
DVitamin D toxicity↑Ca, ↑P; rodenticides, supplements, plants
AAddison's disease30–50% of dogs; ↑Ca, ↓Na:K, ↑K
RRenal diseaseMost common cause in horses (↑Ca, ↓P)
NNeoplasia / HCMMost common in dogs; ↑Ca, ↓P, ↑PTHrp
IIdiopathicMost common in cats; diagnosis of exclusion
TToxin / young animalsYoung large-breed dogs: ↑Ca, ↑P, ↑ALP (bone growth)

Hypocalcemia — MILD PRAISE mnemonic

LetterConditionKey notes
MMagnesium deficiency↓Mg impairs PTH secretion and action
IInjury to tissuesFat saponification (pancreatitis)
LLactation / eclampsiaDogs: 3–5 wk postpartum; Cows: milk fever 1–4 days post-calving
DD vitamin deficiency↓ GI absorption of Ca and P
PPancreatitisFat saponification + hypoalbuminemia
HHypoparathyroidism↓PTH, ↓Ca, ↑P
RRenal disease↓ Vit D activation, ↑P; 2° hyperparathyroidism
AAlbumin deficiencyMost common cause; pseudohypocalcemia — iCa normal!
IIntake / GI absorption decreasedNutritional 2° hyperparathyroidism
SSepsis↓ iCa; multifactorial
EEthylene glycol toxicityEG → Ca oxalate chelation; Ca oxalate crystals in urine
🩸 Hematology: Eight sub-tabs — CBC methodology (analyzers, parameters, artifacts), erythrocyte biology, anemia classification (reticulocyte interpretation by species), hemolytic anemias (IMHA/oxidative/hemoparasites), non-regenerative anemias, leukocyte patterns (ELSI/SSMILED), hematopoietic neoplasia, and cytology/effusions.
🩸 Hematology: Seven major sections — CBC methodology, erythrocyte biology, anemia classification, hemolytic anemias, non-regenerative anemias, leukocyte patterns, and hematopoietic neoplasia. The CBC is the most important single test of the hematopoietic system.

CBC Components & Tube Selection

CBC components

ComponentMeasures
ErythrogramRBC count, Hgb, Hct/PCV, MCV, MCHC, MCH, RDW, reticulocytes, morphology
LeukogramTotal WBC, differential count (absolute numbers), morphology
ThrombogramPlatelet count, MPV, PDW, morphology
+/− TPP & fibrinogenEspecially horses and ruminants

Key tube rules

TubeUseCritical pitfall
EDTA (purple)CBC; hematology of choiceHeparin NOT for mammals — poor WBC preservation, cell clumping. Reptiles/amphibians: use heparin (EDTA hemolysis)
Serum clot (red/tiger)Chemistry, serologyEDTA contamination → ↓ Ca, ↑ K. Allow to fully clot before spinning.
Sodium citrate (blue)CoagulationMust be ≥90% full or clotting times are falsely prolonged
Heparin (green)Chemistry, blood gas, some avian CBCsNOT for coagulation; some analytes need different RI vs serum
Recommended fill order: (1) blue top → (2) red top → (3) green top → (4) purple top

Analyzer Types — Key Concepts

TypePrincipleKey limitations
QBC (buffy coat)Cells separate by density in spun capillary tube; thickness of band → countCounts depend on normal cell sizes — any size deviation = inaccurate. Hct very accurate (= PCV).
ImpedanceCells disrupt electrical current across aperture → counted and sizedRBC/platelet separated by size only → cats: overlap causes errors. In vitro hemolysis, lipemia, agglutination, Heinz bodies falsely ↑ Hgb (blocks light). Differential only crude (3-part: gran/lymph/mono).
Laser (flow cytometry)Light scatter measures size, density, and granularity as cells pass laserCannot accurately detect morphology changes, maturity shifts, or hemoparasites even with large instruments.
⚠️ All analyzers require blood smear examination to catch morphology changes, hemoparasites, and abnormal cell populations. If you won't do smears, send out to a reference lab.

Key CBC Parameters — Artifacts & Interpretation

PCV vs. Hct

PCV (spun microhematocrit) = gold standard. Hct = machine calculation. Acceptable to differ by ~2% (plasma trapping in PCV). PCV ↑ = erythrocytosis. PCV ↓ = anemia.

Causes of falsely ↑ MCHC

  • In vitro hemolysis
  • Lipemia
  • Agglutination
  • Heinz bodies
  • Acute intravascular hemolysis (real free Hgb)
↑ MCHC is never real (physiologic) — always investigate for artifact first.

Fibrinogen & PP:F ratio (large animals)

PP:F ratio = (plasma TP mg/dL − fibrinogen mg/dL) ÷ fibrinogen mg/dL

SpeciesDehydrationInflammation
Cattle>15<10
Horses>20<15

Fibrinogen is a better indicator of inflammation than WBC changes in ruminants. Horses also respond well. Correlate with clinical signs if ratio is borderline.

Platelet estimate (blood smear)

Average platelets/100× field × 20,000 = rough estimate. Check feathered edge for platelet clumps first — clumps invalidate machine count.

Analyzer Technologies — Impedance vs Laser vs QBC

TechnologyPrincipleKey advantagesKey limitations
Impedance
(most common bench-top)		 Cells pass through aperture between electrodes; disruption in current = count + size Affordable, reliable, easy to use. Accurate RBC + WBC counts in normal samples. RBC/platelet channel separates by SIZE — cat platelets overlap RBC size → inaccurate platelet counts. WBC differential (3-part: gran/lymph/mono) based only on size → highly inaccurate with abnormal WBC profile. False ↑ Hgb from anything blocking light (lipemia, Heinz bodies, high WBC). Cannot detect morphology changes, maturity shifts, or hemoparasites.
Laser / Flow Cytometry
(large commercial labs)		 Cells pass through laser beam; scattered light measures size + density + granularity (internal complexity) Far more accurate than impedance. 5-part differential. Accurate cat platelet counts even when sizes overlap RBCs. Expensive. Even full-sized laser analyzers cannot accurately detect morphology changes, maturity shifts, or hemoparasites — blood smear review always required for these.
QBC (Quantitative Buffy Coat)
(IDEXX VetAutoread)		 Cells stratify by density in spun oversized capillary tube with float; band thickness = count Reported Hct = very accurate (essentially = PCV). Simple. Counts depend on known typical cell sizes — any deviation (macro/microcytosis) → inaccurate counts.
⚠️ Key concept: Regardless of analyzer type, a blood smear must be reviewed to detect morphology changes (spherocytes, schistocytes, Heinz bodies), maturity shifts (band neutrophils, blasts), and hemoparasites. No analyzer replaces smear evaluation for these findings.

🦠 Hemoparasites — Key Organisms

⚠️ Regardless of analyzer type, hemoparasites are only detected on blood smear or PCR — analyzers cannot identify them. Sensitivity of smear examination is low for many organisms; PCR is test of choice for most.
OrganismSpeciesPathogenicityMorphologyKey facts
Mycoplasma haemofelis
(formerly H. felis)		 🐱 Cats HIGH Tiny rings, dots or linear chains on RBC surface; can fall off in stored blood Causes feline infectious anemia (FIA) — extravascular IMHA. Anemia should be regenerative; non-regenerative = suspect FeLV/FIV immunosuppression. PCR = test of choice (smear has low sensitivity). Waxing/waning parasitemia.
M. haemocanis
(formerly H. canis)		 🐕 Dogs LOW in healthy Linear chains with branching 'Y' appearance on RBC surface Only causes hemolytic anemia in splenectomized or immunocompromised dogs; latent in healthy dogs. PCR preferred.
M. haemosuis
(formerly E. suis)		 🐷 Pigs HIGH High numbers on RBC surface Significant disease in piglets and adults; poor weight gain. Can be present in very high numbers.
Babesia canis 🐕 Dogs LOW–MOD (US strain) Large form (2–4 µm), teardrop-shaped, dot-like red nucleus, blue membrane, pale interior. Sometimes in pairs. US strain low virulence (Greyhounds ↑ risk); European/S. African strains more virulent. Mild anemia + mild–moderate thrombocytopenia. Tick-transmitted.
Babesia gibsoni 🐕 Dogs MODERATE Small form (<2 µm), small dot-like nucleus, thin membrane, pale interior — difficult to see Pit bulls at ↑ risk; dog-bite transmission. Moderate–marked regenerative anemia + thrombocytopenia. Most Coombs' positive; may have spherocytes → differential for secondary IMHA. Use capillary blood (ear vein) + overstaining to improve detection.
Cytauxzoon felis 🐱 Cats (US) HIGHLY FATAL Pyroplasms: round/oval tiny ring-shaped with small peripheral purple nucleus in RBCs. Schizonts (tissue stage) in macrophages lining blood vessels. Spring–early fall; bobcat reservoir. Schizonts occlude blood vessels → multi-organ failure. Non-regenerative anemia + leukopenia + toxic change + thrombocytopenia. Onset rapid (7 days) — early smear ID is critical (no time for PCR). ~50% of cats: organisms NOT in blood → tissue aspirates needed. Aggressive early treatment can be successful.
Anaplasma marginale 🐄 Cattle SEVERE in adults Small (0.5–1 µm) basophilic dots at periphery of RBCs (marginated) Must differentiate from Howell-Jolly bodies (HJ tend to be central, larger, less numerous). Age-dependent severity: <1 yr = subclinical; yearlings/2-yr = moderate; older naive cattle = often fatal. Peak parasitemia precedes anemia. Serology/PCR for herd diagnosis.
🔬 Smear tip: Mycoplasmas are loosely adherent and can fall off in stored blood — examine fresh samples. Distinguish from stain precipitate by looking for organisms at very edges of cells, slightly indenting the membrane.

Erythrocyte Biology — Production, Hgb Synthesis, Destruction

Production & EPO

  • Rubriblast → erythrocyte: ~7–8 days
  • EPO produced by kidney in response to hypoxia
  • EPO effects: ↑ developing cells, ↓ maturation time, ↑ reticulocyte release
  • CKD → ↓ EPO → non-regenerative, normocytic/normochromic anemia

Nutrients required for erythropoiesis

NutrientRole / deficiency effect
IronEssential for heme. Deficiency → microcytic then hypochromic. In adults, iron deficiency = chronic blood loss until proven otherwise.
CopperRequired for iron metabolism. Deficiency → microcytic/hypochromic (similar to iron def.)
Vitamin B12 (cobalamin)Nucleic acid synthesis. Deficiency in dogs → ↑ anisocytosis (not macrocytosis as in humans). Can occur from GI malabsorption or hereditary malabsorption.
Folic acidNucleic acid synthesis. Deficiency → macrocytic anemia
CobaltComponent of B12. Deficiency on cobalt-poor pastures → anemia in cattle.

Hemoglobin variants

VariantCauseKey feature
MethemoglobinIron oxidized → can't carry O₂. Causes: oxidant drugs, nitrates/nitrites, congenital MetHgb reductase deficiency.Dark brown blood; does NOT return to normal with air exposure
CarboxyhemoglobinCO binds Hgb instead of O₂Both arterial and venous blood bright cherry red

Lead poisoning effects on RBCs

  • Inhibits heme synthesis enzymes including rate-limiting enzyme
  • Inhibits rRNA degradation → basophilic stippling
  • Mild anemia, rubricytosis; inappropriate BS relative to polychromasia
  • Dogs most sensitive; cattle, horses, sheep intermediate; pigs resistant

RBC destruction (normal)

Senescent RBCs → phagocytized by macrophages (mainly spleen) → heme → bilirubin (UCB) → liver conjugation → bile. Rate-limiting step = secretion of CB into bile canaliculi.

RBC Morphology — Shape Changes & Inclusions

Key poikilocytes — high-yield associations

CellAppearanceKey associations
SpherocytesSmall, dark, no central pallorIMHA hallmark; also zinc/bee/snake tox, fragmentation anemia
SchistocytesFragmented RBCsDIC, hemangiosarcoma, glomerulonephritis
AcanthocytesIrregular blunt spiculesDogs: hemangiosarcoma, DIC, liver disease; Cats: hepatic lipidosis
EchinocytesEven short spikes (sea urchin)Artifact (crenation) OR dehydration, electrolyte depletion, glomerulonephritis. Type III in dogs = rattlesnake envenomation (~90%)
EccentrocytesHemoglobin pushed to one side; fused membrane pale skirtOxidative damage: red maple (horses), allium, other oxidants
Target cellsBell-shaped, central dense + clear haloRegenerative anemia, cholestatic liver disease, iron deficiency
Ghost cellsVery pale RBC-sized membranesAcute intravascular hemolysis only (cleared rapidly)

Key inclusions

InclusionWhat it isSignificance
Heinz bodiesDenatured Hgb aggregates on membrane; refractile with Romanowsky, blue with NMBOxidative damage. Cats: up to 10% normal in health (tiny). High numbers + anemia → oxidative hemolytic anemia
Howell-Jolly bodiesNuclear remnant; dark blue dot near edgeRare = unremarkable. ↑ = regenerative anemia, splenic dysfunction/splenectomy
Basophilic stipplingAggregated residual RNA; fine blue dotsAppropriate: strong regeneration (especially ruminants). Inappropriate: lead poisoning, dyserythropoiesis
nRBCs (rubricytes)Nucleated RBCs in circulationAppropriate: strong regeneration. Inappropriate: splenic disease/splenectomy, BM disease (neoplasia, endotoxemia, lead)
Rouleaux = stacking like coins → hyperglobulinemia, hyperfibrinogenemia. Normal in horses, cats (mild), pigs. Agglutination = grape-like clumps → IMHA. Distinguish by saline dispersion test (agglutination persists; rouleaux disperses).

Anemia Classification Framework

Three classification axes: (1) bone marrow response — regenerative vs. non-regenerative; (2) RBC indices — MCV/MCHC pattern; (3) underlying cause. Use all three together.

Reticulocyte response — species differences

SpeciesRetic in healthResponse to anemiaNotes
DogsLowRobust — highest of all speciesBest reticulocyte responder
CatsLowGood responseAggregate reticulocytes = current response. Punctate = prior event (stay 1–3 weeks)
RuminantsNoneModerate; BS is sign of regenerationWill not have robust retic response — lack ≠ non-regenerative
HorsesNoneRarely released even with anemiaUse rising PCV/MCV + ↑ RDW; bone marrow eval if needed

Reticulocyte absolute count — interpretation (dogs/cats)

DegreeDog (/µL)Cat (/µL)
None/inadequate<95,000<60,000
Mild95–150,00060–100,000
Moderate150–300,000100–200,000
Marked>300,000>200,000

Formula: absolute retic = % retics × RBC count (convert % by moving decimal ×2 left)

⚠️ Reticulocyte response begins ~3 days post-stimulus, peaks 4–7 days, returns to RI before PCV fully recovers. Always use serial PCVs for best assessment.

MCV / MCHC Patterns

PatternCommon causesKey point
Normocytic, normochromicMost common pattern. Non-regenerative, pre-regenerative, or regenerative with insufficient reticulocytes to shift indicesAlways check reticulocytes before calling non-regenerative
Microcytic, hypochromicIron deficiency anemia (classic pattern)Microcytosis occurs BEFORE hypochromasia (MCH drops before MCHC)
Macrocytic, hypochromicStrongly regenerative anemia with high reticulocyte numbersOnly strong regeneration shifts both indices
Macrocytic, normochromicFeLV cats (dyserythropoiesis), cobalt-deficient ruminants, some dog breeds, regeneration (variable)Check FeLV status in cats
Microcytic, normochromicEarly/developing iron deficiency, portosystemic shuntsPSS: may or may not be mildly anemic

Distinguishing Blood Loss vs. Hemolysis

FeatureBlood LossHemolysis (EV)Hemolysis (IV)
Plasma protein↓ (proteins lost with blood)Normal to ↑Normal to ↑
HemoglobinemiaAbsentAbsentPresent (pink serum)
HemoglobinuriaAbsentAbsentPresent acutely
SplenomegalyNormal to smallEnlargedNo
BilirubinAbsentOften ↑ at presentationAbsent acutely (develops later)
MCHCNormalNormal to ↓↑ acutely (free Hgb)
ReticulocytosisAfter 3 daysOften present at presentationNot present acutely
Only about 1/3 of patients with chronic blood loss will have concurrent hypoproteinemia. Only about 1/3 with chronic blood loss have hypoproteinemia — lack of hypoproteinemia does NOT rule out blood loss.

IMHA — Immune Mediated Hemolytic Anemia

Key rule: Primary IMHA is always a diagnosis of exclusion — rule out all secondary causes first. Most IMHA is extravascular (macrophage-mediated). Spherocytes are the hallmark but lack of spherocytes does NOT rule it out.

Antibody types

TypeCharacteristic
Incomplete (IgG)Bound to RBC but won't cause agglutination — detected by Coombs test. Most IMHA cases.
Complete (IgM)Bridges multiple RBCs → gross or microscopic agglutination. Cold agglutinin disease.
Warm-reactingBind at body temp (37°C) — most IMHA
Cold-reactingIgM; active only at cold temps → acral necrosis (ear tips, extremities) in cold weather

Direct Coombs Test (DAT)

Detects antibody/complement on RBCs by inducing agglutination. Performed at 37°C in EDTA blood. Do NOT perform if autoagglutination is confirmed — the endpoint is the same (agglutination).

False negatives: low antibody level, prozone effect (serial dilutions fix this), drug hapten not included in test, glucocorticoid therapy. False positives: non-specific Ig in inflammation/neoplasia, recent transfusion.

Classic lab findings (dogs)

  • Strongly regenerative anemia
  • Spherocytes (hallmark in dogs)
  • ↑ nRBCs
  • Inflammatory leukogram (proportionate to tissue hypoxia)
  • Hyperbilirubinemia, bilirubinuria
  • Agglutination present in minority of cases

Atypical IMHA variants (dogs)

  • PIMA: antibodies against RBC precursors → non-regenerative peripherally, spherocytes may be present
  • PRCA: pure red cell aplasia — targets very early erythroid precursors; non-regenerative in BM and blood
  • Cold agglutinin disease: acral necrosis in cold weather

Serious complications of canine IMHA

  • DIC — reported in up to 1/3 of cases
  • Pulmonary thromboembolism — life threatening
  • Thrombocytopenia (concurrent IMT = Evan's syndrome)

Neonatal Isoerythrolysis (NI)

Mother becomes sensitized to blood group-incompatible RBCs during pregnancy → antibodies in colostrum → neonate ingests → hemolysis.

SpeciesNotes
Horses/mule foalsMost clinically important; ~90% of equine NI cases involve Aa or Qa antigens. Donkey RBCs have "donkey factor" absent in horses → mule pregnancies at high risk.
CatsUnique: Type B queens produce anti-A antibodies without prior sensitization. Type A/AB kittens of Type B queens at risk → major contributor to fading kitten syndrome.
Dogs, cattle, pigsRare
Prevention: cross-match mare's serum to stallion's RBCs in last weeks of pregnancy; give at-risk foals colostrum from a different mare.

Oxidative Hemolytic Anemia

Mechanisms

ChangeMechanism
Heinz bodiesDenatured Hgb adheres to membrane → EV hemolysis (splenic pitting/phagocytosis)
EccentrocytesMembrane protein oxidation → Hgb displaced to one side; pyknocyte when membrane tag removed
MethemoglobinIron oxidized → ↓ O₂ transport; does NOT cause anemia alone
Cats are more susceptible to Heinz body formation than dogs due to more reactive Hgb. Cat spleens less sinusoidal → less efficient HB removal.

Major oxidant causes by species

CauseSpeciesNotes
Allium (onion, garlic, chives)All species; dogs/cats most sensitiveOften inadvertently fed in baby foods, home diets, leftovers. Cattle can acclimate slowly.
Red maple (wilted/dried leaves)Horses primarilyMetHgb, HB/eccentrocytes, hemoglobinuria, heme-protein nephropathy; ghost cells acutely
Brassica (cabbage, kale, rape)Ruminants primarily
AcetaminophenDogs and especially catsCats have limited metabolism → no safe dose. MetHgb + HB + liver necrosis.
ZincAll; dogs most commonPennies post-1983, metal objects. Spherocytes may also form.
CopperSheep especiallyStore excess in liver; acute release after stress → IV hemolytic anemia
NitratesCattleMetHgb formation

Hypophosphatemia-Induced Hemolysis & Key Hemoparasites

Hypophosphatemia (<1 mg/dL)

Phosphorus required for ATP production in RBCs. Severe deficiency → acute intravascular hemolysis.

  • Diabetic ketoacidosis — especially cats. Monitor P and K carefully as insulin drives both into cells.
  • Refeeding syndrome (electrolyte derangements)
  • Bovine post-parturient hemoglobinuria (2–4 weeks post-calving; multifactorial)

Key hemoparasites

OrganismSpeciesKey features
M. haemofelisCatsMost pathogenic hemotropic Mycoplasma; severe EV hemolytic anemia; waxing/waning. PCR = test of choice.
Babesia gibsoniDogs (Pit bulls)Small form; moderately virulent; moderate-marked hemolytic anemia + thrombocytopenia; Coombs+ common. Dog bites transmit. Identify on smear with ear vein blood + overstaining.
Babesia canisDogs (Greyhounds)Large form; US strain low virulence; mild anemia + thrombocytopenia
Anaplasma marginaleCattlePrimarily EV regenerative hemolytic anemia
Cytauxzoon felisCatsPancytopenia; anemia typically non-regenerative (unusual for hemolytic disease)

Non-Regenerative Anemias — Overview

Most common type of anemia in domestic animals. Caused by either reduced erythropoiesis (hypocellular BM) or defective erythropoiesis (hypercellular BM). RBCs have a long lifespan → decreased production takes weeks to become clinically apparent.
CausePatternKey distinguishing features
Anemia of inflammation/chronic disease (AI/ACD)Normocytic, normochromic (rarely micro/hypo)Most common non-regen anemia. Any inflammatory/infectious/neoplastic cause. Onset as rapid as 3 days. Mild. Iron sequestered (not depleted) — ferritin ↑ (vs. iron def where ferritin ↓).
CKD — EPO deficiencyNormocytic, normochromic; moderate to severeLoss of renal EPO production. ↑ BUN/Cr/P, isosthenuric USG. Accelerated RBC destruction from uremia also contributes.
Iron deficiencyInitially normocytic normochromic → microcytic → hypochromicIn adults = chronic blood loss (parasites, GI, coagulopathy) until proven otherwise. Piglets need supplementation. Thrombocytosis in ~50%. Poikilocytosis (schistocytes, keratocytes, blister cells, target cells).
Endocrine diseaseNormocytic, normochromic; mildHypothyroidism (~1/3 of dogs), hypoadrenocorticism (can be masked by ↓ circulating volume), hypopituitarism
MyelophthisisPancytopenia; abnormal smear findingsReplacement of BM by fibrosis, neoplasia, granulomas. Atypical morphology, inappropriate rubricytosis, disorderly left shift, blast cells in circulation.
Aplastic anemiaPancytopenia; BM replaced by fatNeutropenia + thrombocytopenia may precede anemia (short neutrophil lifespan). Requires BM biopsy (not aspirate) to confirm hypocellularity. Key causes: estrogen (dogs/ferrets), E. canis (chronic), FeLV, drugs (chloramphenicol, TMS, griseofulvin), bracken fern toxicity (cattle).
FeLVVariable: normocytic/normochromic, macrocytic/normochromic (dyserythropoiesis), or pancytopeniaMultiple BM effects; also neoplastic transformation (lymphoma, leukemia, MDS). Cyclic anemia possible.
PIMA / PRCANon-regenerative despite immune targetingImmune-mediated attack on RBC precursors in BM → appears non-regenerative peripherally. Spherocytes may be present. PRCA = complete absence of RBC precursors.

Iron Panel — AI/ACD vs. Iron Deficiency

ParameterIron deficiencyAI/ACD
Serum iron↓↓ (iron sequestered)
TIBC (transferrin)Normal (dogs/cats) or ↑ (other species)↓
Transferrin saturation↓↓
Serum ferritin↓ (best test)↑ (stores present)
BM iron stores↓↑ (cats do not normally store BM iron)
Serum ferritin is the best serum test for distinguishing iron deficiency from AI/ACD. Iron deficiency responds to iron supplementation; AI/ACD won't.

Lead Poisoning — Non-Regenerative Anemia Pattern

ParameterFindingMechanism
Anemia severityMild (can be more severe with chronic exposure)Lead inhibits heme synthesis enzymes + shortens RBC lifespan → low-grade hemolysis
Rubricytosis↑↑ nRBCs — disproportionate to degree of anemiaLead inhibits pyrimidine-5′-nucleotidase → defective ribosomal degradation → nRBCs linger in circulation
Basophilic stipplingPresent — disproportionate to degree of polychromasiaSame mechanism: ribosomes aggregate as basophilic granules. Classic finding in dogs with lead toxicity.
PolychromasiaMinimal — KEY: BS and rubricytosis are out of proportion to the mild regenerative responseMild hemolysis only; not proportional to rubricytosis/BS severity
Species sensitivityDogs > cattle ≈ horses ≈ sheep > cats >> pigs (resistant)Species variation in enzyme sensitivity and exposure routes (cats: grooming lead paint dust)
💡 Pattern recognition: Rubricytosis + basophilic stippling DISPROPORTIONATE to mild polychromasia = think lead poisoning (especially in dogs). Other cause of basophilic stippling: regeneration in ruminants.

PIMA & PRCA — Precursor-Directed Immune-Mediated Anemia

PIMA (Precursor-Directed IMA)

  • Immune targeting of early erythroid precursors in bone marrow
  • Because destruction occurs before cells are released → presents as non-regenerative peripherally
  • Spherocytes may be present (immune attack)
  • Moderate to severe anemia; often responds to immunosuppressive therapy
  • Diagnosis: BM aspirate shows decreased/absent erythroid precursors with otherwise intact other cell lines

PRCA (Pure Red Cell Aplasia)

  • More severe form of PIMA
  • Complete absence of RBC precursors in bone marrow
  • Other cell lines (WBC, platelets) intact — distinguishes from aplastic anemia
  • Severely non-regenerative anemia with no reticulocyte response
  • BM biopsy: erythroid hypoplasia/aplasia only
⚠️ Distinguish PIMA/PRCA from aplastic anemia: Aplastic anemia = ALL cell lines affected (pancytopenia); PIMA/PRCA = RBC line only. BM biopsy (not aspirate) confirms; biopsy best shows cellularity.

Leukocyte Response Patterns — The Three Major Patterns

Epinephrine Response (Physiologic) — mnemonic ELSI

Epinephrine causes Lymphocytes and Segmented neutrophils to Increase

  • Rapid onset (~immediate), short-lived (~30 min)
  • Neutrophilia: MNP → CNP shift (marginated to circulating). Lymphocytosis: mechanism unclear, splenic contraction may play a role
  • Most pronounced in cats (up to 39,000 neuts, 35,000 lymphs). Foals, young/excitable dogs and pigs also affected
  • Transient thrombocytosis may also be seen

Corticosteroid Response (Stress) — mnemonic SSMILED

Stress and steroids cause Segs and Monocytes to Increase; Lymphs and Eosinophils Decrease

  • Single stimulus: develops over several hours, peaks 4–8 hr, lasts ~24 hr
  • Long-term exposure: response decreases in magnitude; 48–72 hr to return to baseline on resolution
  • Neutrophilia: MNP → CNP shift (adhesion molecules down-regulated). Monocytosis: redistribution. Lymphopenia: redistribution to lymph tissues + lymphotoxic with high doses.
  • Best in dogs; moderate in cats/horses; least dramatic in cattle (may just see eosinopenia ± monocytopenia)
  • Do NOT call epinephrine response a "stress response" — stress = corticosteroid response only

Inflammatory Response

  • Left shift: immature neutrophils (bands or earlier) in peripheral blood
  • Orderly left shift: least mature forms in lowest numbers — inflammatory
  • Degenerative left shift (DLS): immature cells > mature cells OR any left shift with neutropenia → serious; animals more likely to die
  • Toxic change: cytoplasmic basophilia, vacuolation, Döhle bodies, toxic granulation — acquired during marrow maturation. Moderate-marked → search for bacterial agent
  • Toxic ≠ degenerate: toxic = blood = cytoplasmic change; degenerate = tissue cytology = nuclear swelling

Neutrophil BM storage pool — species differences

SpeciesStorage poolNeutropenia with inflammation?
DogsLarge (~5 day supply)Rarely — requires overwhelming inflammation
Cats, horsesIntermediateMore likely; still serious
RuminantsVery smallYes — any significant acute inflammation
Neutropenia in dogs = medical emergency. In ruminants = expected with acute moderate inflammation. Trends over time critical.

Individual WBC Types — Key Disorders

Lymphocytes

ChangeKey causes
LymphocytosisEpinephrine response; young animals; chronic E. canis (dogs, up to 30,000/µL — mimic CLL); BLV-induced persistent lymphocytosis (cattle, non-neoplastic); hypoadrenocorticism (↓ endogenous steroids); lymphoid neoplasia
LymphopeniaCorticosteroid response; viral diseases (parvovirus, panleukopenia, distemper, FIP, FIV); chylothorax/PLE/lymphangiectasia; SCID in Arabian foals (fatal — profound lymphopenia, absent Ig)

Monocytes

Monocytosis: stress response (dogs); tissue necrosis, hemolysis, hemorrhage; chronic/granulomatous inflammation. First cells seen after BM recovery. Rapid maturation, no BM storage.

Eosinophils

ChangeKey causes
EosinophiliaParasites with tissue migration; hypersensitivity (asthma, dermatitis, flea allergy, eosinophilic bronchopneumopathy); mast cell tumors (dogs/horses — paraneoplastic); feline eosinophilic granuloma complex; hypoadrenocorticism; hypereosinophilic syndrome (Rottweilers and others)
EosinopeniaCorticosteroid response (most reliable cattle finding)

Basophils & Mast Cells

Basophilia parallels eosinophilia (IgE-mediated, parasitism, allergy). Normal high counts in rabbits (10–15%) and tortoises/turtles (30–60%).

Mastocythemia (mast cells in blood): acute GI inflammation (parvoviral enteritis), mast cell tumors, systemic mastocytosis (cats — often with splenic involvement).

Erythrocytosis & Thrombocytopenia/Thrombocytosis

Erythrocytosis classification

TypeMechanismKey features
RelativeHemoconcentration (dehydration) or splenic contraction (epinephrine)↑ PP with dehydration; young/excitable animals with splenic contraction. AHD syndrome (dogs): PCV >55% + paradoxically normal or ↓ PP due to proportionally greater plasma loss
Primary (polycythemia vera)Autonomous RBC proliferation, EPO-independent; myeloproliferativeNormal O₂ sat, normal EPO, normal renal function. Diagnosis of exclusion. Risk of thrombosis.
Secondary appropriate↑ EPO from chronic hypoxia (↓ PaO₂)Chronic heart disease, R→L shunting, severe lung disease, high altitude
Secondary inappropriateAberrant EPO secretionRenal tumors/cysts (localized hypoxia), rare EPO-secreting tumors

Thrombocytopenia mechanisms

  • Destruction: IMT (primary or secondary to drugs/infection/neoplasia); Evans syndrome (IMHA + IMT together)
  • Consumption: DIC, microangiopathies, vasculitis
  • Decreased production: BM damage, myelophthisis, immune destruction of megakaryocytes
  • Sequestration: splenic congestion, hemangiosarcoma

Thrombocytosis

  • Redistribution: splenic contraction (mild, transient)
  • Reactive (secondary): most common — inflammation, neoplasia, iron deficiency
  • Primary (essential thrombocythemia): rare neoplastic disorder; extremely high counts

Hematopoietic Neoplasia — Leukemia Classification

Classification is by (1) degree of differentiation: acute (blast cells, rapid course) vs. chronic (well-differentiated, protracted course) and (2) lineage: myeloid vs. lymphoid. Note: in hematopoiesis, "myeloid" includes erythroid and megakaryocytic cells.

Key leukemia types

TypeKey features
AML (Acute myeloid)>30% blast cells in BM. Moderate-high blasts in blood. Non-regenerative anemia, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia (myelophthisis). Poor response to therapy.
MDS<30% BM blasts. Peripheral cytopenias + dysplasia in multiple cell lines + hypercellular BM. Can progress to AML.
CML (Chronic myeloid)High numbers of well-differentiated cells. Must exclude non-neoplastic causes by exclusion. Protracted course; treatment may not be needed.
ALL (Acute lymphoid)Leukemic blood profile, severe anemia, other cytopenias from myelophthisis. High BM blast cells.
CLL (Chronic lymphoid)Older patients; often serendipitous finding. Persistent mild-marked ↑ in small well-differentiated lymphocytes. Mild non-regenerative anemia. BM inconsistently involved. Differentiate from chronic E. canis lymphocytosis by clonality testing (PARR).

Lymphoproliferative disorders

DisorderKey features
LymphomaArises in lymph tissues. BM involved in small % only. Neoplastic cells rarely in circulation (leukemic phase).
PlasmacytomaGenerally benign solitary tumor; skin and mucous membranes (dogs). Cats: internal/bone tumors more aggressive.
Multiple myelomaMalignant BM plasma cell tumor. Triad: monoclonal gammopathy (hyperglobulinemia) + lytic bone lesions + Bence-Jones proteinuria (Ig light chains in urine).

Bone marrow sampling — when to aspirate vs. biopsy

IndicationPreferred
Cell identification and cellular detailAspirate (cytology)
Cellularity and tissue architectureBiopsy
Suspected aplastic anemia or myelofibrosisBiopsy (these don't aspirate well)
"Dry tap" (nothing comes out)Biopsy — can happen with myelofibrosis OR hypercellular leukemia

🔭 Cytology & Effusions

📍 Cytology content has moved to its own dedicated tab. Click 🧫 Cytology in the top navigation to access all cytology content with lecture slides — collection techniques, approach, inflammation patterns, neoplasia (CoM + 4 cell types + LYMPH mnemonic), and lymph node evaluation.
💧 Body Cavity Effusions are also in the 🧫 Cytology tab → 💧 Effusions subtab, covering all effusion types, classification table, special tests, and large animal normals.
🩺 Hemostasis: Primary hemostasis (platelet plug — adhesion/secretion/aggregation), secondary hemostasis (coagulation cascade with pathway/factor/test mapping), fibrinolysis, DIC (three stages with diagnostic criteria), anticoagulant mechanisms (ATIII/Protein C&S), vWD (most common inherited coagulopathy in dogs), hemophilias, and the approach to clinical bleeding disorders.

Hemostasis Overview — Three Components

Primary hemostasis — platelet plug formation (temporary seal). Defects → petechiae, ecchymoses, mucosal bleeding.
Secondary hemostasis — coagulation cascade → stable cross-linked fibrin plug. Defects → hematomas, hemarthrosis, deep tissue bleeding.
Fibrinolysis — plasmin dissolves the fibrin clot after healing. Plasminogen → plasmin via tPA (from endothelium). D-dimers = specific breakdown products of cross-linked fibrin.

Primary hemostasis steps

  1. Vessel injury → vasoconstriction, exposure of subendothelium
  2. Adhesion — vWF bridges platelet GpIb receptor to exposed collagen
  3. Activation — shape change, ADP/TXA₂ release, GpIIb-IIIa exposed
  4. Aggregation — fibrinogen/vWF bridges GpIIb-IIIa on adjacent platelets
  5. Platelet plug — loose, temporary; needs fibrin reinforcement (2° hemostasis)
BMBT measures primary hemostasis (platelet count, function, vascular integrity). Prolonged BMBT with normal platelet count → thrombocytopathy. Coagulopathies (factor deficiencies) do NOT prolong BMBT unless platelet defect is concurrent.

Platelet Biology — Production, Key Facts, Acquired Disorders

Production & thrombopoietin

  • Platelets fragment from megakaryocytes; released directly to bloodstream
  • Thrombopoietin (TPO) is the main regulator; produced by hepatocytes, renal tubular epithelium, BM stroma. Inverse relationship: low platelet mass → TPO rises → stimulates megakaryocytes
  • Platelet lifespan 5–10 days. Spleen stores ~30–40% of total platelet mass
  • Feline platelets: largest MPV of domestic species, more size variability; may equal an RBC in size
  • Equine platelets stain faintly — may be hard to identify on smears
  • Spontaneous bleeding risk: platelet count <20,000/µL
  • Greyhounds and Cavalier KCS: normally lower platelet counts in health

Platelet granule contents

GranuleContents / Function
AlphaFibrinogen, FV, vWF, PDGF, thrombospondin, PF4 — coagulation and adhesion
DenseADP, ATP, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, serotonin — amplify platelet activation
LysosomesGlycosidases, proteases, lipases — degradation

Inherited platelet disorders (know these)

DisorderDefectSpecies/breed
vWD (Type I/II/III)vWF deficiency/dysfunctionDogs (Dobermans, Scotties, Shelties); rare in cats, horses, cattle
Glanzmann thrombastheniaGpIIb-IIIa deficiency → no aggregation, no clot retractionOtterhounds, Great Pyrenees
Chediak-HigashiDense granule deficiency; abnormal leukocyte/melanocyte granulation; dilute coat colorBlue Persian cats, Herefords, Brangus cattle, Aleutian mink
MacrothrombocytopeniaBeta1-tubulin mutation; large platelets, normal functionCavalier King Charles Spaniels
CalDAG-GEFI disorderAbnormal fibrinogen receptorBasset Hounds, Spitz, bovines

Acquired platelet dysfunction

  • Aspirin: irreversibly acetylates cyclooxygenase → inhibits TXA₂ for life of platelet (up to 5 days, as megakaryocytes also affected)
  • NSAIDs (ibuprofen): reversibly inhibit COX — effect <6 hours depending on half-life
  • β-lactam antibiotics: reversibly inhibit agonist receptors → ↓Ca²⁺ flux
  • Uremia, liver disease, FeLV, Ehrlichia, DIC (FDPs competitively inhibit fibrinogen binding)

Coagulation Cascade — Pathways, Factors & Tests

PathwayKey factorsInitiating eventTest
ExtrinsicIII (tissue factor) + VII → XaTissue factor released from injured epitheliumPT (factors VII, X, V, II, fibrinogen)
IntrinsicXII→XI→IX→VIII → XaFactor XII contacts exposed collagenAPTT/ACT (XII, XI, IX, VIII, X, V, II, fibrinogen)
CommonX + V → II (thrombin) → fibrin → XIII crosslinksBoth pathways converge at XaPT + APTT both prolonged

Vitamin K–dependent factors (II, VII, IX, X + Protein C, S, Z)

Vit K required for gamma-carboxylation (Ca²⁺ binding). Rodenticide (brodifacoum/warfarin) blocks vit K recycling. Factor VII has the shortest half-life → PT prolonged first in early rodenticide toxicity. PIVKA test detects non-carboxylated precursors.

Lab tests — what they measure & sensitivity

TestEvaluatesSensitivity (factors must be <)
ACTIntrinsic + common (requires platelet phospholipid)<5% of normal — very insensitive
APTTIntrinsic + common<30% of normal
PTExtrinsic + common<20% of normal
TCTFibrinogen quantity/quality (common pathway end)Fibrinogen <100 mg/dL; also prolonged by heparin, FDPs
D-dimersCross-linked fibrin degradation (plasmin)Specific for fibrinolysis of stabilized clot; ↑ in DIC, thrombosis
FDPsAny fibrin/fibrinogen degradationLess specific than D-dimers
PIVKANon-carboxylated vit-K dependent factors in circulationBest for rodenticide/vit K deficiency
TEGGlobal coagulation (initiation → fibrinolysis); whole bloodBest test for hypercoagulability and peracute DIC
ATIIIAnticoagulant; ↓ in fulminant DIC (consumed)—

DIC — Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation

DIC is a complication, not a primary diagnosis. Causes: neoplasia, IMHA, pancreatitis, heatstroke, heartworm disease, septicemia, massive trauma, intravascular hemolysis.
StagePT/APTTPlateletsFDPs/D-dimersATIIISchistocytesBest test
Peracute (hypercoag)NormalMild ↓Normal/mild ↑NormalAbsentTEG
Fulminant (consumptive)↑↑Moderate–severe ↓↑↑↓PresentCombination
Low-grade (compensated)Normal/mild ↑Normal/mild ↓↑Normal/mild ↓May be presentFDPs/D-dimers

DIC diagnosis: ≥3 of — prolonged PT/APTT, thrombocytopenia, ↑FDPs/D-dimers, ↓ATIII, RBC fragmentation (schistocytes)

Virchow's Triad (predisposing factors for thrombosis)

  • Endothelial injury (dominant factor)
  • Altered blood flow (stasis or turbulence)
  • Hypercoagulability (↓ATIII, ↓Protein C/S, platelet activation)

Anticoagulant pathways

AnticoagulantAction
ATIIIBinds/inactivates thrombin and other serine proteases. Heparin greatly enhances ATIII activity
Protein C + SInactivate Va and VIIIa. Thrombin + thrombomodulin → activates Protein C
Protein Z + ZPIDegrade Xa and XIa
TFPIInhibits tissue factor/VIIa complex

vWD, Hemophilias & Approach to Bleeding Disorders

von Willebrand Disease (vWD)

  • Most common inherited coagulopathy of dogs. High prevalence in Dobermans (was up to 60%), Scottish terriers, Shelties
  • vWF: bridges platelet GpIb to collagen; also protects and carries FVIII in plasma
  • Three types: Type I (partial ↓, most common), Type II (qualitative/structural defect, loss of HMW multimers), Type III (virtually absent vWF)
  • vWF antigen <50% = affected; 50–69% = carrier/low risk; >70% = unaffected
  • Clinical signs: mucosal bleeding, prolonged bleeding from wounds. Type III may show features of secondary hemostatic defect due to low FVIII
DisorderDefectSpeciesTest affected
Hemophilia AFVIII deficiency (sex-linked)Dogs, cats, cattle, horses↑APTT, normal PT
Hemophilia BFIX deficiency (sex-linked)Dogs, cats↑APTT, normal PT
Hemophilia CFXI deficiencyDogs, Holstein cattle↑APTT — mild
Hageman's diseaseFXII deficiencyCats, dogs↑APTT — no bleeding

Interpreting PT/APTT patterns

PatternPathway affectedThink of…
↑PT only, normal APTTExtrinsic only (FVII)Early rodenticide/vit K def, hereditary FVII deficiency
↑APTT only, normal PTIntrinsic onlyHemophilia A/B/C, Hageman's disease, vWD (severe)
↑PT + ↑APTTCommon or multiple defectsDIC, liver failure, rodenticide (advanced), FX/V/II/fibrinogen deficiency

Clinical bleeding pattern

  • Primary defect (platelet/vascular): petechiae, ecchymoses, mucosal bleeding (epistaxis, gingival, hematuria, melena)
  • Secondary defect (coagulation factors): hematomas, hemarthrosis, deep tissue/subcutaneous bleeding
  • DIC: both patterns combined

Cell-Based Model of Coagulation — 3 Phases

⚠️ Study Q: Describe the difference between the cell-based and cascade models. The cascade model describes factor order; the cell-based model explains WHERE coagulation happens (on cell surfaces) — these are complementary, not competing models.

① Initiation

Occurs on tissue factor (TF)-bearing cells (subendothelial fibroblasts, macrophages). Vascular injury exposes TF → TF + VIIa → small amounts of Xa, IXa, and thrombin generated. Not enough thrombin for full clot — just primes the system.

② Amplification

Moving from TF-bearing cell to platelet surface. Damage allows platelets to contact extravascular tissue → platelets adhere, activate, accumulate activated cofactors on surface → procoagulant stimulus amplified. Platelets + cofactors prepare for massive thrombin generation.

③ Propagation

Thrombin generated on platelet surface. Tenase + prothrombinase complexes assembled on activated platelet phospholipid surface → massive thrombin burst → fibrinogen → fibrin → stable cross-linked clot.

🔑 Key distinction from cascade model: The cascade model describes the sequential activation of factors; the cell-based model explains that coagulation is regulated by cell surfaces (TF-bearing cells first, then platelet surface). Coagulation doesn't occur freely in plasma — it's spatially regulated to the site of injury.
🫘 Kidney & Urinalysis: Azotemia classification (pre-renal/renal/post-renal with USG thresholds), BUN/Cr/SDMA physiology, CKD staging and full lab pattern (EPO anemia, hyperphosphatemia, secondary hyperparathyroidism), AKI phases and causes, complete urinalysis (collection, USG, dipstick by analyte, sediment/casts), glomerular disease, pyelonephritis, Fanconi syndrome, ethylene glycol toxicity, and ruptured urinary bladder electrolyte pattern.

Azotemia — Classification & Key Concepts

TypeUSGKey features
Pre-renal>1.035 dogs, >1.045 cats, >1.025 large animals↓circulating volume (dehydration, CHF, shock, Addison's, hemorrhage). Rapid resolution with rehydration. ↑TP, ↑albumin, erythrocytosis support dehydration. BUN:Cr ratio may be disproportionately ↑ with GI hemorrhage or protein catabolism.
Renal1.008–1.012 (isosthenuric); may be less than concentrated early≥75% nephron loss. BUN:Cr both ↑ proportionately. Cats may retain some concentrating ability after developing azotemia (unlike dogs).
Post-renalVariableObstruction or rupture (uroperitoneum). Ruptured bladder: ↓Na, ↓Cl, ↑K (urine is hypo-Na, hypo-Cl, hyper-K). Creatinine in fluid >> serum Cr (equilibrates slowly).

SDMA: Increases with ~40% GFR loss — detects CKD earlier than Cr. Not affected by muscle wasting (unlike Cr). Ruminants: BUN unreliable (salivary/rumen excretion) — use Cr instead.

CKD Staging (IRIS system) & AKI Phases

CKD staging (dogs/cats) based on serum creatinine ± SDMA measured ≥2× in a stable patient. BUN is NOT used for staging. Substaging by: (1) UP:C ratio (non-proteinuric/borderline/proteinuric) and (2) systolic blood pressure risk.

AKI phases: (1) Initiation — insult occurs, may go unrecognized; intervention here can prevent progression; (2) Maintenance — irreversible injury established; removing cause does not speed recovery; (3) Recovery — urine output increases, azotemia resolves; may take weeks to months.

Common AKI causes: ethylene glycol, aminoglycosides, Easter lily (cats), grapes/raisins (dogs), heavy metals, NSAIDs, leptospirosis, ischemia (shock, anesthesia, hemorrhage), heme pigment nephropathy (myoglobin/hemoglobin).

Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) — Key Lab Findings

  • Azotemia (≥75% nephron loss), isosthenuria
  • Hyperphosphatemia (↓GFR), Ca × P product >70 → risk of metastatic calcification
  • Non-regenerative anemia (↓EPO from kidney)
  • Normal to ↓albumin (more likely ↓ if glomerular disease)
  • Metabolic titration acidosis in dogs/cats (↑AG, ↓HCO₃⁻)
  • Hypokalemia (cats, cattle) — except horses who may be mildly hyperkalemic
  • Renal secondary hyperparathyroidism: ↓vit D activation → ↓ionized Ca → ↑PTH → bone resorption ("rubber jaw" in young dogs)
  • Hypermagnesemia (↓GFR-related)

AKI vs. CKD — distinguishing features

FeatureAKICKD
Body conditionGood (acute)Poor (chronic)
Kidney sizeNormal to large, painfulSmall
AnemiaAbsent unless concurrentPresent (↓EPO)
Urine sedimentActive — granular/epithelial casts, pyuria, hematuriaInactive
Azotemia onsetHours–days; rapid progressionMonths–years; may be stable

Urinalysis — Complete Components

Collection methods

MethodBest useKey limitation
Free-catchUSG; large animalsContaminated — not for culture
CatheterizationQuantitative culture if cystocentesis not possibleRisk of UTI (20% in female dogs); trauma → ↑RBCs
CystocentesisCulture — sample of choiceMust avoid friable bladder; bacteria always abnormal
Manual expressionNoneNOT recommended — reflux risk

USG — concentrating ability thresholds

Dogs>1.030 = adequate concentration
Cats>1.040 = adequate
Large animals>1.025 = adequate
Isosthenuria1.008–1.012 = no tubular modification
Hyposthenuria<1.008 = tubules actively diluting (ADH problem; kidneys DO work)

Glucose renal thresholds

Dog>180 mg/dLCat>280 mg/dL
Cow>100 mg/dLHorse>160 mg/dL
Bird>600 mg/dL

Dipstick — key facts per parameter

ParameterKey points
ProteinAlbumin-specific. False + in alkaline urine (horses/ruminants). Interpret with USG — 1+ in SG 1.008 = significant; trace in 1.040 = minor. UP:UC >5 = almost always glomerular. Microalbuminuria = 1.0–30 mg/dL (detected by species-specific ELISA, not dipstick) — screening for occult renal disease. Not needed if dipstick proteinuria is already present.
GlucoseGlucosuria without hyperglycemia → renal tubular defect (Fanconi syndrome, primary renal glucosuria in Basenjis)
KetonesNitroprusside reaction — detects acetoacetate/acetone, NOT β-hydroxybutyrate (most common ketone in DKA, but others are present concurrently)
BilirubinOnly conjugated bilirubin passes glomerulus. ANY bilirubinuria in cats = significant. Male dogs: trace-1+ in concentrated urine = normal (renal tubular conjugation).
Occult bloodPositive = hematuria OR hemoglobinuria OR myoglobinuria. Differentiate: hematuria — urine clears on centrifuge, RBCs in sediment. Hemoglobinuria — doesn't clear, no RBCs, pink plasma. Myoglobinuria — doesn't clear, no RBCs, clear plasma, ↑CK.

Casts

Cast typeSignificance
Hyaline1–2/lpf may be normal. ↑ with proteinuria, passive renal congestion
GranularTubular degeneration/necrosis (ischemia, nephrotoxins)
Cellular (epithelial)Acute tubular injury — active renal disease
WBC castsRare; tubulointerstitial inflammation — pyelonephritis
RBC castsRare; hemorrhage into tubules
WaxyProlonged renal stasis, oliguria, chronic severe disease — never normal
Normal: <5 RBCs/hpf, <5 WBCs/hpf (pyuria = >5), <5 epithelial cells/hpf. UP:UC only on INACTIVE urine sediment — no hematuria/pyuria.

Glomerular Disease, Pyelonephritis & Fanconi Syndrome

Glomerulonephritis — key findings

  • Persistent proteinuria (3–4+ dipstick), UP:UC typically >3–5
  • Hypoalbuminemia (<1.5 g/dL with full nephrotic syndrome)
  • Nephrotic syndrome: marked proteinuria + hypoalbuminemia + ascites/edema + hypercholesterolemia
  • Hypercoagulability — loss of ATIII in urine → pulmonary thromboembolism risk
  • Progressive CKD from glomerulosclerosis
  • Inactive sediment OR waxy/granular casts

Pyelonephritis

  • Active sediment: WBCs, RBCs, bacteria; WBC casts (uncommon but diagnostic of tubular infection)
  • Inflammatory leukogram with acute severe disease
  • Chronic: may be clinically silent unless it progresses to CKD
  • A simple LUTI (cystitis) will NOT cause systemic CBC or chemistry changes

Fanconi Syndrome

  • Proximal tubular dysfunction → renal loss of amino acids, phosphate, glucose, Ca, K, small proteins
  • Key finding: glucosuria with normoglycemia + PU/PD
  • Hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis (non-gap, secretory)
  • Familial in Basenjis (~10–30% of US Basenjis)
  • Acquired: contaminated chicken jerky treats, antibiotics, chemotherapy, leptospirosis

Ethylene Glycol Toxicity — Stage 2 classic pattern

  • Marked titrational acidosis (↑↑AG, ↓↓HCO₃⁻)
  • Severe hypocalcemia (oxalic acid binds Ca)
  • Calcium oxalate monohydrate crystalluria ("picket fence")
  • Crystalluria onset: as early as 3 hr in cats, 6 hr in dogs
  • Cats: stage 3 (oliguric AKI) within 12–24 hr; dogs: 26–72 hr

SDMA — Early GFR Marker

FeatureCreatinineSDMA (symmetric dimethylarginine)
GFR loss before elevation~75% loss of GFR (late detection)~40% loss of GFR (earlier detection)
Affected by muscle mass?YES — muscle wasting (cachexia, CKD) falsely lowers CrNO — not affected by body condition score
False increase?Muscle injury, high meat dietHigh cell turnover (e.g. rapid tumor cell die-off during cancer treatment)
Source / clearanceMuscle creatine → Cr; filtered + small tubular secretionMetabolic byproduct of protein methylation; freely filtered, no reabsorption, no extrarenal excretion
Clinical useStandard azotemia markerEarlier CKD detection; especially useful in cats with low muscle mass. Included on all IDEXX routine panels (proprietary).
Validated speciesAll domestic speciesDogs, cats, horses, rats
🔑 Study Q answer: BUN + Cr increase after ~75% GFR loss; SDMA increases after ~40% GFR loss → SDMA detects CKD earlier. Like creatinine, SDMA does not localize kidney disease — just correlates with decreased GFR regardless of cause.

Fanconi Syndrome — Proximal Tubular Failure

Pathophysiology

  • Proximal renal tubule dysfunction → failure to reabsorb multiple solutes
  • Renal losses of: amino acids, phosphate, glucose, calcium, potassium, small MW proteins
  • Distinguishing feature: glucosuria in a normoglycemic patient (glucose ≠ high in blood, but leaks into urine)

Clinical signs

  • PU/PD
  • Glucosuria (normoglycemic)
  • Hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis (proximal tubule can't reabsorb HCO₃⁻)
  • Can progress to renal failure

Causes

  • Familial: Basenjis (10–30%), Norwegian Elkhounds
  • Acquired (toxin/drug-induced proximal tubular injury):
  • Contaminated chicken jerky treats
  • Some antibiotics and chemotherapy agents
  • Leptospirosis
  • Copper storage hepatopathy
  • Some chemical food additives
⚠️ Do not confuse with primary renal glucosuria (Basenjis also): primary renal glucosuria = glucosuria only, without full Fanconi syndrome. Full Fanconi = glucosuria + aminoaciduria + phosphaturia + acidosis.
⚗️ Electrolytes & Acid-Base: Sodium (ECF osmolality, hyper/hyponatremia, pseudohyponatremia), chloride (Na–Cl difference by species, selective Cl changes = acid-base clue), potassium (species RBC K⁺ differences, pseudohyperkalemia), anion gap (titrational vs secretory acidosis), metabolic alkalosis with paradoxical aciduria, and step-by-step blood gas interpretation with the Henderson-Hasselbalch relationship.

Sodium — Regulates ECF Osmolality

Always interpret Na⁺ with the patient's hydration status. Normal Na does not mean normal total body Na or water.
ConditionKey causesMechanism
HypernatremiaWater deprivation, DI, osmotic diarrhea, salt poisoning, grain overload/urea toxicity (ruminants), bile peritonitis↓TBW relative to Na, or ↑Na intake
HyponatremiaVomiting/diarrhea, Addison's disease, Na-wasting nephropathies, third-space loss, CHF, hepatic cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome↑Na loss, water excess, or Na/water shifts
PseudohyponatremiaLipemia, hyperproteinemiaWater phase displaced by lipid/protein in diluted (indirect ISE) samples
EDTA is unacceptable for electrolytes — causes artifactual hypernatremia/hyperkalemia and marked hypocalcemia.

Chloride — always interpret with Na⁺

Changes in Cl⁻ usually parallel Na⁺. Disproportionate Cl changes indicate acid-base disorder.

FindingMeaning
↑Na–Cl difference (selective ↓Cl)Loss of HCl → metabolic alkalosis (vomiting, displaced abomasum)
↓Na–Cl difference (selective ↑Cl)Loss of NaHCO₃ → secretory/hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis (diarrhea, RTA)

Normal Na–Cl difference: dogs/cats 29–42 mmol/L · horses 34–43 · cattle 35–45

Corrected Cl⁻ = measured Cl × (mean Na ÷ measured Na)

Pseudohyperchloremia: potassium bromide (seizure medication) causes false ↑Cl⁻ by all methods → also causes a negative anion gap

Potassium — Major Intracellular Cation

Serum K⁺ ≠ total body K⁺ — most is intracellular. Always interpret with acid-base status. Markedly ↑K⁺ → atrial standstill, asystole. Markedly ↓K⁺ → muscle weakness, respiratory failure.
ConditionKey causes
HyperkalemiaAddison's disease (most classic), urinary obstruction (cats/foals), oliguric/anuric renal failure, metabolic acidosis (H⁺-K⁺ shift), rhabdomyolysis, heatstroke
HypokalemiaGI loss (vomiting/diarrhea), renal loss (osmotic diuresis, ketones), metabolic alkalosis, insulin excess (drives K into cells), sweating in horses, anorexia
PseudohyperkalemiaK₃EDTA tube, marked thrombocytosis (platelets release K during clotting), marked leukocytosis (leukemia). Also: horses, pigs, primates, Akita dogs have HIGH RBC K⁺ — hemolysis → ↑ serum K⁺ in these species

Anion Gap (AG)

AG = (Na⁺ + K⁺) − (Cl⁻ + HCO₃⁻)
Represents unmeasured anions (UA) minus unmeasured cations (UC)
DirectionInterpretationCauses
↑ AG (>normal)Titrational (gap) metabolic acidosis — HCO₃⁻ buffering ↑H⁺Renal failure (uremic acids), lactic acidosis, ketoacidosis, ethylene glycol toxicity
↓ AG↓ unmeasured anions or ↑ unmeasured cationsHypoalbuminemia (most common; albumin = major unmeasured anion), hypercalcemia, hypergammaglobulinemia, hemodilution
Negative AGArtifactPotassium bromide → false ↑Cl⁻
Acidosis → ↑K⁺ and ↑ionized Ca²⁺. Alkalosis → ↓K⁺ and ↓ionized Ca²⁺

Acid-Base Disorders — Classification & Compensation

DisorderpHHCO₃⁻pCO₂Compensation direction
Metabolic acidosis↓↓↓↓ (respiratory comp)Hyperventilation ↓pCO₂
Metabolic alkalosis↑↑↑↑ (respiratory comp)Hypoventilation ↑pCO₂
Respiratory acidosis↓↑ (renal comp)↑↑Kidneys retain HCO₃⁻
Respiratory alkalosis↑↓ (renal comp)↓↓Kidneys excrete HCO₃⁻
Key rule: With compensation, HCO₃⁻ and pCO₂ move in the same direction. Respiratory compensation is faster (hours) than metabolic (days). Compensation is never complete — pH still indicates the primary disorder.

Henderson-Hasselbalch relationship: H⁺ = 24 × (pCO₂ ÷ HCO₃⁻). Therefore: ↑pCO₂ → ↑H⁺ (acidosis); ↑HCO₃⁻ → ↓H⁺ (alkalosis). Base excess: negative = metabolic acidosis; positive = metabolic alkalosis. pO₂ reflects oxygenation (not acid-base); pO₂ should be ~5× the inspired O₂% (room air 21% → pO₂ ~100 mmHg).

Two types of metabolic acidosis

TypeAGNa–Cl diffMechanism & causes
Titrational (gap)↑NormalHCO₃⁻ buffering excess H⁺. Causes: renal failure, lactic acidosis, ketoacidosis (DKA, bovine ketosis), ethylene glycol toxicity
Secretory (non-gap hyperchloremic)Normal↓ (↑Cl⁻)Loss of HCO₃⁻ from GI or kidneys. Causes: diarrhea, renal tubular acidosis (RTA)

Metabolic alkalosis

  • Loss of HCl (vomiting, displaced abomasum in cattle) → ↑HCO₃⁻, ↓Cl⁻
  • Paradoxical aciduria (classic in ruminants with DA): marked hypochloremia + hypokalemia + hypovolemia → kidneys reabsorb HCO₃⁻ with Na, retain K, excrete H⁺ → acid urine despite systemic alkalosis

Blood gas interpretation steps

  1. pH → acidosis or alkalosis?
  2. HCO₃⁻ → metabolic component (↓=acidosis, ↑=alkalosis)
  3. pCO₂ → respiratory component (↑=acidosis, ↓=alkalosis)
  4. Calculate AG → titrational vs. secretory acidosis
  5. Evaluate Na–Cl difference → selective Cl disorder?
  6. Assess compensation → same-direction HCO₃⁻ and pCO₂?

Paradoxical Aciduria

Mechanism

  • Occurs in metabolic alkalosis + hypokalemia (classic: pyloric outflow obstruction)
  • HCl loss → metabolic alkalosis → kidney tries to excrete excess HCO₃⁻ (alkaline urine expected)
  • BUT: concurrent hypokalemia → kidney prioritizes K⁺ reabsorption over HCO₃⁻ excretion
  • Kidney exchanges H⁺ (not Na⁺) for K⁺ in distal tubule → acidic urine despite systemic alkalosis
  • This perpetuates the metabolic alkalosis (H⁺ leaving body in urine, not HCO₃⁻)

Clinical scenario

⚠️ Classic case: Dog or cat with pyloric obstruction (gastric outflow obstruction). HCl lost in vomit → hypochloremia → metabolic alkalosis → hypokalemia (K⁺ shifts intracellularly). Urine pH is acidic despite alkalotic blood. Also seen with: severe hypokalemia from any cause, particularly in horses (sweat losses) and cattle with CKD (see alkalotic CKD in ruminants).

Lab pattern

  • ↑ blood pH / ↑ HCO₃⁻ (metabolic alkalosis)
  • ↓ Cl⁻ (selective loss)
  • ↓ K⁺ (hypokalemia)
  • Urine pH acidic (paradoxical — should be alkaline)

Soft Tissue Mineralization — Ca × P Product

Ca × P productInterpretation
<70Soft tissue mineralization unlikely
≥70Risk of soft tissue mineralization — monitor and intervene

Classical sites of mineralization: blood vessels, kidneys, stomach wall, lungs, heart, intercostal muscles, intestinal submucosa.

Common clinical contexts: CKD secondary hyperparathyroidism (↑P + ↑PTH), hypercalcemia of malignancy (↑Ca), hypervitaminosis D (↑Ca + ↑P). Mineralization causes progressive organ dysfunction (renal mineralization worsens CKD, pulmonary mineralization impairs gas exchange).

🦋 Endocrine — Expanded: Five sub-tabs covering all lecture content. CP-1 = lab eval of thyroid & adrenal (Stowe); CP-2 = thyroid & adrenal deep-dive with pattern interpreter; CP-3 = endocrine pancreas (DM, DKA, feline HAC/acromegaly, insulinoma); Lab Cases = Jazzi, Sidney, Yogi, Sally; Flashcards = 16 vignettes across all topics.
🐱 Feline Hyperthyroidism: Older cats (>95% are ≥10 yrs). Most common cause: functional thyroid adenoma (~70% bilateral). Hyperthyroid cats are stress-sensitive — handle carefully at vet visits. Excess T4 drives a hypermetabolic, hyperdynamic state.

Signalment

  • Older cats — only ≤5% are younger than 10 years
  • No breed predisposition reported
  • Impaired stress tolerance — stressful vet visits can cause cardiac complications
Lesions
  • Functional adenoma — 70% bilateral; ectopic tissue possible
  • Thyroid carcinoma — uncommon (~5%); potential for metastasis or ectopic location

Clinical Signs

Owner ComplaintsPE Findings
Weight lossUnderweight body condition
PolyphagiaPalpable thyroid mass
VomitingHeart murmur
PU/PDTachycardia
Increased activity / restlessnessHypertension
⚠️ Hyperthyroidism can mask underlying renal disease — elevated GFR in hyperthyroid cats artificially lowers BUN/Cr. After treatment, GFR normalizes and previously hidden CKD may become apparent. Always assess renal function after achieving euthyroidism.

CBC Findings

Stress response — leukogram changes consistent with stress

High-normal to mildly ↑ RBC mass — erythrocytosis from increased metabolic demand

High MCV — seen in some hyperthyroid cats

Other CBC parameters often unremarkable. Stress leukogram expected given the sympathoadrenal state. High-normal PCV may normalize post-treatment.

Chemistry Panel Findings

FindingFrequencyMechanism / Notes
Liver enzyme ↑ (ALT, ALP, AST)>90%Most common finding. Mild to moderate. Multifactorial — hepatic congestion, malnutrition, direct thyroid hormone effect. Resolves with treatment.
Hyperphosphatemia36–43%Thyroid hormone impedes renal phosphorus excretion
Mild to moderate azotemia20–40%Increased GFR may mask underlying CKD. True prevalence revealed only after treatment.
Hyperglycemia~22%Glucose intolerance / decreased insulin sensitivity from excess thyroid hormone

Urinalysis Findings

FindingNotes
USG variably decreased~50% can still concentrate; some dilute; rest show inadequate concentration. Medullary washout from ↑GFR. May reflect underlying renal disease.
Mild proteinuriaResponds to treatment. May indicate hypertension or glomerular disease.
Bacterial UTIIncreased susceptibility; always culture if proteinuria or pyuria noted

Total T4 (TT4) — Primary Screening Test

Increased TT4 — confirms hyperthyroidism in the right clinical context. Diagnostic in >90% of cases.

High-normal TT4 (false negative): <10% of cases

  • Early or mild disease
  • Biologic fluctuations (T4 fluctuates throughout the day)
  • Suppression from non-thyroidal illness (NTI)
  • Severe hypoproteinemia
🐱 Monitoring note: Avoid using more than one measurement method when monitoring. Each method has its own reference interval — always interpret using the RI for that specific assay.

Free T4 (fT4) — Reflex / Confirmatory Test

ResultInterpretationCaveat
Increased fT4Supports hyperthyroidism when TT4 is high-normalSome cats with NTI also have ↑fT4 — opposite of dogs. Some NORMAL cats can have elevated fT4!
Normal fT4Less helpful on its own—
⚠️ fT4 is NEVER a standalone screening test in cats. Must always be interpreted alongside TT4. High-normal TT4 + elevated fT4 = supports hyperthyroidism. Elevated fT4 with normal TT4 = could be NTI or a normal cat.

Diagnostic Approach Summary

Clinical suspicion
Older cat
Weight loss + polyphagia
→
TT4
First line
Screen
→
Clearly elevated
Hyperthyroid confirmed
TT4 high-normal
Suspicious but not diagnostic
→
Add fT4
Must look at both together
→
High-normal TT4 + ↑fT4
Supports hyperthyroidism

Canine Hyperthyroidism — Quick Note

  • Rare in dogs — most commonly from over-supplementation with L-thyroxine
  • A few case reports of contaminated raw diets (thyroid tissue in food)
  • 90% of thyroid carcinomas in dogs are non-functional — do NOT cause hyperthyroidism
  • Iatrogenic: monitor TT4 during L-thyroxine supplementation and reduce dose if elevated
🐕 Canine Hypothyroidism: Most common endocrine disease in dogs. Primary acquired disease: lymphocytic thyroiditis (immune-mediated) or idiopathic thyroid atrophy. Signs are insidious and mimic many diseases. Must destroy ≥75% of the gland before clinical signs develop.

Signalment & Pathogenesis

  • Medium to large purebred dogs — increased risk
  • Middle-aged or older — mean ~7 years
  • Signs are insidious; often mimic other diseases

Two major lesions:

  • Lymphocytic thyroiditis — immune-mediated; produces autoantibodies
  • Idiopathic thyroid atrophy — may be end-stage lymphocytic thyroiditis

Must destroy ≥75% of gland before clinical hypothyroidism develops.

Clinical Signs

  • Lethargy / exercise intolerance
  • Obesity / weight gain (despite normal or reduced appetite)
  • Cold intolerance
  • Thinning hair / alopecia (bilateral symmetric)
  • Dry or poor-quality hair coat
  • Skin hyperpigmentation / pyoderma / seborrhea
  • Rare: peripheral neuropathy, myopathy, reproductive failure

CBC Findings

Mild normocytic normochromic non-regenerative anemia — anemia of endocrine disease (AID). Thyroid hormone normally supports erythropoiesis; deficiency leads to mild anemia.

Chemistry Panel Findings

FindingFrequencyMechanism
Hypercholesterolemia / hypertriglyceridemia75–80%Thyroid hormones critical for normal lipid metabolism; deficiency → lipid accumulation. Most common chemistry finding.
Mild ↑ CKVariableMyopathy; abnormal muscle metabolism in hypothyroid state
Mild ↑ liver enzymesVariableSecondary hepatic effects; lipid accumulation in hepatocytes
ℹ️ Cross-disease connection: Both hypothyroidism and HAC cause hyperlipidemia. Distinguishing them requires clinical context + thyroid vs. adrenal testing. Hypothyroid = cold, lethargic, obese. HAC = pot belly, alopecia, polyphagia.

Three Tests — TT4 + fT4-ED + TSH

TestSensitivitySpecificityKey Characteristics
TT4HighLowerBest for RULING OUT hypothyroidism. If TT4 >1.5 ng/dL, hypothyroidism unlikely. Can be falsely suppressed by NTI, drugs (glucocorticoids, TMS, anticonvulsants, some NSAIDs). T4AAs can FALSELY NORMALIZE or even increase TT4.
fT4 by equilibrium dialysis (fT4-ED)HighHighGold standard. Equilibrium dialysis removes proteins (including T4AAs) before measuring. Less susceptible to NTI suppression. Low-normal in up to 20% of hypothyroid dogs.
TSHModerateHighHigh in 70–80% of hypothyroid dogs. 20–30% have normal TSH (reason unknown). Relatively unaffected by NTI. High TSH + low T4 = classic pattern.

Thyroid Autoantibodies (TGAA, T3AA, T4AA)

  • Positive in 35–55% of hypothyroid dogs; indicates lymphocytic thyroiditis
  • Never used to diagnose hypothyroidism — only indicates underlying thyroid pathology
  • Positive TGAA alone (with normal T4/TSH) = early or silent disease; dogs may take 1–3 years to progress
  • At 1 year: ~20% progress, ~15% become negative, ~65% stay the same
⚠️ T4AA-specific artifact: Positive T4AA can falsely normalize or increase TT4 when non-dialysis methods are used. This explains a discordant pattern of normal/elevated TT4 with high TSH. Solution: use fT4-ED (dialysis removes antibodies) and measure T4AAs.

Breed & Age Considerations

SituationEffect on TT4Action
Sighthounds, Nordic breeds, English setters, BasenjisNormally LOWER TT4Use breed-specific RI; don't over-diagnose hypothyroidism
Puppies (<4 months)Normally HIGHER TT4Use puppy-specific RI
Non-thyroidal illness (euthyroid sick)Suppressed TT4Avoid testing sick patients! Retest when stable.
Glucocorticoids, TMS, anticonvulsants, some NSAIDsSuppress TT4Note medications before interpreting; stop if possible before testing
📊 Learn to read the TT4 + fT4-ED + TSH triplet. This table is the core of the hypothyroidism lecture — know what each pattern means and why.

Major Diagnostic Patterns (Slide 25 Table)

TT4fT4-EDTSHInterpretationComments / Next steps
NNN Euthyroid (normal) Don't pursue further unless compelling clinical evidence
LLH Hypothyroid — classic Straightforward diagnosis; treat with L-thyroxine
LLN Likely hypothyroid 20–30% of hypothyroid dogs have normal TSH. Rule out severe concurrent disease first.
NNH Early / compensating hypothyroidism Body compensates by ↑TSH. Also: recovery from NTI. Retest in 3–6 months.
LNN Euthyroid sick syndrome TT4 suppressed by illness; fT4 tends to be preserved. Don't treat for hypothyroidism.
NLH Consider T4AA interference T4AAs may be falsely normalizing TT4. Measure T4AAs; use fT4-ED.

Case Example — Maggie (11 yr FS Labrador)

Normal TPR, weight gain, mild progressive ↑BUN, ↑liver enzymes, ↑cholesterol, ↑urine protein/UPCR

TestResultRIFlag
TT412 nmol/L11–60 nmol/LNormal (but borderline)
fT4-ED5 pmol/L6–42 pmol/LLOW ↓
TSH46 mU/L0–30 mU/LHIGH ↑
T4AA32%0–20%Positive
T3AA21%0–10%Positive
TGAA61%<20% neg, 20–35% inconclusive, >35% posPositive
🔎 Pattern = T4AA interference. T4AA likely normalizing TT4. Low fT4-ED + High TSH + Positive autoantibodies = Hypothyroidism due to lymphocytic thyroiditis.
☀️ Hyperadrenocorticism (Cushing's Disease): Excessive cortisol secretion. PDH (pituitary-dependent, 85%) vs. ADH (adrenal-dependent, 15%) vs. iatrogenic. Middle-aged to older dogs. Insidious onset; PU/PD + dermatologic signs dominant.

Clinical Signs

  • PU/PD — cortisol inhibits ADH (antagonizes collecting duct)
  • Polyphagia
  • Pot-belly / abdominal distention (hepatomegaly + muscle atrophy)
  • Muscle weakness and atrophy
  • Panting · Lethargy · Testicular atrophy · Hepatomegaly

Dermatologic Signs (COMMON)

  • Bilaterally symmetric alopecia — spares head and distal extremities
  • Failure to regrow hair after clipping
  • Pyoderma / recurrent skin infections
  • Comedone formation
  • Skin hyperpigmentation
  • Calcinosis cutis (calcium deposits in skin)

HAC Lesions by Type
