TypePrevalencePituitaryAdrenalsACTH
PDH — Pituitary Dependent85%Tumor of pars distalis — excess ACTHBilaterally enlargedHIGH
ADH — Adrenal Dependent15%Suppressed by high cortisolOne adrenal has tumor; opposite adrenal ATROPHIEDLOW
Iatrogenic—SuppressedBoth adrenals ATROPHIEDLOW

CBC Findings

Stress leukogram — neutrophilia, lymphopenia, eosinopenia, monocytosis. Thrombocytosis — occasionally.

Chemistry Panel — High-Yield Findings

FindingFrequencyMechanism
ALP ↑↑ (often >1000 IU/L)90–95%Steroid-induced ALP isoenzyme (C-ALP) + intrahepatic cholestasis from glycogen accumulation. Does NOT correlate with severity.
Mild ALT ↑ (<400 IU/L)CommonHepatocyte swelling from glycogen accumulation (steroid hepatopathy) — not true hepatocellular damage
Hyperlipidemia / hypercholesterolemia~90%Cortisol promotes lipolysis and inhibits peripheral lipid uptake
Mild fasting hyperglycemiaVariableCortisol → gluconeogenesis + insulin resistance. Usually below renal threshold.
Normal to LOW BUNCommonCortisol inhibits ADH → diuresis → urea washout. Low BUN in a dog with PU/PD = suspect HAC.
Normal to LOW creatinineCommonMuscle catabolism → less creatinine produced

Urinalysis Findings

FindingFrequencyNotes
USG <1.015–1.020~85%Dilute urine from ADH inhibition by cortisol
Proteinuria45–65%Mild. Usually does not cause hypoalbuminemia. UPCR recommended.
Glucosuria~5%Concurrent DM only
UTI40–50%ALWAYS culture urine as part of HAC workup — cortisol suppresses immunity; UTIs often subclinical
⚠️ HAC mimic warning: The combination of markedly elevated ALP (often >10× URL) + stress leukogram + low-normal BUN/Cr + dilute urine in a middle-aged dog with pot-belly and alopecia = pursue HAC testing.

Three Screening Tests — Side-by-Side

TestSensitivitySpecificityAdvantagesDisadvantages
UCCRHighLowEasy; owner collects at home (MINIMIZE STRESS). Use to RULE OUT HAC. Normal (<15–20) makes HAC unlikely.Many false positives. Must collect at home. Never diagnose HAC on UCCR alone.
LDDSTVery high
95–99% PDH		Good in non-ill dogsMost sensitive screening test. Can ALSO discriminate PDH from ADH. Multi-time-point test.8-hour test; expensive. Not for monitoring. Cannot diagnose iatrogenic HAC.
ACTH Stimulation TestModerate
60–70% PDH		15–25% false positivesShorter (1–2 hrs). ONLY test for iatrogenic HAC. ONLY test for Addison's. Used for treatment monitoring.Less sensitive than LDDST. Cannot differentiate PDH from ADH. Cross-reacts with administered steroids.

LDDST Interpretation — Cutoffs

8-hr cortisol
>1.4 μg/dL = POSITIVE
<1.4 μg/dL = negative
→
If positive:
examine 4-hr + 8-hr
for suppression pattern
→
4-hr <1.4 OR <50% baseline
OR 8-hr <50% baseline
→ PDH
→
No suppression
→ ADH or PDH
ℹ️ 60% of PDH dogs DO suppress with LDDST. Suppression = definitively PDH. No suppression = could be either — need US and/or eACTH to differentiate.

ACTH Stim Test — Interpretation Cutoffs

Post-stim cortisolInterpretation
<17 μg/dLNormal; if still suspicious, run LDDST
17–22 μg/dLGray zone; run LDDST for further evaluation
>22 μg/dLPositive for HAC
No stimulation / inadequate stimulationIatrogenic HAC — adrenals atrophied from exogenous steroids

Case — Sweet Pea (14 yr, 22 kg mixed breed)

Referred for ↑liver enzymes. PU/PD. PE: panting, pot-belly, cranial organomegaly, hind limb muscle atrophy, dry brittle coat, comedones.

LabValueFlag
Platelets729HIGH ↑
Lymphocytes0.497LOW ↓
BUN10LOW ↓
Cholesterol380HIGH ↑
ALP1513HIGH ↑↑ (~17× URL)
ALT243HIGH ↑
USG1.020Dilute
Proteinuria1+Present
Endocrine TestValueFlag
LDDST Resting2.3 μg/dLNormal baseline
LDDST 4-hr<1.0 μg/dLSUPPRESSED → PDH pattern
LDDST 8-hr1.9 μg/dLNot suppressed → POSITIVE HAC
USBilateral adrenomegalyPDH confirmed
TT40.71 μg/dL (LOW)Concurrent hypothyroidism!
Diagnosis: PDH + concurrent hypothyroidism. Low TT4 is likely from glucocorticoid-induced thyroid suppression. Treat HAC first, recheck TT4 after cortisol normalizes.

Discrimination Tests — PDH vs. ADH

TestPDH resultADH resultNotes
LDDST suppression patternSuppression at 4-hr or 8-hr (<50% baseline or <1.4)No suppression60% of PDH suppress; suppression = definitively PDH.
Endogenous ACTH (eACTH)HIGHLOW to undetectableLabile sample — chilled, plastic tubes, no gel separator
Abdominal ultrasoundBoth adrenals enlargedOne enlarged + one atrophiedFirst-line discrimination per ACVIM consensus
ℹ️ ACVIM consensus: When LDDST shows no suppression, use abdominal US and/or eACTH for discrimination. ACTH stim test cannot discriminate PDH from ADH.

UCCR Example Cases

PatientWhy testedUCCROutcome
SierraAccidents in house, dilute urine with proteinuria, no liver enzyme ↑23 (slightly ↑)Sample collected in-clinic — stress artifact. Glomerular proteinuria, not HAC.
DaleProteinuria, ↑liver enzymes, hepatomegaly, bilateral adrenomegaly on US258 (markedly ↑)ACTH stim positive. HAC confirmed. Trilostane therapy.
AbigailProteinuria, no other HAC signs46 (↑)Inflammatory glomerulonephropathy. Positive UCCR = chronic stress (false positive).
PeliAdrenal mass on US — rule out functional adenoma14 (normal)HAC ruled out. Mass is non-functional.
❄️ Hypoadrenocorticism (Addison's Disease): Adrenocortical hypofunction → decreased aldosterone and/or cortisol. "The great pretender" — waxing and waning vague signs mimicking GI, renal, and neurologic disease. Classic: hyperkalemia + hyponatremia → low Na:K ratio.

Signalment

  • Young to middle-aged — average 4–5 years
  • ~70% female
  • Predisposed breeds: Great Dane, WHWT, Bearded Collie, Poodles (standard/mini/toy), Basset Hounds
  • Familial: Standard Poodle, Portuguese Water Dog, Bearded Collie, Labrador Retriever

Lesion types:

  • Primary (most common) — IM destruction or atrophy of entire adrenal cortex → ↓ aldosterone AND ↓ cortisol
  • Atypical primary — glucocorticoid deficiency but normal electrolytes
  • Iatrogenic — abrupt withdrawal of exogenous glucocorticoids

Clinical Signs

Owner ComplaintsPE Findings
Poor appetite / anorexiaDepression / lethargy
Lethargy / depressionThin body condition
Weight lossWeakness / dehydration
Vomiting / regurgitationShock / collapse
DiarrheaBradycardia (hyperkalemia)
Waxing and waning illnessWeak femoral pulses
PU/PDHypothermia / abdominal pain
❄️ Key clinical feature: Signs wax and wane — bouts of illness that transiently respond to IV fluids and/or steroids. This "roller coaster" history in a young female dog from a predisposed breed = THINK ADDISON'S.

Aldosterone Effects — Why the Electrolyte Pattern

Normal aldosterone
→ Kidney reabsorbs Na
→ Kidney excretes K
→ Kidney excretes H⁺
→
Addison's: no aldosterone
→ Na LOST in urine
→ K RETAINED
→ H⁺ RETAINED (acidosis)
→
Result:
Hyponatremia
Hyperkalemia
Non-gap metabolic acidosis
→
Low Na:K ratio
(<27 in most)
Classic hallmark

CBC Findings

FindingFrequencyMechanism
Mild-to-severe normocytic normochromic non-regenerative anemia20–30% at presentation; 70% after rehydrationLack of cortisol → AID. GI hemorrhage. Masked by hemoconcentration at presentation.
Relative erythrocytosis~10%From dehydration/hemoconcentration — anemia masked
Eosinophilia and/or lymphocytosis10–20%Absence of cortisol = absence of stress response. Lack of stress leukogram in a sick dog = RED FLAG for Addison's.

Chemistry Panel — Electrolytes & Classic Pattern

FindingFrequencyMechanism
Hyperkalemia~95%↓ renal K excretion (no aldosterone). Causes bradycardia; severe = cardiac arrhythmia.
Hyponatremia~86%Renal Na and water wasting (no aldosterone).
Low Na:K ratioMostNormal >27. Addison's: typically <27, often <20. Highly suspicious.
Azotemia (↑BUN, ↑Cr)BUN 85–95%Pre-renal from hypovolemia. Can mimic renal failure. Resolves with fluid therapy.
Hypercalcemia~30%Mechanism uncertain; resolves with treatment
Hypoglycemia~20%Cortisol supports gluconeogenesis
Non-gap metabolic acidosis40–50%Renal bicarbonate loss (H⁺ not excreted).
⚠️ Addison's can perfectly mimic acute renal failure: azotemia + dilute urine (USG <1.030 in ~88%). Key differentiator: low Na:K ratio + response to fluid therapy. ALWAYS rule out Addison's in a young dog with azotemia + electrolyte abnormalities.

Diagnostic Approach — Two Steps

Step 1: Resting cortisol
Inexpensive screening
→
<2 μg/dL
Proceed to ACTH stim
→
>2 μg/dL
Addison's RULED OUT
Step 2: ACTH stim test
Gold standard
→
Post-stim <2 μg/dL
Addison's confirmed
→
Post-stim >2 μg/dL
Addison's unlikely
ℹ️ When else do you see a flat ACTH stim response? Iatrogenic HAC — exogenous steroids suppress the adrenal cortex. Bilateral adrenalectomy. Recent trilostane/mitotane therapy. Context is key.

Primary vs. Atypical Addison's

TypeElectrolytesCortisol (ACTH stim)Aldosterone
Primary (typical)Hyperkalemia + hyponatremiaFlat — both zones destroyedLow to undetectable
AtypicalNormal electrolytes — zona glomerulosa partially sparedFlat — glucocorticoid zone destroyedUsually low or undetectable
⚠️ Don't miss atypical Addison's: Normal Na:K does NOT rule out Addison's. A sick dog with vague waxing/waning signs + flat ACTH stim but normal electrolytes = atypical hypoadrenocorticism.

Case — Noelle (3 yr FS Manchester Terrier)

Acute onset inappropriate mentation + lateral recumbency. History: occasional bouts of diarrhea (thought dietary), difficulty maintaining weight. PE: 5–8% dehydrated, tacky MM, T=95°F (hypothermic), P=120, non-ambulatory, obtunded.

CBC

TestValueFlag
RBC / HCT / HGBAll lowMild anemia
Neutrophils4.493Normal — NO stress leukogram!
Lymphocytes2.246Normal-to-high in a SICK dog
Eosinophils1.165Normal (no eosinopenia)
PP4.6 (LOW)Hypoproteinemia

Chemistry Highlights

TestValueFlag
Glucose57 (LOW)Hypoglycemia
BUN40 (HIGH)Azotemia
Creatinine1.6 (HIGH)Azotemia
Calcium11.5 (HIGH)Hypercalcemia
Sodium135 (LOW)Hyponatremia ↓
Potassium7.7 (HIGH)Hyperkalemia ↑↑
Na:K ratio17.5CRITICALLY LOW (<27)
HCO₃14 (LOW)Metabolic acidosis
USG1.030Concentrated — but azotemic!
❄️ Next step: ACTH stimulation test. Classic Addisonian pattern: hyperkalemia (7.7) + hyponatremia + Na:K 17.5 + azotemia + hypoglycemia + no stress leukogram. Treat immediately with IV fluids + dexamethasone while awaiting results.
ℹ️ The USG is 1.030 — doesn't this rule out Addison's? No. In acute Addisonian crisis, the medullary gradient hasn't washed out yet. USG can range from 1.004–1.050 in Addison's. Concentrated urine + azotemia + low Na:K = Addison's is still top differential.
⚡ 8 clinical vignettes covering all four diseases. Click to flip.
VIGNETTE · FELINE HYPERTHYROIDISM
A 12-year-old cat presents with weight loss despite polyphagia, tachycardia, and a palpable ventral neck mass. TT4 is high-normal at 58 nmol/L (RI 11–60). What is your next test and what result would support hyperthyroidism?
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
Next test: fT4. TT4 is high-normal — add fT4 (never a standalone screening test, but useful when TT4 is high-normal). Supporting result: elevated fT4 + high-normal TT4 together. Elevated fT4 alone is insufficient — some normal cats and NTI cats also have elevated fT4.
VIGNETTE · MASKED RENAL DISEASE
A 13-year-old cat is treated with methimazole for hyperthyroidism. Two months later, BUN and creatinine are now elevated. The owner asks if the medication "caused kidney disease." What is the correct explanation?
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
Pre-existing CKD was unmasked, not caused. Hyperthyroidism ↑GFR, which artificially lowers BUN/Cr — masking underlying renal disease. When corrected, GFR normalizes → CKD becomes apparent. Always re-evaluate renal function after achieving euthyroidism.
VIGNETTE · CANINE HYPOTHYROIDISM PATTERN
An 8-year-old Golden Retriever with weight gain and bilaterally symmetric alopecia has TT4 = 12 nmol/L (RI 11–60, "normal"), fT4-ED = 4 pmol/L (LOW), TSH = 52 mU/L (HIGH). How do you interpret this?
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
Hypothyroidism with T4AA interference. Low fT4-ED + High TSH = hypothyroid despite "normal" TT4. T4AAs bind T4 in the assay, inflating measured TT4. fT4 by equilibrium dialysis removes these antibodies — giving the true (low) value. Measure T4AAs to confirm. Lymphocytic thyroiditis is the underlying cause.
VIGNETTE · EUTHYROID SICK SYNDROME
A dog with severe parvoviral enteritis has TT4 = 6 nmol/L (LOW), fT4-ED = 12 pmol/L (normal), TSH = 8 mU/L (normal). Should you diagnose hypothyroidism and start L-thyroxine?
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
No — euthyroid sick syndrome (ESS). Pattern: low TT4 + normal fT4-ED + normal TSH. NTI suppresses TT4; fT4 tends to be preserved; TSH normal (not elevated as expected in true hypothyroidism). Treat the underlying illness. Retest when stable. Never diagnose hypothyroidism in acutely ill patients.
VIGNETTE · HAC DISCRIMINATION
A dog's LDDST shows: Baseline 3.2, 4-hr = 0.9 (<1.4), 8-hr = 2.6 (>1.4). What type of HAC does this support and why?
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
PDH (pituitary-dependent HAC). 8-hr >1.4 = POSITIVE. 4-hr <1.4 = satisfies PDH suppression criterion. Transient suppression at 4 hrs followed by escape at 8 hrs = hallmark PDH. ADH would show NO suppression at either time point.
VIGNETTE · HAC vs. HYPOTHYROIDISM
Two dogs both present with weight gain, lethargy, and hypercholesterolemia. Dog A has bilateral symmetric alopecia sparing head and legs, dilute urine, ALP of 1,400. Dog B has cold intolerance, thick dull coat, mild CK elevation. Which is which?
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
Dog A = HAC. Alopecia sparing head/extremities + dilute urine + marked ALP = Cushing's. Run LDDST or ACTH stim. Culture urine. Dog B = Hypothyroidism. Cold intolerance + thick coat + CK elevation = classic hypothyroid. Run TT4 + fT4-ED + TSH. Both cause hyperlipidemia — clinical context differentiates.
VIGNETTE · ADDISON'S vs. RENAL FAILURE
A 4-year-old FS Poodle presents obtunded with BUN 45, Cr 2.0, USG 1.018, Na 133, K 7.4 (Na:K = 18), and no stress leukogram. She has had 3 similar episodes this year that resolved with IV fluids. What is the most likely diagnosis?
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
Hypoadrenocorticism (Addison's disease). Young female Poodle + waxing-waning illness + episodic response to fluids + hyperkalemia + hyponatremia + Na:K = 18 + no stress leukogram in a sick dog. First step: resting cortisol. If <2 μg/dL → ACTH stim. Treat empirically with IV fluids + dexamethasone (doesn't cross-react with cortisol assay).
VIGNETTE · IATROGENIC
A dog on long-term prednisone has PU/PD, pot-belly, elevated ALP, bilateral alopecia. ACTH stim: baseline 0.8, post-stim 0.9 (both near zero). What type of HAC is this and what are the adrenal glands doing?
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
Iatrogenic HAC. Exogenous prednisone → suppressed endogenous ACTH → bilateral adrenal cortical atrophy. Adrenal glands cannot respond to ACTH stim because they are atrophied. Flat ACTH stim = iatrogenic, not primary Addison's. If prednisone abruptly withdrawn → iatrogenic Addison's — must taper slowly.
🔴 Hyperglycemia framework: Caused by (1) insufficient insulin secretion, (2) insulin resistance/antagonism, or (3) excess glucose input. Apply this mechanism lens to every cause below — it makes the list learnable, not memorizable.

Physiologic Causes of Hyperglycemia

CauseMechanismKey details
Postprandial Dietary glucose absorption exceeds immediate insulin response Monogastrics only; mild elevation; resolves in ~4 hours. Not seen in ruminants (VFA absorption predominates).
Epinephrine (stress) Epinephrine antagonizes insulin effects; promotes glycogenolysis Short-lived — dissipates when stressor removed. Most profound in cats — can reach 300 mg/dL (may exceed renal threshold → glucosuria without true DM). Key species-specific fact.
Cortisol Promotes gluconeogenesis + insulin resistance/antagonism Endogenous (HAC, PPID) or exogenous (steroid therapy). Increases usually mild.
⚠️ Feline stress hyperglycemia: A stressed cat with a blood glucose of 280 mg/dL and glucosuria does NOT have diabetes mellitus. The stress response alone can reach or exceed the renal threshold (~260–300 mg/dL in cats). Fructosamine is the key discriminating test — it will be normal in stress hyperglycemia.

Pathologic Causes — Mechanism-Based Framework

↓ Insulin secretion
  • Diabetes mellitus (Type 1 / cats) — beta cell destruction or exhaustion → insufficient insulin output
  • Acute pancreatitis — beta cell damage from local inflammation → decreased insulin production (also cortisol from stress)
  • Bovine milk fever — hypocalcemia physically interferes with insulin production and secretion (Ca²⁺ required for exocytosis)
↑ Insulin resistance / antagonism
  • Diabetes mellitus (Type 2 / cats) — peripheral insulin resistance
  • Acromegaly — excess GH → gluconeogenesis + insulin antagonism; classically seen in cats with progesterone-driven GH from mammary tumors
  • Hyperadrenocorticism (HAC) — high cortisol → insulin resistance
  • Equine PPID — high ACTH → elevated cortisol → insulin resistance; also direct pars intermedia effects
  • Hyperthyroidism — thyroxin promotes insulin resistance → mild hyperglycemia
  • Pheochromocytoma — excess catecholamines → insulin antagonism (similar to physiologic epinephrine response, but sustained)
⚠️ Acute pancreatitis mechanism is dual: Both (1) physical damage to beta cells → decreased insulin output AND (2) stress-driven cortisol release → insulin resistance. Neither mechanism alone fully explains the hyperglycemia. If pancreatitis is severe and chronic, it can progress to secondary diabetes mellitus (exocrine pancreatic insufficiency + endocrine failure).
🔑 Milk fever (bovine hypocalcemia): The hyperglycemia here is mechanism-unique — Ca²⁺ is required for insulin exocytosis from beta cells. When serum calcium drops severely (periparturient hypocalcemia), insulin cannot be released normally even though the beta cells are intact. This is transient and resolves with calcium supplementation.

Exogenous / Pharmacologic / Toxicologic Causes

AgentMechanismNotes
Steroids (exogenous) Gluconeogenesis promotion + insulin resistance Anti-inflammatory or immunosuppressive doses; usually mild elevation; same mechanism as endogenous cortisol
Dextrose-containing fluids Direct glucose load exceeds insulin response Expected iatrogenic finding; not pathologic; monitor in diabetics or animals with impaired insulin secretion
Xylazine / Detomidine (α₂ agonists) Inhibit insulin release from pancreatic beta cells (α₂ receptor–mediated) Common in equine and bovine sedation; clinically significant especially in diabetics
Propranolol (β-blocker) Blocks β₂-mediated insulin release + inhibits glycogenolysis recognition (masks hypoglycemia symptoms) Also used in hyperthyroid cats — monitor glucose
Ketamine Various — sympathomimetic effects + catecholamine release Generally mild; clinically significant primarily in already-compromised animals
Ethylene glycol Inhibits glycolysis and Krebs cycle → indirect stimulation of gluconeogenesis Hyperglycemia is early finding; concurrent renal failure (oxalate crystals) and metabolic acidosis dominate the clinical picture
ℹ️ α₂ agonists and glucose: Xylazine and detomidine inhibit insulin release via α₂ receptors on pancreatic beta cells. This is clinically relevant in horses and cattle where these are standard sedatives. Glucosuria can occur if blood glucose rises sufficiently — do not mistake for diabetes. Resolution occurs as drug wears off.

Other / Miscellaneous Causes

ConditionSpeciesMechanismClinical note
Early endotoxemia / sepsis All Insulin resistance from inflammatory mediators Early = hyperglycemia Late = hypoglycemia — biphasic. The transition is clinically important: a previously hyperglycemic septic patient who becomes hypoglycemic has worsened.
Hyperammonemia Horses, cattle Elevated ammonia may stimulate glucagon release → gluconeogenesis Mechanism incompletely understood; seen in hepatic encephalopathy cases
Moribund animals All; especially cattle Likely massive catecholamine and cortisol release + impaired glucose utilization Cattle particularly prone to very high glucose values near death. Do not misinterpret as DM at necropsy.
Colic (horses) Horses Blood shunted from peripheral tissues → glucose not consumed peripherally Proposed mechanism; stress response also contributes; monitor as part of colic workup
⚠️ Sepsis biphasic glucose pattern: Early sepsis → hyperglycemia (insulin resistance). Late/advanced sepsis → hypoglycemia (decreased gluconeogenesis + increased consumption by organisms + endotoxin-mediated decrease in glucose production). Tracking glucose trend in septic patients provides prognostic information.

Hyperglycemia — All Causes at a Glance

CategoryCauseCore mechanism
PhysiologicPostprandial (monogastrics)Glucose absorption; resolves ~4 hr
Epinephrine / stress (cats up to 300 mg/dL)Insulin antagonism; short-lived
Cortisol (endogenous)Gluconeogenesis + insulin resistance
PathologicDiabetes mellitus↓ Insulin secretion and/or ↑ insulin resistance
AcromegalyGH → gluconeogenesis + insulin antagonism
HyperadrenocorticismHigh cortisol → insulin resistance
Equine PPIDHigh ACTH → cortisol → insulin resistance
HyperthyroidismThyroxin → insulin resistance (mild)
PheochromocytomaCatecholamines → insulin antagonism
Bovine milk feverHypocalcemia → impairs insulin secretion
Acute pancreatitisBeta cell damage + cortisol (dual)
ExogenousSteroids (exogenous)Same as cortisol; usually mild
Dextrose-containing fluidsDirect glucose load
Xylazine/detomidine, propranolol, ketamineVarious; inhibit insulin release or action
Ethylene glycolInhibit glycolysis → indirect gluconeogenesis
OtherEarly endotoxemiaInsulin resistance
Hyperammonemia (horses, cattle)Glucagon stimulation
Moribund animals (esp. cattle)Catecholamines + impaired utilization
Colic (horses)Peripheral blood shunting
🔵 Hypoglycemia framework: Caused by (1) increased insulin secretion, (2) decreased insulin antagonism, (3) decreased gluconeogenesis, (4) increased glucose utilization, or (5) artifactual. Always rule out pre-analytical artifact first — it's the most common cause.

Artifactual (Pre-Analytical) Hypoglycemia

CauseMechanismMagnitudeKey rule
Delayed processing / sample left on clot Erythrocytes and leukocytes remain metabolically active after clotting → continue consuming glucose in vitro ~10% decrease per hour Most common cause of artifactual hypoglycemia. Separate serum promptly. Use sodium fluoride (NaF) tube if delay anticipated.
Extreme leukocytosis Large leukocyte mass consumes excess glucose in vitro Mild Uncommon; effect compounded by delayed processing. Clinical context: marked leukemia-level WBC counts.
Erythrocytosis Increased RBC mass → excess glucose consumption in vitro Mild Uncommon; again compounded by delay in processing.
⚠️ Always rule out artifact first. Before working up a patient for insulinoma or other serious causes of hypoglycemia, confirm the sample was processed promptly. A glucose of 45 mg/dL in a blood tube that sat at room temperature for 4 hours could represent a true glucose of ~65 mg/dL — within or near the lower reference interval. Repeat with fresh sample if suspicious.
ℹ️ NaF (sodium fluoride) tubes: For situations where glucose processing will be delayed, NaF tubes inhibit glycolysis and preserve glucose concentration in vitro. Point-of-care glucometers also avoid this issue by measuring immediately.

Pathologic Hypoglycemia — By Mechanism

↑ Insulin secretion

Insulinoma (functional pancreatic beta cell neoplasm)

  • Middle-aged to older dogs most common (cats rare)
  • Autonomous, unregulated insulin secretion — does not respond to hypoglycemia feedback
  • Classic: episodic collapse/seizures after exercise or fasting (when glycogen stores depleted)
  • Whipple's triad: (1) clinical signs of hypoglycemia, (2) low blood glucose during signs, (3) signs resolve with glucose supplementation
↓ Insulin antagonism

Hypoadrenocorticism (Addison's disease)

  • Hypocortisolemia → tissues are more sensitive to insulin → enhanced glucose uptake
  • Truly low glucose is relatively uncommon — more often trending low or at the lower end of the reference interval
  • Always consider in a dog with vague GI signs + electrolyte abnormalities (hyponatremia, hyperkalemia)

Pathologic Hypoglycemia — Continued

↓ Gluconeogenesis
  • Hepatic insufficiency: Liver is the primary site of gluconeogenesis; advanced liver disease → impaired glucose production. Usually trending low rather than severely low; other liver markers will be markedly abnormal.
  • Neonatal/juvenile hypoglycemia: Young toy/mini breed dogs; relative liver immaturity → impaired gluconeogenesis between feedings. Susceptible to hypoglycemia when meals are skipped.
  • Advanced starvation: Fat and muscle stores depleted → no gluconeogenic substrates → impaired gluconeogenesis. Seen only in severely emaciated animals.
↑ Glucose utilization
  • Advanced pregnancy: Multiple fetuses + poor diet → demand exceeds supply. Especially sheep (pregnancy toxemia).
  • Lactation: Peak lactation → excess glucose demand; ketosis from fatty acid metabolism may develop. Dairy cattle most affected.
  • Exertional hypoglycemia: Consumption exceeds gluconeogenesis rate. Hunting dogs, sledding dogs, endurance racing horses. Prolonged intense exertion.
🔑 Insulinoma diagnostic approach: Document concurrent low glucose + elevated (or inappropriately normal) insulin → amended insulin:glucose ratio. A dog with glucose of 40 mg/dL should have essentially undetectable insulin. Any measurable insulin in that context is "inappropriately high" = functional insulin-secreting tumor until proven otherwise.

Exogenous / Toxicologic Causes of Hypoglycemia

CauseMechanismClinical notes
Insulin overdose (absolute) Excess exogenous insulin → excessive glucose uptake by peripheral tissues Owner errors: wrong dose, dosed twice, used wrong syringe type (U-40 vs U-100). Treat with dextrose bolus + adjust dose.
Insulin overdose (relative) Insulin dose previously appropriate but patient's requirements have decreased Disease remission (especially cats), weight loss, improved diet. Monitor for signs of overregulation.
Oral hypoglycemic agents (e.g., glipizide) Sulfonylureas stimulate beta cell insulin release regardless of glucose level Used in cats with Type 2 DM. Overdose → hypoglycemia; less commonly used now that injectable insulin preferred.
Xylitol excess (dogs) Potent stimulator of insulin release from pancreatic beta cells — unique to dogs (cats less sensitive) Low doses (≥0.1 g/kg): hyperinsulinemia → profound hypoglycemia. Higher doses (≥0.5 g/kg): also direct hepatotoxicity and liver failure. Treat with dextrose + liver support.
⚠️ Xylitol dual dose-dependent toxicity: Low dose → hypoglycemia only. High dose → hypoglycemia PLUS acute liver failure. The two effects are independent mechanisms. Dogs that present with xylitol ingestion of >0.5 g/kg should be monitored for both hypoglycemia (dextrose) and hepatotoxicity (ALT, bilirubin, coagulation panel). Cats appear relatively insensitive — the mechanism for species difference is unclear.

Multifactorial / Paraneoplastic Hypoglycemia

Paraneoplastic hypoglycemia

  • Tumors associated: leiomyosarcoma (most common non-beta cell tumor causing hypoglycemia), hepatocellular carcinoma, renal carcinoma
  • Mechanism 1: secretion of insulin-like growth factors (IGF-1, IGF-2) that activate insulin receptors
  • Mechanism 2: excessive glucose consumption by large tumors (particularly leiomyosarcomas)
  • Key distinction from insulinoma: insulin levels are LOW (not elevated) — the tumor is not secreting insulin, it's secreting IGFs

Sepsis-associated hypoglycemia

  • Multifactorial — at least three simultaneous mechanisms:
  • 1. Decreased hepatic glucose production (gluconeogenesis impaired by hepatic dysfunction)
  • 2. Increased glucose consumption by inflamed tissues
  • 3. Consumption by microorganisms (bacteria consume glucose)
  • 4. Endotoxin-mediated decreased glucose production
  • Late sepsis hypoglycemia = poor prognostic sign
⚠️ Paraneoplastic vs insulinoma: Both cause hypoglycemia in middle-aged to older dogs. Distinguishing feature: measure insulin during hypoglycemia. Insulinoma → insulin elevated or inappropriately normal. Paraneoplastic (leiomyosarcoma/HCC) → insulin appropriately LOW (the hypoglycemia is not insulin-driven). Imaging to find the tumor is the next step.

Hypoglycemia — All Causes at a Glance

CategoryCauseCore mechanism
ArtifactualDelayed processing (most common)~10% decrease/hour from in vitro cell glucose consumption
Extreme leukocytosisExcess WBC consumption; mild
ErythrocytosisExcess RBC consumption; mild
PathologicInsulinoma↑ Insulin secretion — autonomous beta cell tumor
Hypoadrenocorticism↓ Insulin antagonism — trending low more than severely low
Hepatic insufficiency↓ Gluconeogenesis — advanced disease
Neonatal/juvenile hypoglycemia↓ Gluconeogenesis — liver immaturity; toy/mini breeds
Advanced starvation↓ Gluconeogenesis — substrate depletion
Advanced pregnancy / lactation↑ Glucose utilization — excess fetal/neonatal demand
Exertional (hunting/sledding dogs, race horses)↑ Glucose utilization > gluconeogenesis rate
ExogenousInsulin overdose (absolute or relative)Excess insulin → excess uptake
Oral hypoglycemics (glipizide)Stimulate insulin release
Xylitol (dogs)Potent insulin secretagogue; also hepatotoxic at high doses
MultifactorialParaneoplastic (leiomyosarcoma, HCC, renal carcinoma)IGF secretion + tumor glucose consumption; insulin is LOW
Advanced sepsis↓ Production + ↑ consumption + endotoxin effects
🔬 Fructosamine: A glycated plasma protein (primarily albumin) formed when sustained hyperglycemia causes covalent glucose-protein bonds. Reflects average glucose concentration over the prior 2–3 weeks. The preferred glycated protein test in veterinary medicine — does NOT increase with transient/stress hyperglycemia.

What Is Fructosamine?

  • Glycated plasma proteins form when glucose reacts with the amino group of proteins (Maillard reaction) to form a stable covalent bond
  • The level is proportionate to both the magnitude and the duration of hyperglycemia
  • In veterinary medicine, fructosamine measures glycated albumin primarily (albumin t½ = 2–3 weeks → reflects that time window)
  • Human medicine uses HbA1c (glycated hemoglobin, reflects ~3 months) — less validated in veterinary species because RBC lifespan differs

Glycated Protein Formation — Why Duration Matters

Sustained
hyperglycemia
(days–weeks)
→
Glucose + albumin
Maillard reaction
Covalent bond
→
Fructosamine ↑
Integrated 2–3 wk
glucose record
Transient
hyperglycemia
(minutes–hours)
→
Brief glucose spike
Insufficient time
for bond formation
→
Fructosamine NORMAL
Stress/epinephrine
hyperglycemia

Primary Uses in Veterinary Medicine

Main use: DM monitoring

  • Assess average glucose control over the preceding 2–3 weeks
  • Identifies periods of poor control not captured by single glucose measurements
  • Useful when owner-measured home glucose curves are unavailable

Secondary use: DM vs. stress hyperglycemia

  • Particularly relevant in cats (stress can reach 300 mg/dL)
  • Cat with high in-clinic glucose + normal fructosamine → stress, not DM
  • Cat with high in-clinic glucose + elevated fructosamine → sustained hyperglycemia → DM likely

Interpretation: What Causes Fructosamine to Rise or Fall?

Fructosamine changeCauseMechanism
↑ Elevated fructosamine Untreated diabetes mellitus Sustained hyperglycemia → progressive albumin glycation
Poorly controlled / under-regulated DM Ongoing hyperglycemia despite insulin therapy → continued glycation
↓ Decreased fructosamine Over-regulated DM (insulin excess) Sustained hypoglycemia → less glycation; also albumin turnover may be increased
Severe hypoproteinemia Less albumin available for glycation → fewer total glycated proteins; result is falsely low
Hyperthyroidism Increased protein turnover (faster albumin replacement) → shorter window for glycation → fructosamine appears lower than expected
⚠️ Critical limitation — hypoproteinemia: Fructosamine is a concentration measurement of glycated albumin. If total albumin is severely low (protein-losing enteropathy, liver failure, PLE), there is simply less substrate to glycate. A patient with severe hypoalbuminemia can have a "normal" or low fructosamine despite sustained hyperglycemia — the result is falsely reassuring. Always interpret fructosamine alongside total protein/albumin.
🔬 Hyperthyroidism and fructosamine: Feline hyperthyroidism accelerates protein turnover throughout the body — albumin is degraded and replaced faster than normal. The glycated albumin that forms is cleared more quickly, so fructosamine levels appear lower than the glucose record would predict. This is why fructosamine is unreliable for DM monitoring in a cat that is also hyperthyroid. Treat hyperthyroidism first, then reassess DM control.

Fructosamine — Key Exam Points Summary

QuestionAnswer
What does fructosamine measure?Average glucose over the prior 2–3 weeks (reflects albumin glycation)
Does stress hyperglycemia in cats increase fructosamine?No — too transient. This is its primary diagnostic advantage.
What is the preferred glycated protein test in veterinary medicine?Fructosamine (not HbA1c)
Why is fructosamine low in severe hypoproteinemia?Insufficient albumin substrate → fewer glycated proteins measured
Why is fructosamine low in hyperthyroidism?Increased protein turnover → faster albumin clearance → less glycation time
When is fructosamine elevated?Untreated DM or poorly controlled DM
When might fructosamine be falsely normal despite DM?Concurrent severe hypoproteinemia or hyperthyroidism
⚡ 6 clinical vignettes — covering glucose abnormalities, fructosamine interpretation, and mechanism-based differentials. Click each card to flip.
VIGNETTE · FELINE · STRESS VS DM
A 7-year-old domestic shorthair cat is presented for wellness. Glucose = 310 mg/dL. Urinalysis shows glucosuria. Fructosamine = 225 µmol/L (reference interval 190–365). The cat was stressed and hissing during blood draw. What is your interpretation and next step?
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
Stress hyperglycemia, NOT diabetes mellitus. Cats can have stress-induced glucose up to ~300 mg/dL (rarely higher) from epinephrine release — this is enough to exceed the renal threshold and produce glucosuria. The normal fructosamine confirms that sustained hyperglycemia has NOT been present over the past 2–3 weeks. If this were true DM, fructosamine would be elevated. Next step: re-check glucose in a calm environment (home glucometer or quiet revisit) and repeat UA. No insulin therapy warranted at this time.
VIGNETTE · HYPOGLYCEMIA · MECHANISM
A 9-year-old male Labrador presents with episodic collapse after runs in the yard. Glucose = 38 mg/dL. Insulin level drawn at the same time = 48 µIU/mL (reference interval 5–20). What is the diagnosis and what does the concurrent insulin tell you?
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
Insulinoma (functional pancreatic beta cell neoplasm). At a glucose of 38 mg/dL, insulin should be essentially undetectable — normal feedback would completely suppress secretion. An insulin of 48 µIU/mL is dramatically "inappropriately elevated" for this glucose level. This is Whipple's triad: (1) signs of hypoglycemia (collapse), (2) documented low glucose during signs, (3) clinical signs resolve with glucose. Autonomous insulin secretion from the tumor is the mechanism. Next: abdominal ultrasound to localize the pancreatic mass; surgical exploration if not seen on imaging.
VIGNETTE · ARTIFACTUAL
A blood sample is drawn from a healthy 5-year-old Beagle at 8 AM. The serum is not separated and the tube sits at room temperature until 12 PM when the analyzer runs it. Glucose = 51 mg/dL. The repeat same-day sample processed immediately = 88 mg/dL. Explain the discrepancy.
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
Artifactual hypoglycemia from delayed processing. Blood cells (primarily erythrocytes) remain metabolically active after clotting and continue to consume glucose in vitro. Rate: approximately 10% decrease per hour. Sample sat for 4 hours → expected ~40% decrease (88 → ~53 mg/dL; observed 51 mg/dL — consistent). This is the most common cause of artifactual hypoglycemia in practice. Prevention: promptly separate serum from clot, use NaF tube when delay anticipated, or use point-of-care glucometer immediately. Never start a hypoglycemia workup without confirming proper sample handling.
VIGNETTE · HYPERGLYCEMIA · PATHOLOGIC
A 10-year-old Thoroughbred gelding has a shaggy coat, muscle wasting, polyuria/polydipsia, and a pot-bellied appearance. Glucose = 165 mg/dL. ACTH is markedly elevated. What disease is present, what is the mechanism of hyperglycemia, and what other endocrine disorder should you consider alongside it?
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
Equine Pituitary Pars Intermedia Dysfunction (PPID). Shaggy coat (hypertrichosis), PU/PD, muscle wasting, and pot-belly are classic. High ACTH → elevated cortisol → gluconeogenesis + insulin resistance → hyperglycemia. Associated disorder: Equine Metabolic Syndrome (EMS) — insulin dysregulation/resistance can occur concurrently or independently. The combination of PPID + EMS significantly increases laminitis risk. Basal ACTH + TRH stimulation test confirms PPID. Fasting insulin + glucose used to assess EMS component. Treatment: pergolide (dopamine agonist).
VIGNETTE · FRUCTOSAMINE · INTERPRETATION
A diabetic cat has been on insulin for 6 months. Owner reports good clinical response (less PU/PD). In-clinic glucose = 95 mg/dL. Fructosamine = 178 µmol/L (RI 190–365). What does the low fructosamine suggest and what should you do next?
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
Over-regulation of diabetes — the insulin dose is too high. Low fructosamine in a known diabetic indicates that glucose has been averaging BELOW normal over the past 2–3 weeks, not just at this measurement. The cat is being run too hypoglycemic. Check: (1) is there concurrent hypoproteinemia? (albumin low would falsely lower fructosamine — check total protein). (2) Is the cat hyperthyroid? (increased protein turnover falsely lowers fructosamine). If albumin and thyroid function are normal, reduce the insulin dose and recheck glucose curves. Chronic mild hypoglycemia is a risk for Somogyi effect (rebound hyperglycemia) and clinical hypoglycemic episodes.
VIGNETTE · HYPOGLYCEMIA · XYLITOL
A 3-year-old Jack Russell terrier ate sugar-free gum 2 hours ago. Now laterally recumbent and seizing. Glucose = 24 mg/dL. What is the toxin, its specific mechanism in dogs, and what is the dual dose-dependent concern at higher ingestion amounts?
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
Xylitol toxicosis. Mechanism: xylitol is a potent and rapid stimulator of insulin release from pancreatic beta cells in dogs (cats are relatively insensitive). Even low doses (≥0.1 g/kg) cause a large insulin surge → profound hypoglycemia within 30–60 minutes. Immediate treatment: IV dextrose bolus (0.5 mL/kg 50% dextrose diluted) + CRI dextrose supplementation. Dual concern at higher doses (≥0.5 g/kg): acute hepatotoxicity develops 12–24 hours later, independent of the hypoglycemia mechanism. Monitor ALT, bilirubin, and coagulation panel. Treat with IV dextrose + liver-protective agents (SAMe, N-acetylcysteine). Report common sources: sugar-free gum, peanut butter, dental products.
🟡 Diabetes mellitus: Persistent hyperglycemia from insufficient insulin secretion (Type I) or insulin resistance (Type II). Dogs almost always Type I; cats usually Type II but can transition to Type I. Diagnosis: persistent hyperglycemia + glucosuria + compatible clinical signs.

Type I vs Type II — Species Breakdown

Type I — ↓ Insulin secretion

Dogs: nearly 100%

  • Immune-mediated destruction of beta cells
  • Chronic relapsing pancreatitis → progressive beta cell loss

Some cats:

  • Amyloid deposition → beta cell degeneration
  • Glucose toxicity — sustained hyperglycemia itself contributes to beta cell dysfunction (secondary Type I)
Type II — Insulin resistance

Most cats

  • Obesity, poor diet, sedentary lifestyle → peripheral insulin resistance
  • Can transition to Type I over time (beta cell exhaustion)
  • Still require insulin therapy even when Type II

Rare in dogs:

  • Hyperadrenocorticism — high cortisol → insulin resistance
  • Exogenous steroid administration (same mechanism)

Signalment — Who Gets DM?

FeatureDogsCats
Peak age7–10 years6–10 years
Sex predispositionFemales > males (immune-mediated pattern)Neutered males predominantly
Breed predispositionSamoyeds, Keeshonds, Poodles, Miniature Schnauzers reportedNot identified
Dominant typeType I (~100%)Type II (most), can progress to Type I
⚠️ Glucose toxicity in cats: Sustained hyperglycemia is itself toxic to beta cells, impairing insulin secretion further. A cat that begins as Type II can progress to functional Type I. This is why early, aggressive glucose control in newly diagnosed diabetic cats can sometimes allow remission — reversing glucose toxicity before permanent beta cell damage occurs.

History & Clinical Signs

Hallmark signs (both species)

  • Weight loss despite good to ravenous appetite — cells can't use glucose; catabolism of fat and muscle for energy
  • PU/PD — glucosuria → osmotic diuresis → compensatory polydipsia

Additional signs

  • Bilateral cataracts: dogs >> cats — sorbitol accumulation in lens from aldose reductase activity; can develop rapidly in dogs
  • Dry, dull haircoat
  • Weakness, lethargy
  • Increased susceptibility to infections — acquired neutrophil dysfunction; bacteria love glucose
  • Concurrent pancreatitis: dogs primarily

Cat-specific findings

  • Plantigrade stance — diabetic peripheral neuropathy; cat walks on hocks rather than toes. Unique to cats (rarely seen in dogs).
  • Cataracts less common than dogs
  • Often obese at diagnosis (Type II background)
🐱
SLIDE 6 — PLANTIGRADE STANCE (PHOTO)
Cat walking on hocks (plantigrade) due to diabetic peripheral neuropathy. Myelin sheath degeneration from sustained hyperglycemia. Can be reversible with glucose control.
Plantigrade stance = feline DM until proven otherwise

CBC — Findings in DM

FindingExplanation
Often unremarkable (uncomplicated DM)Healthy controlled diabetics may have a completely normal CBC
↑ PCV + ↑ plasma proteinDehydration from osmotic diuresis (not drinking enough to compensate)
Inflammatory leukogram (neutrophilia ± left shift)Concurrent infection — always culture urine; diabetics at high risk of occult UTI

Chemistry Panel — Findings in DM

FindingMechanismKey notes
Hyperglycemia (fasting >200–300 mg/dL)Insufficient insulin → glucose cannot enter cellsTypically exceeds renal threshold → glucosuria. If low, consider artifact or stress hyperglycemia.
HyperlipidemiaInsulin deficiency → increased lipolysis → elevated triglycerides and cholesterolMay only be finding in otherwise healthy diabetic. Lipemic serum can interfere with other analytes.
Mild ↑ ALP and ALTVacuolar hepatopathy from lipid accumulation in hepatocytesNot from primary liver disease; secondary to DM. Moderate elevation.
Azotemia (variable)Pre-renal from dehydrationPresent only if dehydrated. USG will be reduced (glucosuria effect — see UA).
Mild hyponatremia + hypochloremiaDilutional — glucose is an active osmol that pulls water from interstitial/cellular space into vascular spaceKey exam point: Normal Na in face of marked chronic hyperglycemia = dehydration. The expected finding is low Na; if Na is normal, the patient is more dehydrated than they appear.
↑ Lipase (if concurrent pancreatitis)Pancreatitis is common concurrent disease, especially in dogsDogs: get fPLI or cPLI to evaluate. Pancreatitis may have triggered or worsened DM.

Urinalysis — Findings in DM

FindingMechanism / Notes
4+ Glucosuria (typically marked)Blood glucose exceeds renal threshold (dogs ~180 mg/dL, cats ~260–300 mg/dL). Most diabetics have marked glucosuria.
USG 1.025–1.035Glucosuria → osmotic diuresis → medullary washout → reduced concentrating ability. Counter-intuitive: the solute load keeps USG from going too low. If USG <1.020, look for additional mechanisms (renal disease, etc.)
Mild proteinuriaTwo causes: (1) glomerular damage from chronic hyperglycemia, (2) UTI. Always culture!
Trace ketonuriaNormal in controlled diabetics. If >trace → heading toward DKA. Monitor closely.
Sediment: ± WBCs, RBCs, bacteriaBacteria love glucose → high risk of occult UTI. Culture ALL diabetic patients at initial workup regardless of sediment findings.
⚠️ Always culture the urine. Diabetic patients have acquired neutrophil dysfunction AND glucosuria provides a nutrient-rich environment for bacteria. Many will have occult UTI with a completely unremarkable sediment. Missing an occult UTI means missing a concurrent disease that can make DM impossible to control. Culture is mandatory at initial workup.
🔴 Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Life-threatening complication of DM where insulin deficiency drives uncontrolled lipolysis → excess free fatty acids → hepatic ketoacid production → metabolic acidosis + profound electrolyte derangements. Most have a concurrent triggering disorder.

DKA Pathogenesis — The Ketone Cascade

Insulin ↓↓
Uninhibited
lipolysis
→
↑ Free fatty acids
Delivered to
liver
→
↑ Ketoacid
production
(β-oxidation)
→
Titrational
metabolic acidosis
↓ HCO₃⁻ ↑ AG
→
Electrolyte
derangements
K, P, Na losses

Why Doesn't Every Diabetic Get DKA?

  • Not all untreated diabetics develop DKA — the mechanism is not fully understood
  • Some animals on insulin still develop DKA — likely due to relative insulin deficiency + concurrent stressor
  • Most DKA patients have a concurrent triggering disorder: infection, pancreatitis, other hormonal disease (HAC, acromegaly, PPID), or any significant illness that increases counter-regulatory hormones
  • Counter-regulatory hormones (cortisol, glucagon, catecholamines) further suppress insulin action and promote ketogenesis
🔑 Clinical implication: Always look for the concurrent disease when treating DKA. Treating ketoacidosis without addressing the triggering cause will result in relapse. Pancreatitis and infections are the most common triggers.

History & Clinical Signs — DKA Progression

Early stage — diabetic ketosis
  • Signs similar to uncomplicated DM (PU/PD, weight loss, good appetite)
  • Distinguishing feature: markedly increased urine ketones (>trace)
  • Patient may appear relatively stable at this stage
Late stage — diabetic ketoacidosis
  • Lethargy → depression → obtundation
  • Anorexia
  • Vomiting (ketones + metabolic acidosis)
  • Abdominal pain if concurrent pancreatitis

PE Findings — DKA

FindingMechanism
Dehydration (often profound)Osmotic diuresis + vomiting + inadequate intake
Depression / weaknessMetabolic acidosis + electrolyte derangements
VomitingKetones + acidosis irritate GI tract; also from concurrent pancreatitis
Acetone-like breathAcetone (one of the 3 ketone bodies) is volatile and exhaled. Pathognomonic odor.
Kussmaul respirations (slow, deep)Compensatory respiratory response to severe metabolic acidosis — hyperventilation reduces pCO₂ to partially correct pH
Abdominal painConsider concurrent pancreatitis; also from mesenteric ischemia
⚠️ Kussmaul respirations are a late finding indicating severe metabolic acidosis. Deep, labored breathing without respiratory disease in a vomiting, lethargic, diabetic-history patient is a DKA emergency.

DKA Chemistry Panel — All Key Findings

AnalyteExpected findingMechanism — High-yield explanation
GlucoseHigher than non-ketotic DMVolume contraction limits renal glucose excretion → glucose accumulates in blood to higher levels than typical uncomplicated DM
BUN/CreatininePre-renal azotemiaSevere dehydration from osmotic diuresis + vomiting. Note: USG will NOT be maximally concentrated due to glucosuria
Bicarbonate / CO₂↓ LowKetoacids (acetoacetate, β-hydroxybutyrate) consume bicarbonate buffer → titrational metabolic acidosis
Anion gap↑ HighKetoacid anions replace bicarbonate → high AG (unmeasured anions) metabolic acidosis
SodiumUsually ↓ low (may be normal with dehydration)Dilutional (glucose osmol) + osmotic diuresis losses + ketones excreted as sodium salts. Total body Na is ALWAYS low regardless of serum level.
PotassiumVariable — low, normal, or highSee K/P sub-tab for detailed explanation. Total body K is ALWAYS low.
PhosphorusOften ↓ low (may be normal or high)See K/P sub-tab. Total body P is ALWAYS low. Critical threshold: P <1 mg/dL → hemolysis risk.
Lipase / amylase↑ if concurrent pancreatitisCommon DKA trigger; also can be incidentally elevated in DKA without true pancreatitis from multiple mechanisms

DKA Urinalysis

  • Significant ketonuria — the key distinguisher from uncomplicated DM (which has trace to negative ketones)
  • All other findings similar to uncomplicated DM: 4+ glucosuria, USG 1.025–1.035, mild proteinuria, ± sediment changes
  • Culture urine — same infection risk as in uncomplicated DM, possibly higher due to concurrent illness

Potassium in DKA — The Critical Electrolyte

Why serum K is variable

Serum K can be low, normal, or high (~50% are low at presentation). This is because serum K reflects shifting between compartments, not total body stores.

Forces pushing K into blood (elevating serum K):

  • Acidosis → H⁺ enters cells, K⁺ exits (electroneutral exchange)
  • Hyperosmolality → K solvent drag out of cells
  • Insulin deficiency → less K uptake by cells

Why total body K is ALWAYS low

  • Osmotic diuresis → renal K losses
  • Ketones excreted as potassium salts → additional renal loss
  • Anorexia → no dietary K intake
  • Vomiting → GI losses
⚠️ When you give insulin: Insulin drives K into cells → serum K will DROP rapidly. If K is already low, treatment can cause fatal hypokalemia. ALWAYS supplement K before or with insulin administration.

Phosphorus in DKA — The Hemolysis Risk

P parallels K in many ways

  • Serum P often low, may be normal or high (shifting)
  • Hyperosmolality + acidosis + insulin deficiency → shift P from intracellular to extracellular → subsequent renal loss
  • Total body P is always low regardless of serum level

Critical threshold

When insulin is given → P driven into cells → serum P can drop dangerously.

Serum P <1 mg/dL = HIGH RISK of intravascular hemolysis

Phosphorus is essential for RBC membrane integrity (2,3-DPG synthesis). Monitor P every 4–6 hours during insulin treatment.

The "Treat With Insulin" Monitoring Protocol — What Happens to Each Electrolyte

ElectrolyteBefore insulinDuring/after insulinWhy
Potassium (K)Variable (serum); low (total body)Will drop — supplement K in fluidsInsulin drives K into cells; must supplement before giving insulin if K already low
Phosphorus (P)Variable (serum); low (total body)Will drop — monitor every 4–6 hrInsulin drives P into cells; P <1 mg/dL → hemolysis
Sodium (Na)Low (dilutional + losses)Rises as glucose fallsAs glucose drops, less osmotic pull of water into vascular space → Na rises
GlucoseVery highFalls with insulinDon't drop glucose too fast — can cause cerebral edema
BicarbonateLow (metabolic acidosis)Rises as ketones clearKetones metabolized as insulin works → acid load decreases → HCO₃⁻ recovers
🐱 Feline conditions causing secondary DM: Both feline HAC and acromegaly present as poorly controlled diabetes. Think of them whenever a diabetic cat cannot be regulated despite appropriate insulin doses. The clue is insulin resistance — a cat needing unusually high insulin doses needs an underlying cause investigated.

Feline Hyperadrenocorticism — Overview

Lesions

  • 75–80%: Pituitary-dependent (PDH) — bilateral adrenal hyperplasia from excess ACTH
  • 20–25%: Adrenal-dependent (ADH) — functional adrenal tumor
  • Less common than dogs (important clinical fact)

Clinical signs

  • PU/PD (most common) — caused by secondary poorly controlled DM
  • Fragile, easily bruised skin (second most common) — handle with extreme care: degloving injuries can occur
  • NOT pot-bellied, not as dramatic as dogs

Minimum database findings

  • Hyperglycemia, glucosuria
  • Hypercholesterolemia
  • Mildly increased ALT
  • Proteinuria, pyuria, bacteriuria
🔑 No steroid ALP isoenzyme in cats! Dogs with HAC have massively elevated ALP (steroid isoenzyme induction). Cats lack this isoenzyme — so feline HAC does NOT cause the dramatic ALP rise seen in dogs. Missing this distinction leads to missed diagnoses.
⚠️ When to screen for feline HAC: If you have a diabetic cat that cannot be adequately controlled despite appropriate insulin doses and diet — screen for feline HAC (and acromegaly). The secondary diabetes is driven by cortisol-induced insulin resistance; treating DM alone without addressing the cortisol excess is insufficient.

Feline Acromegaly — Pathophysiology

  • Excess growth hormone secretion from a functional adenoma of the pars distalis (somatotropic cells)
  • GH has anabolic effects → overgrowth of connective tissue, bone, and viscera
  • GH promotes insulin resistance → secondary diabetes is nearly universal at diagnosis
  • Most common in older male cats
  • Degree of insulin resistance can be extreme — patients may remain uncontrolled on very high insulin doses

Clinical Signs — Biphasic Weight Pattern (Key Exam Point)

Early disease
GH ↑
→ DM develops
Weight LOSS
→
GH anabolic effects
dominate
Weight GAIN
→
Late disease
Anabolic overgrowth
Progressive

This biphasic weight pattern is a distinguishing clinical clue: the cat loses weight as DM symptoms predominate, then begins gaining weight DESPITE continued poorly controlled diabetes as GH anabolic effects take over.

Later developing signsExplanation
Large cat, disproportionately large head/feet/abdomenConnective tissue and visceral overgrowth from excess GH anabolic action
Widened bridge of noseFacial bone and connective tissue enlargement
Prognathia inferior (protruding mandible)Seen in ~1/3 of cases; mandibular bone overgrowth
Respiratory stridorUpper airway soft tissue overgrowth
🐱 Clinical tip: Changes are insidious — ask owners to bring an old photo of the cat for comparison. The physical changes accumulate slowly and may not be obvious without a baseline reference.

Clinical Pathology Findings — Acromegaly

From secondary uncontrolled DM (most findings)

  • Hyperglycemia, glucosuria
  • Hypercholesterolemia
  • Mild ↑ ALP and ALT

GH-specific additional findings

  • Mild hyperphosphatemia WITHOUT azotemia — GH promotes phosphate retention; unique finding vs other causes of hyperphosphatemia
  • Mild erythrocytosis — GH promotes erythropoiesis
  • Persistent hyperglobulinemia — mechanism uncertain

Diagnostic Testing — Acromegaly

TestResult interpretationNotes
Serum IGF-1 (insulin-like growth factor-1) <70 nmol/L = negative
70–100 nmol/L = grey zone
>100 nmol/L = positive		 Indirect test — GH induces IGF-1. Preferred initial screen. If grey zone or negative but still suspicious: retest in 3–6 months.
MRI or CT (pituitary) May visualize pituitary adenoma Advanced imaging; confirms presence and size of tumor. May be normal early.
ℹ️ Testing is indirect because GH levels are pulsatile and unreliable as a single measurement. IGF-1 is a more stable downstream marker induced by GH — it reflects cumulative GH excess over time, analogous to how fructosamine reflects cumulative glucose.

Feline HAC vs Acromegaly — Side by Side

FeatureFeline HACFeline Acromegaly
Primary hormone excessCortisolGrowth hormone (GH)
Tumor location75–80% pituitary (PDH); 20–25% adrenal (ADH)Pars distalis (somatotropic cell adenoma)
SignalmentCats (less common than dogs)Older male cats
Secondary DM?Yes — high incidence, poorly controlledYes — nearly universal, extreme insulin resistance
ALP elevation?No — cats lack steroid ALP isoenzymeNo
Characteristic skin findingFragile, easily bruised skin — degloving riskLarge cat, broad nose, prognathia inferior
GH-specific lab findingsNoneHyperphosphatemia (no azotemia), erythrocytosis, hyperglobulinemia
Preferred diagnostic testACTH stimulation, LDDSTSerum IGF-1 (>100 nmol/L = positive)
Trigger to screenDiabetic cat, poorly controlled, fragile skinDiabetic cat, extreme insulin resistance, weight gain despite poor control
🔑 The clinical clue both share: A diabetic cat that cannot be regulated on appropriate insulin doses. Feline HAC → fragile skin → degloving injuries. Acromegaly → large cat gaining weight despite uncontrolled DM, broad nose, prognathia. When you can't control a diabetic cat, always ask: is there an underlying driver of insulin resistance?
🟢 Insulinoma: Functional islet cell tumor secreting autonomous, unregulated insulin → episodic hypoglycemia. Common in ferrets, uncommon in dogs, rare in cats. Key: insulin level must be elevated or inappropriately high DURING documented hypoglycemia to be diagnostic.

Insulinoma — Key Facts

Species prevalence

SpeciesPrevalence
FerretsVery common — most common endocrine tumor
DogsUncommon but will encounter in practice
CatsRare

Tumor characteristics

  • Very small primary tumor (1–3 cm) — often missed on imaging
  • Secretes wide variety of hormones; insulin effects usually predominate
  • Frequent metastasis to regional lymph nodes and liver — mets can be larger than the primary!
  • Malignant behavior common despite bland histology
🔑 Autonomous secretion: The tumor secretes insulin regardless of blood glucose level. Normal beta cells stop secreting when glucose drops below ~60–70 mg/dL. The insulinoma does not — it continues secreting even as the patient becomes severely hypoglycemic. This lack of feedback inhibition is the pathophysiologic hallmark.

Signalment & Clinical Signs

Signalment

SpeciesWho gets it?
DogsMiddle-aged to older; large breeds predisposed (Labs, Golden Retrievers, Standard Poodles, German Shepherds)
FerretsMiddle-aged to older (4–6 years); very common

Mechanism of clinical signs

  • Two components: direct hypoglycemia effects (CNS glucose deprivation) + compensatory catecholamine release
  • Signs are episodic — may only last minutes! The tumor secretes in bursts, so glucose fluctuates

Clinical Signs — Episodic Hypoglycemia

SignMechanism
SeizuresSevere CNS glucose deprivation → neuronal dysfunction. Often post-exercise or fasting (glycogen depleted)
Weakness / collapseNeuroglycopenia + catecholamine-induced muscle fatigue
Muscle fasciculationsCatecholamine-driven neuromuscular excitability
Bizarre behavior / disorientationGlucose deprivation of higher cortical centers
Weight gain (sometimes)Persistent insulin → anabolic state → increased fat deposition; similar to an over-regulated diabetic
Peripheral neuropathy (sometimes)Mechanism uncertain; may be paraneoplastic
⚠️ Episodic = diagnostic challenge. The patient may be completely normal between episodes. A single glucose measurement during a normal period can be within reference interval. Don't rule out insulinoma from one normal glucose.

Diagnostic Approach — Minimum Database

TestExpected findingImportant caveat
Blood glucoseHypoglycemia 30–50 mg/dL — very suggestiveCan be EPISODIC — may be normal at a single time point. Serial measurements may be needed.
PE findingsOften unremarkable between episodesSometimes weight gain or peripheral neuropathy detectable

Advanced Diagnostic Testing

TestWhat it showsCritical requirement
Immunoreactive insulin level Elevated or inappropriately normal insulin during documented hypoglycemia MUST be measured SIMULTANEOUSLY with hypoglycemia — insulin measured when glucose is normal is uninterpretable. At glucose of 30–50 mg/dL, any detectable insulin is "inappropriately high."
Imaging (abdominal US, CT) May visualize pancreatic mass or hepatic metastases May be completely NEGATIVE if primary tumor is small. Negative imaging does not rule out insulinoma.
🔑 The insulin interpretation rule: Insulin must always be interpreted in the context of concurrent blood glucose. At glucose of 30–50 mg/dL, a normal beta cell should secrete ZERO insulin. Therefore, ANY measurable insulin level during severe hypoglycemia is inappropriately elevated = diagnostic of autonomous insulin secretion = insulinoma until proven otherwise.

Whipple's Triad — Classic Insulinoma Diagnosis

1

Clinical signs of hypoglycemia (seizure, collapse, weakness)

2

Documented low blood glucose DURING clinical signs

3

Resolution of signs with glucose supplementation

⚡ 6 clinical vignettes — covering DM, DKA electrolytes, feline HAC, acromegaly, and insulinoma. Click each card to flip.
VIGNETTE · DM LABWORK · SODIUM
An 8-year-old female Samoyed with known DM presents for a recheck. Glucose = 380 mg/dL. Sodium = 142 mEq/L (RI 140–155). The owner reports she's been drinking and urinating normally. Why is a normal sodium concerning in this patient?
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
Normal Na with marked chronic hyperglycemia = clinically significant dehydration. Glucose is an active osmol — it pulls water from interstitial and intracellular spaces into the vascular compartment. This dilutional effect normally lowers sodium. At glucose of 380 mg/dL, we EXPECT hyponatremia (dilutional). A "normal" sodium means the dilutional effect is being counteracted by significant dehydration (hemoconcentration raising Na back toward normal). This dog is more dehydrated than clinical signs suggest. Assess hydration carefully and consider fluid therapy.
VIGNETTE · DKA · ELECTROLYTES
A 6-year-old diabetic cat presents in DKA. Serum K = 4.8 mEq/L (RI 3.5–5.5). You begin IV insulin therapy. What will happen to serum K and why, and what must you do before or immediately with insulin?
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
Serum K will drop rapidly — supplement K before or with insulin. Even though serum K is currently normal (within RI), total body K stores are DEPLETED. K has been shifted from intracellular to extracellular space by acidosis and hyperosmolality — masking the deficit. When insulin is given, it drives K back into cells → serum K can drop precipitously to dangerous hypokalemic levels. ~50% of DKA patients have low serum K at presentation; the other 50% are also low total body. ALWAYS add KCl to IV fluids before insulin administration. Monitor K every 4–6 hours during treatment.
VIGNETTE · DKA · PHOSPHORUS
During DKA treatment, serum phosphorus drops from 1.8 to 0.8 mg/dL 8 hours after starting insulin. The patient develops pale mucous membranes and the plasma looks pink. What happened and what should you have been monitoring?
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
Intravascular hemolysis from severe hypophosphatemia. Phosphorus dropped below the critical threshold of 1 mg/dL. Phosphorus is essential for RBC membrane integrity (required for 2,3-DPG synthesis and ATP production). Severe hypophosphatemia → RBC membrane fragility → intravascular hemolysis → pink plasma (hemoglobinemia) + pale mucous membranes (anemia). This is preventable by monitoring P every 4–6 hours during insulin therapy and supplementing KPO₄ in IV fluids if P approaches the critical threshold. Insulin drives P into cells (same mechanism as K), so total body P depletion becomes severe rapidly.
VIGNETTE · FELINE HAC
A 12-year-old neutered male DSH has been diabetic for 8 months and despite escalating insulin doses is uncontrolled (fasting glucose consistently 400–500 mg/dL). The skin on his neck tears during examination. ALP is mildly elevated, ALT is mildly elevated. What disease should you screen for, and why is ALP not massively elevated like in dogs?
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
Feline hyperadrenocorticism (HAC). Poorly controlled DM despite appropriate insulin + fragile/easily torn skin = screen for feline HAC. The secondary diabetes is driven by cortisol-induced insulin resistance — insulin therapy alone cannot overcome this unless the cortisol excess is addressed. ALP is only mildly elevated (not markedly) because cats lack the steroid isoenzyme of ALP. In dogs, high cortisol induces production of a steroid-specific ALP isoenzyme causing 10–100× increases. In cats, this isoenzyme does not exist — so feline HAC does NOT cause dramatic ALP elevation, and you cannot use ALP as a screening tool the way you would in dogs. Test: ACTH stimulation or LDDST (adapted feline protocols).
VIGNETTE · FELINE ACROMEGALY
An 11-year-old male cat has been diabetic for 2 years. Initially required 2 units insulin twice daily; now requiring 12 units twice daily. He has gained weight despite poorly controlled DM. His nose bridge looks wider than in old photos. Serum IGF-1 = 115 nmol/L (RI 5–70). Phosphorus = 5.4 mg/dL with BUN/Cr normal. Interpret.
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
Feline acromegaly. IGF-1 >100 nmol/L = positive (70–100 = grey zone, <70 = negative). Weight gain DESPITE uncontrolled DM + extreme insulin resistance + facial changes (widened nose) = classic acromegaly. The hyperphosphatemia without azotemia is a GH-specific finding — growth hormone promotes phosphate retention independent of kidney function. Normal BUN/Cr rules out renal hyperphosphatemia. Additional GH-specific findings may include mild erythrocytosis and hyperglobulinemia. Next step: pituitary MRI or CT to visualize the somatotropic adenoma. Treatment options include radiation therapy or surgical hypophysectomy at specialized centers.
VIGNETTE · INSULINOMA · FERRET
A 5-year-old ferret presents for episodic staring, drooling, and hind limb weakness lasting 2–3 minutes. PE is unremarkable between episodes. Blood glucose during an episode = 35 mg/dL. Immunoreactive insulin = 18 µIU/mL (RI 5–20). The owner asks "is the insulin level high enough to diagnose insulinoma?" How do you explain the result?
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
Yes — the insulin is diagnostically elevated (inappropriately high). The key concept: insulin must ALWAYS be interpreted relative to concurrent glucose. At a glucose of 35 mg/dL, the normal pancreatic response is to secrete essentially ZERO insulin — hypoglycemia should completely suppress beta cell output. Any measurable insulin at this level = autonomously secreted, feedback-independent insulin = insulinoma. The insulin level of 18 µIU/mL is within the numerical reference interval, but the reference interval assumes NORMAL glucose. At glucose of 35, even 5 µIU/mL would be "inappropriately high." Whipple's triad is satisfied: episodic hypoglycemia signs + documented low glucose + appropriate diagnostic result. Insulinoma is extremely common in ferrets this age.
🟠 Jazzi: 11yo FS Shih Tzu. Increased liver enzymes, PU/PD, alopecia, skin lesions, accidents in the house. PE: HR 120, panting, BCS 6/9, calcinosis cutis on left ear, mild muscle atrophy right hind limb, severe tail-tip alopecia.

CBC

TestResultRIUnits
WBC20.52 H4.39–11.61×10³/µL
RBC7.455.7–8.01×10⁶/µL
HGB16.313.8–20.3g/dL
HCT47.939.2–55.9%
MCV64.361.8–75.1fL
MCH21.920.2–25.3pg
MCHC34.030.8–35.4g/dL
RDW13.011.3–13.5%
Platelets626 H190–468×10³/µL
MPV11.47.9–13.8fL
PCV4539–58%
Plasma Protein6.76.1–7.5g/dL
Neutrophils18.719 H2.841–9.112×10³/µL
Lymphocytes0.450 L0.594–3.305×10³/µL
Monocytes1.126 H0.075–0.85×10³/µL
Eosinophils0.2250.03–1.264×10³/µL

Chemistry Panel

TestResultRIUnits
Glucose137 H70–131mg/dL
BUN166–26mg/dL
Creatinine0.4 L0.7–1.5mg/dL
Phosphorus4.92.5–5.6mg/dL
Calcium10.09.3–11.5mg/dL
Total Protein5.25.2–7.3g/dL
Albumin3.23.0–3.9g/dL
Globulins2.41.7–3.8g/dL
Cholesterol363 H124–344mg/dL
Bilirubin0.20–0.2mg/dL
ALP1047 H16–140IU/L
ALT323 H12–54IU/L
GGT94 H0–6IU/L
CK23343–234IU/L
Sodium147140–156mmol/L
Potassium4.64.0–5.3mmol/L
Chloride110108–122mmol/L
HCO₃2418–25.8mmol/L
Anion Gap17.411.2–19.9
Amylase557236–1337IU/L
Lipase7112–147IU/L

Urinalysis — Cystocentesis

TestResult
ColorLight Yellow
TurbiditySlightly Cloudy
USG1.010 — markedly dilute
pH7
Protein1+
GlucoseNEGATIVE
KetonesNEGATIVE
BilirubinNEGATIVE
BloodTrace
RBCs0–5 /HPF
Epithelial CellsRare /HPF
Fat DropletsMODERATE
Urine Creatinine14.3 mg/dL
Urine Protein31.46 mg/dL
UPCR31.46 ÷ 14.3 = 2.2 (RI <0.5)
Step 1 — What does the CBC tell you?Click to reveal ▾▾

Classic glucocorticoid stress leukogram:

  • Mature neutrophilia 18.7 — cortisol causes demargination and delayed egress
  • Lymphopenia 0.45 — cortisol drives lymphocyte redistribution
  • Monocytosis 1.126 — classic steroid effect
  • Eosinophils LOW NORMAL — cortisol causes eosinopenia; trending low
  • Thrombocytosis 626 — occasionally seen with steroid excess
🔑 Mature neutrophilia + lymphopenia + monocytosis in a dog = steroid (stress) leukogram → points to HAC.
Step 2 — What does the chemistry panel tell you?Click to reveal ▾▾
FindingMechanism in HAC
ALP 1047 (↑↑↑)Steroid-specific ALP isoenzyme induced by cortisol + intrahepatic cholestasis. Cardinal HAC chemistry finding. Does NOT indicate liver failure.
ALT 323 + GGT 94Vacuolar hepatopathy (glycogen accumulation) + biliary component from cortisol effect.
Cholesterol 363Cortisol promotes lipolysis → hyperlipidemia. Common in HAC (~90%).
Glucose 137 (mild ↑)Cortisol → insulin resistance + gluconeogenesis. Did NOT reach renal threshold (no glucosuria).
Creatinine 0.4 (↓)Muscle wasting from cortisol catabolism → less creatinine produced. Clue for muscle atrophy.
Diagnosis: Canine HAC (Cushing's Syndrome). Calcinosis cutis on ear = nearly pathognomonic. Cortisol → abnormal calcium-phosphate metabolism → calcium salts precipitate in dermis.
Step 3 — What does the UA tell you?Click to reveal ▾▾
FindingInterpretation
USG 1.010Cortisol inhibits ADH → diuresis. 85% of HAC dogs have USG <1.015–1.020. Essentially isosthenuric.
Glucose NEGATIVEConfirms blood glucose (137) has NOT exceeded renal threshold (~180 mg/dL).
UPCR 2.2Markedly elevated (RI <0.5). HAC → systemic hypertension → glomerular hypertension → protein loss. Should NOT result in hypoalbuminemia.

Next Steps

TestPurposeExpected result
UCCR (home sample!)Sensitive screening — rule out if negativeLikely elevated (>15–20)
LDDST or ACTH stim testConfirm HAC and distinguish typeLDDST: fails to suppress at 8hr. ACTH stim: >22 µg/dL
Abdominal ultrasoundPDH (bilateral adrenomegaly) vs ADH (unilateral mass)PDH most likely (85%)
Urine culture40–50% of HAC dogs have occult UTIMay be positive even with negative sediment
Blood pressureHypertension → contributes to UPCR elevationLikely elevated
🔴 Sidney: 10yo F Min Pin. Known DM + HAC (insulin BID + lysodren SID). Did NOT receive insulin last night or this morning. Owner applied syrup to gums for suspected hypoglycemia. PE: depressed, 5% dehydrated, T=98.3°F, HR=80, intermittent bradycardia with sinus pauses, weak and slightly ataxic.

Quick Labs (PCV/TP)

TestResultRIUnits
PCV4139–58%
Plasma Protein7.9 H6.1–7.5g/dL

UA — Cystocentesis

TestResult
ColorLight Yellow
USG1.024
pH5
ProteinNEGATIVE
Glucose4+
KetonesNEGATIVE
BloodNEGATIVE
BilirubinNEGATIVE
RBCs0–5 /HPF
Epithelial CellsRare /HPF
Fat DropletsFew

Chemistry Panel

TestResultRIUnits
Glucose607 H70–131mg/dL
BUN51 H6–26mg/dL
Creatinine1.8 H0.7–1.5mg/dL
Phosphorus5.7 H2.5–5.6mg/dL
Calcium12.2 H9.3–11.5mg/dL
Magnesium2.9 H1.8–2.5mg/dL
Total Protein7.5 H5.2–7.3g/dL
Albumin4.1 H3.0–3.9g/dL
Globulins3.51.7–3.8g/dL
A:G Ratio1.050.9–1.8
Cholesterol260124–344mg/dL
Bilirubin0.10–0.2mg/dL
ALP208 H16–140IU/L
ALT58 H12–54IU/L
GGT20–6IU/L
CK20343–234IU/L
Sodium127 L140–156mmol/L
Potassium9.3 H4.0–5.3mmol/L
Chloride103 L108–122mmol/L
HCO₃15 L18–25.8mmol/L
Anion Gap18.311.2–19.9
Na:K Ratio13.6 L27.7–35.9
Amylase514236–1337IU/L
Lipase13212–147IU/L
Step 1 — The critical electrolyte patternClick to reveal ▾▾
Na 127 (↓↓) + K 9.3 (↑↑) + Na:K = 13.6 — life-threatening electrolyte crisis.
FindingMechanism
Hyponatremia (Na 127)Lysodren over-suppressed adrenal cortex → aldosterone deficiency → Na wasting + glucose osmol → dilutional hyponatremia.
Hyperkalemia (K 9.3)Aldosterone deficiency → K not excreted. K 9.3 is critically high.
Bradycardia + sinus pausesHyperkalemia depolarizes cardiac conduction → slowed conduction → bradycardia, heart block, arrhythmias.
🔑 Lysodren over-suppression → iatrogenic Addisonian crisis + severely uncontrolled DM from missed insulin. NOT hypoglycemia — glucose = 607.
Step 2 — The glucose picture and why this is NOT hypoglycemiaClick to reveal ▾▾

Glucose 607 = profoundly HYPERGLYCEMIC. Owner thought this was a hypoglycemic episode — it was NOT. The ataxia and weakness are from electrolyte crisis (K 9.3), not low glucose. 4+ glucosuria confirms renal threshold exceeded. Ketones NEGATIVE = not DKA yet, but could progress.

Emergency Priorities

PriorityActionRationale
1EKG + continuous cardiac monitoringK 9.3 → life-threatening arrhythmias already present
2IV 0.9% NaCl (NOT LRS — has K)Volume + dilutes hyperkalemia + provides Na
3Dexamethasone IVCortisol deficiency from lysodren. Use dexamethasone (won't interfere with ACTH stim)
4ACTH stimulation testPost-stim cortisol <2 µg/dL = confirms adrenal insufficiency
5Resume insulin carefullyInsulin drives K intracellularly — monitor K during treatment
🟡 Yogi: 13yo MC Chow Chow. 2 days vomiting and lethargy, eating less, drinking more. Owner found large clear fluid near bed (likely urine!). T=101.1, HR=114, panting, mild non-painful hepatomegaly. Slightly weak.

CBC

TestResultRIUnits
WBC28.19 H4.88–12.74×10³/µL
RBC6.205.7–8.01×10⁶/µL
HGB14.113.8–20.3g/dL
HCT42.039.2–55.9%
MCV67.764.0–75.2fL
MCH22.922.7–26.8pg
MCHC37 H34.5–36.6g/dL
RDW12.711.3–13.5%
Platelets493 H190–468×10³/µL
MPV14.7 H7.9–13.8fL
PCV4033–58%
Plasma Protein8.7 H6.1–7.5g/dL
Neutrophils22.552 H2.529–12.876×10³/µL
Bands0.846 H0×10³/µL
Lymphocytes0.282 L0.48–2.941×10³/µL
Monocytes5.074 H0.212–1.049×10³/µL
Eosinophils0.2820–1.338×10³/µL
Comments: Slight lipemia

UA — Free Catch

TestResult
ColorLight Yellow
USG1.019
pH5

Glucose4+
Ketones2+
BloodNEGATIVE
WBCs0–5 /HPF
RBCs0–5 /HPF
Bacteria1+ (free catch — rule out contamination)

Chemistry Panel

TestResultRIUnits
Glucose522 H73–116mg/dL
BUN87 H8–27mg/dL
Creatinine1.7 H0.5–1.6mg/dL
Phosphorus3.52.0–6.7mg/dL
Calcium9.79.2–11.6mg/dL
Magnesium2.9 H1.7–2.4mg/dL
Total Protein6.15.5–7.2g/dL
Albumin2.92.8–4.0g/dL
Globulins3.22.0–4.1g/dL
A:G Ratio0.910.6–2.0
Cholesterol334 H138–317mg/dL
Bilirubin0.10–0.2mg/dL
ALP722 H14–120IU/L
ALT441 H16–73IU/L
GGT9 H3–8IU/L
CK24848–380IU/L
Sodium138 L147–154mmol/L
Potassium4.23.9–5.2mmol/L
Chloride97 L104–117mmol/L
HCO₃11 L16–24mmol/L
Anion Gap37.2 H16.3–28.6
Na:K Ratio30.227.4–38.4 (NORMAL)
Lipase1978 H22–216IU/L
Step 1 — Is this DKA?Click to reveal ▾▾
YES — DKA: Hyperglycemia (522) + 4+ glucosuria + 2+ ketonuria + high AG metabolic acidosis (HCO₃ 11, AG 37.2). Na:K ratio = 30.2 (NORMAL) → NOT Addison's. Hyponatremia is dilutional from glucose osmol.
Step 2 — Concurrent trigger? What does the CBC tell you?Click to reveal ▾▾

The CBC shows an inflammatory (NOT stress) leukogram — neutrophilia with bands (left shift). This indicates active infection or severe inflammation as a DKA trigger. Lipase 1978 (~9× URL) strongly suggests concurrent pancreatitis as the precipitating cause. Always look for the trigger.

DKA Management Priorities

PriorityActionRationale
1IV 0.9% NaClCorrect dehydration, restore perfusion, dilute glucose
2Potassium supplementation BEFORE insulinK 4.2 now but total body K depleted. Insulin will crash serum K.
3Low-dose regular insulin CRISlowly reduce glucose; do not drop faster than 50 mg/dL/hr
4Monitor K and P every 4–6 hoursP <1 mg/dL = hemolysis risk → supplement KPO₄
5Pancreatitis workup: fPLI or cPLI + USLipase 1978 → pancreatitis likely trigger; must address or DKA will relapse
6Urine culture (cystocentesis)Bacteria on free-catch — may be contamination or true UTI
🟢 Sally: 11yo FS West Highland White Terrier. Several months of episodic weakness and one observed seizure-like episode. Weakness is associated with activity (walking). PE: bilateral luxating patellas; otherwise unremarkable between episodes.

Chemistry Panel

TestResultRIUnits
Glucose44 L73–116mg/dL
BUN138–27mg/dL
Creatinine0.50.5–1.6mg/dL
Phosphorus3.52.0–6.7mg/dL
Calcium11.19.2–11.6mg/dL
Total Protein6.85.5–7.2g/dL
Albumin4.02.8–4.0g/dL
Globulin2.82.0–4.1g/dL
Alb:Glob Ratio1.430.60–2.00
Cholesterol279138–317mg/dL
Bilirubin0.10.0–0.2mg/dL
ALP272 H14–120IU/L
ALT3516–73IU/L
GGT<33.0–8.0IU/L
CK18448–380IU/L
Sodium148147–154mmol/L
Potassium4.23.9–5.2mmol/L
Chloride110104–117mmol/L
Bicarbonate2216–24mmol/L
Anion Gap19.516.3–28.6
Amylase468347–1104IU/L
Lipase4522–216IU/L
Step 1 — The single most important findingClick to reveal ▾▾
Glucose = 44 mg/dL — significantly HYPOGLYCEMIC. Almost everything else is unremarkable. Na:K ratio ~35 = normal (not Addison's). ALT normal, GGT low (not liver disease). Cholesterol normal (not hypothyroid). Hypoglycemia in an older dog with episodic exercise-induced weakness and one seizure-like episode = insulinoma until proven otherwise.
Step 2 — What does the ALP 272 mean?Click to reveal ▾▾

ALP is mildly elevated (~2× URL). ALT is normal and GGT is actually LOW — this is NOT the pattern of HAC (where GGT would also be elevated alongside ALP). The mild ALP is a red herring — likely bone ALP isoenzyme (common in older dogs) or incidental. It does NOT change the diagnosis.

Step 3 — Whipple's Triad and why exercise makes it worseClick to reveal ▾▾

Insulinoma secretes insulin autonomously even when glucose is low. Exercise depletes glycogen → glucagon rises → normally maintains glucose. With insulinoma, insulin suppresses gluconeogenesis and glycogen release → glucose falls further during activity. Whipple's Triad: (1) episodic hypoglycemia signs, (2) documented low glucose (44 mg/dL), (3) resolution with glucose supplementation.

Diagnostic Workup

TestPurposeKey note
Immunoreactive insulinConfirm autonomous insulin secretionMUST measure simultaneously with documented hypoglycemia. At glucose 44, ANY detectable insulin = inappropriately elevated.
Abdominal ultrasoundVisualize pancreatic mass or hepatic metsMay be negative — primary tumors are often 1–3 cm.
CT abdomenMore sensitive for small pancreatic lesionsImportant for surgical planning.
🔑 Surgical resection is the treatment of choice. The bilateral luxating patellas are an incidental orthopedic finding — they do not explain the episodic, exercise-associated hypoglycemic pattern.
🔬 Thyroid & Adrenal Panels Worksheet — Cases from the lab worksheet. Each case includes the raw data, the worksheet questions, and spoiler-revealed answers. Note: endocrine results may be in conventional or international units. Always use the RI provided by the lab for interpretation.
🔵 Bailey: 10yo F/S Shetland Sheepdog. Re-evaluation for atopic dermatitis and flea allergy dermatitis. Diffuse thinning of haircoat and rough/poor quality on trunk and tail. Alopecia, erythema, lichenification and hyperpigmentation of left axillary region. Lichenification of all four paws (forepaws > hindpaws). Alopecia of ventral abdomen.

In-House Thyroid Panel

TestResultRI
T4<0.500 L1.2–5.2 µg/dL
TSH1.12 H0–0.50 ng/mL
Worksheet Questions & Answers
a. Would you consider hypothyroidism based on S/H/PE findings?Click to reveal ▾▾

Yes, hypothyroidism should be considered, but the clinical picture is complicated. The diffuse thinning of haircoat, alopecia, poor-quality coat, lichenification, and hyperpigmentation are consistent with hypothyroidism. However, the same signs also occur with allergic/parasitic skin disease — which Bailey already has (atopic dermatitis, flea allergy). The concurrent inflammatory skin disease could be causing or contributing to the skin changes. The key is that the symmetrical trunk alopecia, rough coat quality, and lichenification pattern warrant thyroid testing to determine if hypothyroidism is an additional complicating factor.

b. Interpret the thyroid panelClick to reveal ▾▾
ResultInterpretation
T4 <0.500 (L)Markedly low — below the lower end of RI. Consistent with hypothyroidism, but could also be suppressed by non-thyroidal illness (euthyroid sick syndrome from concurrent allergic skin disease) or by medications.
TSH 1.12 (H)Elevated — pituitary is sensing inadequate T4 and secreting more TSH to stimulate the thyroid. This supports true hypothyroidism rather than euthyroid sick syndrome (which typically has normal TSH).
