🔑 Pattern: Low TT4 + High TSH = Classic Hypothyroidism. The elevated TSH is the key differentiator from euthyroid sick syndrome (where TSH is typically normal). This pattern strongly supports primary hypothyroidism.
c. Do you need a full thyroid panel, or is this enough?Click to reveal ▾▾

This in-house panel (TT4 + TSH) provides a strong basis for diagnosis and may be sufficient. Low TT4 + high TSH = classic hypothyroidism pattern. However, a full panel (adding fT4-ED and thyroid autoantibodies) would provide additional information:

  • fT4-ED: More resistant to suppression by non-thyroidal illness (the concurrent skin disease); would help confirm if TT4 is being falsely lowered
  • Autoantibodies (TGAA, T4AA, T3AA): Positive TGAA supports lymphocytic thyroiditis as the underlying cause; T4AA can falsely elevate TT4 (less relevant when TT4 is already very low)

Given the strong pattern here (markedly low T4 + elevated TSH), a full panel is not strictly required — but fT4-ED adds confidence, especially with concurrent inflammatory disease that could theoretically suppress TT4.

🔵 Kayla: 12yo F/S Maltese. Presented for chronically elevated liver enzymes. History of iatrogenic hypoadrenocorticism. Thinning hair coat noted on PE.

In-House TT4

TestResultRI
TT40.54 L1.2–5.2 µg/dL

MSU Full Panel

TestResultRI
TT49 L11–60 nmol/L
fT4-ED126–42 pmol/L
TSH170–30 mU/L
T4AA120–20%
T3AA60–10%
TGAA1<20% = NEGATIVE
Worksheet Questions & Answers
a. What justification is there for doing thyroid testing?Click to reveal ▾▾

Three reasons: (1) Chronically elevated liver enzymes — hypothyroidism can cause mild vacuolar hepatopathy and mild liver enzyme increases; (2) Thinning hair coat — classic sign of hypothyroidism (anagen effluvium from inadequate thyroid hormone support of hair follicles); (3) History of iatrogenic hypoadrenocorticism — glucocorticoid withdrawal suppresses thyroid function through multiple mechanisms, and concurrent endocrine disease warrants thyroid evaluation.

b. For the in-house TT4 of 0.54 (L): list the 3 differentialsClick to reveal ▾▾
  1. Hypothyroidism — true primary thyroid failure with inadequate T4 production
  2. Euthyroid sick syndrome — non-thyroidal illness (the chronic liver enzyme elevation, history of iatrogenic hypoadrenocorticism) suppressing TT4 despite normal thyroid function
  3. Drug suppression — glucocorticoids (from iatrogenic Addison's treatment?), TMS, anticonvulsants can suppress TT4; also the history of hypoadrenocorticism treatment may include steroids
c. Based on TT4, fT4-ED, and TSH — which differential is most likely?Click to reveal ▾▾
TestResultInterpretation
TT49 L (RI 11–60 nmol/L)Low — consistent with hypothyroidism, euthyroid sick, or drug suppression
fT4-ED12 (RI 6–42 pmol/L)NORMAL — this is the key finding. fT4-ED is resistant to non-thyroidal illness suppression.
TSH17 (RI 0–30 mU/L)NORMAL — pituitary is not sensing thyroid hormone deficiency
🔑 Pattern: Low TT4 + Normal fT4-ED + Normal TSH = Euthyroid Sick Syndrome OR Drug Suppression. The normal fT4-ED means there is adequate free T4 circulating. The pituitary correctly senses this (normal TSH → not trying to stimulate more thyroid hormone). The low TT4 is from non-thyroidal illness or drug effect suppressing total (protein-bound) T4. All negative AAs confirm no lymphocytic thyroiditis. Do NOT diagnose hypothyroidism here. Treat the underlying disease and retest if signs persist.
🔵 Rusty: 18-month-old M Golden Retriever. Recurrent pyoderma, history of lethargy and weight gain. T=99.9, P=76 (bradycardic for a young dog), R=panting. Comedones in perianal region.

In-House Lipid Result

TestResultRI
Triglycerides (TRIG)318 H28–303 mg/dL

MSU Full Panel

TestResultRI
TT41711–60 nmol/L (within RI)
fT4-ED0 L6–42 pmol/L
TSH75 H0–30 mU/L
T4AA31 H0–20%
T3AA57 H0–10%
THYROG AA362 H>35% = POSITIVE
Worksheet Questions & Answers
a. What evidence supports hypothyroidism from H/S/PE?Click to reveal ▾▾
  • Lethargy and weight gain (despite no change in appetite implied)
  • Recurrent pyoderma — immunosuppression from inadequate thyroid hormone; bacteria thrive in hypothyroid skin
  • Comedones in perianal region — follicular plugging from hypothyroid skin changes
  • HR=76 — relative bradycardia in an 18-month-old Golden Retriever (young large dogs should have HR ~80–120)
  • Panting — atypical for a young dog at rest; may reflect exercise intolerance
  • Hypertriglyceridemia (318 H) — thyroid hormones are essential for lipid metabolism; deficiency → lipid accumulation
b. Interpret TT4, fT4-ED, and TSHClick to reveal ▾▾
TestResultInterpretation
TT4 = 17Within RI (11–60)NORMAL numerically — but must be interpreted in context. T4AA (31%) is elevated and could be falsely normalizing the TT4 measurement (T4AAs are measured AS T4 in standard assays → artificially inflates the result).
fT4-ED = 0 (L)Below RI (6–42)CRITICALLY LOW — essentially undetectable. fT4-ED uses equilibrium dialysis which EXCLUDES T4AAs from the measurement → reveals the true free T4, which is severely deficient.
TSH = 75 (H)Above RI (0–30)MARKEDLY ELEVATED — pituitary is sensing severe thyroid hormone deficiency and maximally stimulating the thyroid. This is the highest-yield finding confirming true hypothyroidism.
🔑 Pattern: Normal TT4 (falsely elevated by T4AA) + Low fT4-ED + Very High TSH = Hypothyroidism with T4 Autoantibody Interference. The T4AA is being measured as T4 on the standard assay, making TT4 appear normal. The fT4-ED (dialysis method) eliminates the autoantibody interference and reveals the truth.
c. What do the high AAs mean?Click to reveal ▾▾

Positive autoantibodies indicate underlying lymphocytic thyroiditis (LT) — the immune-mediated cause of hypothyroidism in this dog. Specifically:

  • TGAA 362 (POSITIVE — >35%): Thyroglobulin autoantibodies have the strongest correlation with the actual lesion of lymphocytic thyroiditis. This is the primary antibody supporting LT.
  • T4AA 31 (elevated — 0–20%) and T3AA 57 (elevated — 0–10%): Less specific for LT but also elevated; critically, T4AA is causing the falsely normal TT4.

AAs are NEVER used to diagnose hypothyroidism directly — they indicate lymphocytic thyroiditis (the process) but not functional failure. However, positive AAs with concurrent low fT4-ED and high TSH confirm both the mechanism (LT) and the consequence (hypothyroidism).

🐱 Simba: 18yo F/S DSH cat. Yearly routine exam. History of idiopathic seizures well controlled with gabapentin. Owners report no change in behavior, no vomiting/diarrhea. PE: Wt 2.77 kg (very thin), BCS 3/9, QAR, HR 240 bpm, MM pink, CRT <2s. Moderate-marked muscle wasting along spine, pelvis, shoulders. Kidneys palpate smooth but small.

Chemistry Panel

TestResultRI
BUN44 H15–37 mg/dL
CREA1.70.7–1.9 mg/dL (upper end)
PHOS6.2 H2.4–5.7 mg/dL
BILIT0.10–0.2 mg/dL
ALP5711–58 IU/L (just within)
ALT161 H27–95 IU/L
GGT<30–3 IU/L

UA & In-House TT4

TestResultRI
USG1.017
TT46.77 H0.78–4.27 µg/dL
Worksheet Questions & Answers
a. What important information did you get from S/H/PE?Click to reveal ▾▾
  • 18 years old — classic signalment for feline hyperthyroidism (only ≤5% of hyperthyroid cats are <10 years)
  • Wt 2.77 kg, BCS 3/9 — significantly underweight; weight loss despite (presumably) adequate appetite = hypermetabolism
  • Moderate-marked muscle wasting — catabolic hypermetabolic state; also consistent with chronic renal disease
  • HR 240 bpm — tachycardia from thyroid hormone-driven cardiac stimulation (hyperthyroid cats commonly develop thyroid-induced HCM)
  • Kidneys palpate smooth but small — small kidneys suggest CKD; this is critical because hyperthyroidism masks CKD by increasing GFR
  • Gabapentin for seizures: some medications can affect thyroid values — gabapentin has minimal thyroid effect but worth noting
b. Interpret the renal parametersClick to reveal ▾▾
FindingInterpretation
BUN 44 H (RI 15–37)Elevated — azotemia present. Could be pre-renal (dehydration from hyperthyroidism) or intrinsic renal (CKD)
CREA 1.7 (RI 0.7–1.9)At the upper end of RI — numerically within normal but creatinine may be falsely lowered by hyperthyroidism (increased GFR dilutes creatinine). This value may be higher than it appears once hyperthyroidism is treated.
PHOS 6.2 H (RI 2.4–5.7)Elevated — in a cat with azotemia, hyperphosphatemia supports CKD (reduced renal phosphate excretion)
USG 1.017Inadequate concentration for an azotemic cat (should be >1.040 if kidneys are normal). This confirms intrinsic renal concentrating defect = CKD
🔑 This cat has CKD masked by hyperthyroidism. Hyperthyroidism increases GFR → dilutes creatinine into near-normal range. After treatment, GFR will normalize and creatinine will rise — potentially revealing IRIS Stage 2 or 3 CKD. The small palpable kidneys support this.
c. What endocrine disease should you always consider with mildly elevated ALT/AST in a cat?Click to reveal ▾▾

Hyperthyroidism — in cats, the most common endocrine cause of mildly to moderately elevated ALT (and occasionally AST). Thyroid hormone excess causes hepatocyte swelling, vacuolation, and possibly mild ischemic hepatic changes from the hyperdynamic circulation. ALT elevation is typically mild to moderate (<3–5× URL) and resolves with successful treatment of hyperthyroidism. ALT 161 H (RI 27–95) is approximately 1.7× URL — consistent with hyperthyroid hepatopathy.

d. Interpret the TT4Click to reveal ▾▾

TT4 = 6.77 µg/dL (RI 0.78–4.27) → ELEVATED. Approximately 1.6× the upper limit of the reference interval. This is diagnostic for feline hyperthyroidism when combined with compatible clinical signs. In cats, TT4 is the primary screening test for hyperthyroidism (opposite of dogs, where TT4 screens for hypothyroidism). An elevated TT4 in a cat with appropriate signalment and clinical signs is sufficient for diagnosis — fT4 is not needed as a screening test and can actually be falsely elevated by non-thyroidal illness in cats (confounding factor).

e. What effects can hyperthyroidism have on renal function?Click to reveal ▾▾

Hyperthyroidism increases GFR through multiple mechanisms:

  • Increased cardiac output and renal perfusion pressure → increased GFR
  • Thyroid hormones directly promote renal hypertrophy and increased nephron function

Consequences:

  • Creatinine is diluted by increased GFR → appears lower than true value (masks CKD)
  • After treating hyperthyroidism, GFR normalizes → creatinine rises to its true value → CKD may unmask
  • This is why pre-treatment renal assessment is critical in hyperthyroid cats
  • Some cats decompensate to overt CKD after treatment — this is NOT caused by treatment; it was pre-existing, simply masked
  • For this reason, treatment should be gradual (methimazole trial before permanent treatment) to allow renal reassessment
🟠 Katy: 13yo FS Terrier mix. Chronic skin infections × 3 years. Generalized erythema and pustules along entire trunk. Alopecia along ventral cervical region, tail, and caudal thighs. CBC and chemistry panel findings show minimal changes. USG = 1.025 with 1+ proteinuria.

ACTH Stimulation Test

TestResultRI
COR0 (baseline)7.81–4.5 µg/dL (elevated baseline)
COR1 (post-ACTH)12.17–20 µg/dL

LDDST

TestResultRI
COR0 (baseline)1.21–4.5 µg/dL (normal)
COR4 (4-hour)<1.00 L<1.4 µg/dL (suppressed ✓)
COR8 (8-hour)<1.00 L<1.4 µg/dL (suppressed ✓)
Worksheet Questions & Answers
a. What evidence from H/S/PE supports testing for HAC?Click to reveal ▾▾
  • Chronic, recurrent skin infections (3 years) — cortisol causes immunosuppression → recurrent pyoderma that is difficult to control
  • Generalized erythema and pustules — HAC predisposes to secondary skin infections
  • Alopecia in characteristic HAC pattern (ventral cervical, tail, caudal thighs) — bilateral symmetric alopecia sparing the head and distal extremities
  • Older dog (13yo) with chronic progressive skin disease — classic HAC age and presentation
  • Proteinuria on UA — HAC causes hypertension → glomerular protein loss
Note: The chemistry panel has "minimal changes." This means the typical ALP ↑↑ of HAC was not present — but this does NOT rule out HAC. ACTH stim sensitivity for PDH is only 60–70%, and this case has significant HAC-compatible clinical signs.
b. ACTH stim test: what value is interpreted, positive/negative/gray zone, and sensitivity?Click to reveal ▾▾
QuestionAnswer
Value interpretedPost-ACTH cortisol (COR1) = 12.1 µg/dL — this is the value used for interpretation
Positive, negative, or gray zone?NEGATIVE — COR1 = 12.1 is within the RI of 7–20 µg/dL. This is a normal stimulation response. Does NOT support HAC on this test alone.
Sensitivity for PDH60–70% — meaning 30–40% of PDH dogs will have a NEGATIVE ACTH stim test. This is a significant false-negative rate.
Sensitivity for ADH55–60% — slightly lower than PDH.
⚠️ A negative ACTH stim test does NOT rule out HAC, especially PDH. The LDDST must also be evaluated.
c. LDDST: first step and conclusionClick to reveal ▾▾
QuestionAnswer
First step in evaluating LDDSTEvaluate the 8-hour cortisol. COR8 = <1.00 µg/dL. The cutoff is <1.4 µg/dL. Since COR8 < 1.4 → NEGATIVE (normal suppression). Does NOT support HAC.
ConclusionLDDST NEGATIVE — the dog suppressed appropriately at both 4 and 8 hours. Normal animals suppress cortisol with dexamethasone. This argues against HAC.
🔑 Both tests are negative. This significantly reduces the likelihood of HAC. In a non-ill dog, a negative LDDST has good specificity — few false negatives. However, the strong clinical signs warrant monitoring. Consider UCCR (urine cortisol:creatinine ratio) from a home sample, abdominal ultrasound, or re-testing in 3–6 months if clinical signs persist. The baseline COR0 = 7.8 is above the RI (1–4.5) — which is NOT interpreted in isolation but does suggest this dog's adrenal axis is somewhat activated (stress, etc.).
🟠 Morgan: 10yo F/S Labrador Retriever mix. Recently diagnosed with diabetes, but has been difficult to get under control.

LDDST

TestResultRI
COR0 (baseline)3.11–4.5 µg/dL (normal)
COR4 (4-hour)<1.00 L<1.4 µg/dL (suppressed ✓)
COR8 (8-hour)3.2 H<1.4 µg/dL (NOT suppressed ✗)
Worksheet Questions & Answers
a. Why test for HAC in a diabetic patient with this history?Click to reveal ▾▾

Difficult-to-control diabetes is a major trigger to screen for concurrent HAC. Cortisol causes insulin resistance — it promotes gluconeogenesis, reduces peripheral glucose uptake, and antagonizes insulin at receptor level. A diabetic dog with concurrent HAC cannot be adequately controlled with insulin alone because cortisol continuously counteracts insulin action. HAC is one of the most common concurrent diseases causing "brittle" or poorly regulated diabetes in dogs. Treating the diabetes without addressing the cortisol excess will fail. Additionally, Lab Retrievers have a breed predisposition to both DM and HAC.

b. Step 1: Interpret the 8-hour resultClick to reveal ▾▾

COR8 = 3.2 µg/dL. The cutoff is <1.4 µg/dL for suppression. 3.2 > 1.4 → the cortisol did NOT suppress at 8 hours. This is a POSITIVE LDDST — supports HAC. In a dog without concurrent illness, this has good specificity (few false positives). Given the compatible clinical history (difficult-to-control DM), this positive result is clinically significant.

c. Three criteria for suppression — which are present in this case?Click to reveal ▾▾
Criteria for suppression (any one = PDH)Present in Morgan?
4-hour cortisol <1.4 µg/dLYES — COR4 <1.00 µg/dL
4-hour cortisol <50% of baselineYES — COR4 <1.00 vs baseline 3.1 = <32% of baseline
8-hour cortisol <50% of baselineNO — COR8 = 3.2 vs baseline 3.1 = ~103% of baseline
🔑 Two of three criteria for PDH suppression ARE met (at 4 hours). This pattern — suppression at 4 hours but escape at 8 hours — is characteristic of PDH. The pituitary tumor responds transiently to dexamethasone but "escapes" suppression by 8 hours.
d. What does suppression at 4 hours indicate?Click to reveal ▾▾

Suppression at 4 hours (with escape at 8 hours) indicates PDH (Pituitary-Dependent Hyperadrenocorticism). The pituitary ACTH-secreting tumor still has some sensitivity to glucocorticoid negative feedback — it suppresses transiently when dexamethasone is given — but it is not fully suppressible. ADH (adrenal-dependent HAC) shows NO suppression at either time point because cortisol secretion is autonomous from the adrenal tumor and ACTH is already suppressed. A complete lack of suppression at either time point means ADH or PDH (indeterminate by LDDST alone → need ultrasound or eACTH).

e. What test is used to monitor therapy for HAC?Click to reveal ▾▾

ACTH stimulation test — used to monitor treatment with trilostane or mitotane. The goal is to achieve adequate but not excessive adrenal suppression (post-ACTH cortisol typically 2–6 µg/dL during treatment). The LDDST is NOT used for monitoring — it is a diagnostic test only. The UCCR can be used at home to assess cortisol production trend over time but ACTH stim provides the definitive monitoring metric.

🟠 Makoa: 12yo M/C German Spitz. 1.5-year history of liver enzyme increases and recent onset of PU/PD and polyphagia.

LDDST

TestResultRI
COR0 (baseline)7.0 H1–4.5 µg/dL (ELEVATED baseline)
COR4 (4-hour)5.2 H<1.4 µg/dL (NOT suppressed ✗)
COR8 (8-hour)5.6 H<1.4 µg/dL (NOT suppressed ✗)
Worksheet Questions & Answers
a. Is there justification for testing for HAC?Click to reveal ▾▾

Yes, strong justification:

  • 1.5-year history of elevated liver enzymes — chronic ALP ↑↑ is the cardinal chemistry finding of HAC (steroid isoenzyme induction)
  • Recent onset of PU/PD — cortisol inhibits ADH → dilute urine + compensatory polydipsia
  • Polyphagia — cortisol stimulates appetite
  • Middle-aged to older dog (12yo) with insidious onset — classic HAC timeline
b. Step 1: Interpret the 8-hour resultClick to reveal ▾▾

COR8 = 5.6 µg/dL. Cutoff is <1.4 µg/dL. 5.6 > 1.4 → NOT suppressed. POSITIVE LDDST — supports HAC. The baseline COR0 = 7.0 is also elevated above the RI (1–4.5 µg/dL), which further supports autonomous cortisol overproduction.

c. Step 2: Is there evidence of suppression?Click to reveal ▾▾
Criteria for suppression (any one = PDH)Present?
4-hour cortisol <1.4 µg/dLNO — COR4 = 5.2
4-hour cortisol <50% of baseline (7.0)NO — 5.2 ÷ 7.0 = 74% of baseline
8-hour cortisol <50% of baselineNO — 5.6 ÷ 7.0 = 80% of baseline
No suppression at 4 hours OR 8 hours. None of the three criteria are met → cannot use the LDDST to discriminate PDH from ADH in this case.
d. Interpretation and what to do nextClick to reveal ▾▾

Interpretation: LDDST is positive (HAC confirmed). No suppression at either time point — the LDDST cannot discriminate PDH from ADH in this case. No suppression does NOT mean ADH — only 60% of PDH dogs suppress on LDDST, so 40% of PDH also show no suppression.

Next: Need discrimination testing. Per ACVIM consensus statement, use abdominal ultrasound and/or endogenous ACTH (eACTH).

e. What could you do next? (discrimination options)Click to reveal ▾▾
TestWhat it tells you
Abdominal ultrasoundPDH → bilateral adrenomegaly. ADH → unilateral adrenal mass + contralateral atrophy. Most accessible discrimination tool in practice.
Endogenous ACTH (eACTH)PDH → HIGH eACTH (pituitary tumor secreting ACTH autonomously). ADH → LOW/undetectable eACTH (autonomous cortisol from adrenal tumor suppresses pituitary). Sample handling critical — EDTA tube, spin and freeze immediately.
High-dose dexamethasone suppression test (HDDST)Less commonly used. Higher dexamethasone dose may suppress some PDH cases that didn't suppress on LDDST. Not discriminatory for ADH.
🟠 Annabelle: 13yo FS Beagle. Presented for PU/PD and worsening urinary incontinence. Bilaterally enlarged adrenals noted on ultrasound.

ACTH Stimulation Test

TestResultRI
COR03.21–4.5 µg/dL
COR1 (post-ACTH)19.57–20 µg/dL

LDDST

TestResultRI
COR04.6 H1–4.5 µg/dL
COR42.6 H<1.4 µg/dL (NOT suppressed)
COR82.9 H<1.4 µg/dL (NOT suppressed)
Worksheet Questions & Answers
a. Interpret the ACTH stim testClick to reveal ▾▾
FindingInterpretation
COR1 = 19.5 µg/dL (RI 7–20)Within RI — just at the upper end of normal. This is a GRAY ZONE / NEGATIVE result. Cutoffs: <17 = negative, 17–20 = gray zone, >22 = positive. 19.5 is technically in the gray zone.
⚠️ The ACTH stim test is NEGATIVE/gray zone for HAC. It should NOT be used alone to diagnose or rule out HAC in this case. The LDDST must be the primary test. Remember: ACTH stim sensitivity for PDH = 60–70%, so many HAC cases will be negative on this test.
b. Interpret the LDDSTClick to reveal ▾▾
StepFindingInterpretation
Step 1: 8-hour resultCOR8 = 2.9 (RI <1.4)POSITIVE — 2.9 > 1.4 → NOT suppressed → LDDST supports HAC
Step 2: Evidence of suppression for PDH?COR4 = 2.6; COR0 = 4.6COR4 ÷ COR0 = 2.6 ÷ 4.6 = 57% (not <50%) → NO suppression at 4 hrs. COR8 ÷ COR0 = 2.9 ÷ 4.6 = 63% (not <50%) → NO suppression at 8 hrs. None of the three criteria for PDH suppression are met.
LDDST is positive (HAC confirmed) but cannot discriminate PDH from ADH by suppression criteria alone.
c. Conclusion for this caseClick to reveal ▾▾
Conclusion: HAC is confirmed by LDDST (positive). The LDDST cannot discriminate PDH from ADH by suppression criteria.

However, the ultrasound already found bilateral enlarged adrenals → this supports PDH (bilateral adrenomegaly = both adrenals being driven by excess ACTH from pituitary tumor). The combination of LDDST positive + bilateral adrenomegaly on US = PDH as the most likely diagnosis.

The ACTH stim test was gray zone/negative — this is expected since ACTH stim sensitivity for PDH is only 60–70%. The LDDST is more sensitive for both PDH and ADH.

Next step: Consider eACTH to confirm PDH (expected: HIGH eACTH). Treat with trilostane or mitotane; monitor with ACTH stim tests.

🟠 Sadie: 11yo F/S Shih Tzu. Referred for thinning hair on head and trunk, skin lesions, and increased frequency of accidents in the house. PE: bilateral thinning of hair over frontal and temporal regions, pinna, and trunk. Localized pyoderma near base of tail. Currently on heartworm preventative, topical flea preventative, and topical triamcinolone as needed for allergic dermatitis.

ACTH Stimulation Test

TestResultRI
COR0 (baseline)1.61–4.5 µg/dL
COR1 (post-ACTH)2.6 L7–20 µg/dL
Worksheet Questions & Answers
a. What important information did you get from the Hx/S/PE?Click to reveal ▾▾
  • Topical triamcinolone as needed — this is the most critical piece of information. Triamcinolone is a potent synthetic glucocorticoid. Even topical glucocorticoids can cause systemic absorption sufficient to cause iatrogenic HAC and suppression of the HPA axis.
  • Hair thinning on head, temporal regions, pinna, and trunk — these are classic HAC-like skin changes, but in this context, they may be from iatrogenic glucocorticoid excess rather than spontaneous HAC
  • Pyoderma near base of tail — consistent with immunosuppression from excess glucocorticoids (either iatrogenic or spontaneous HAC)
  • Accidents in the house = PU/PD → dilute urine from glucocorticoid inhibition of ADH
  • No classic "pot belly" or dramatic ALP elevation mentioned → suggests iatrogenic rather than long-standing spontaneous HAC
b. Interpret the ACTH stim testClick to reveal ▾▾
FindingInterpretation
COR0 = 1.6 (RI 1–4.5)Low-normal baseline — already suggests suppressed adrenal function
COR1 = 2.6 L (RI 7–20)MARKEDLY LOW POST-ACTH CORTISOL — well below the lower end of the RI. This is an INADEQUATE STIMULATION RESPONSE.
The ACTH stim pattern of low baseline + flat (inadequate) post-ACTH response = adrenocortical insufficiency. The adrenal glands cannot respond to ACTH stimulation. This is the diagnostic hallmark of: (1) primary Addison's disease, OR (2) iatrogenic HAC — the exogenous glucocorticoids (triamcinolone) have suppressed the HPA axis → adrenal glands have atrophied from lack of ACTH stimulation → cannot respond when challenged with exogenous ACTH.
c. What is your diagnosis?Click to reveal ▾▾
Diagnosis: Iatrogenic Hyperadrenocorticism (Iatrogenic HAC) with secondary HPA axis suppression.

The topical triamcinolone is being absorbed systemically → causing glucocorticoid excess (skin changes, PU/PD, immunosuppression) → simultaneously suppressing the HPA axis via negative feedback → adrenal glands atrophy from chronic ACTH suppression → adrenals cannot respond to exogenous ACTH on ACTH stim test.

Key distinguishing features:

  • History of exogenous glucocorticoid use (topical triamcinolone)
  • ACTH stim test: LOW post-stim cortisol (inadequate response) → indicates adrenal suppression, not hyperfunction
  • This is the ONLY pattern that can diagnose iatrogenic HAC — the LDDST is useless here (exogenous steroids cross-react with the dexamethasone assay and suppress HPA axis independently)
⚠️ Management: Gradually withdraw the topical triamcinolone (do NOT stop abruptly — adrenal glands need time to recover). Consider switching to a less potent topical steroid or alternative anti-pruritic therapy (oclacitinib, apoquel). The adrenal suppression will resolve over weeks to months as the exogenous steroid is withdrawn. Supplemental glucocorticoids during periods of stress (surgery, illness) may be needed until adrenal recovery is confirmed.
⚡ 16 clinical vignettes — feline hyperthyroidism, canine hypothyroidism, HAC, Addison's, DM, DKA, feline acromegaly, insulinoma. Click to flip.
FELINE HYPERTHYROID · RENAL MASKING
A 13yo cat treated with methimazole has Cr rise from 1.4 to 2.8 mg/dL at 4 weeks. The owner says "the medication caused kidney disease." What actually happened?
↻ flip
ANSWER
Pre-existing CKD was UNMASKED, not caused. Hyperthyroidism ↑GFR artificially lowers Cr below the azotemia threshold. When treated, GFR normalizes → true CKD creatinine appears. Use reversible treatment first (methimazole); reassess renal function at 4 weeks before committing to permanent treatment.
FELINE HYPERTHYROID · fT4 PITFALL
A 11yo cat with suspected hyperthyroidism has TT4 high-normal (58 nmol/L, RI 11–60). Your colleague orders fT4 alone and it comes back elevated. They say "elevated fT4 confirms hyperthyroidism." What's wrong with this reasoning?
↻ flip
ANSWER
fT4 is NEVER a standalone screening test in cats. 5–6% of normal cats have elevated fT4. Additionally, NTI can RAISE fT4 in cats (opposite of dogs). Elevated fT4 alone is non-diagnostic. The correct interpretation: HIGH-NORMAL TT4 + ELEVATED fT4 together = supports hyperthyroidism. fT4 is only useful alongside TT4.
HYPOTHYROID · T4AA INTERFERENCE
A 9yo Lab with weight gain and alopecia: TT4 = 18 nmol/L (normal, RI 11–60), fT4-ED = 4 pmol/L (LOW), TSH = 52 mU/L (HIGH). How do you reconcile the normal TT4?
↻ flip
ANSWER
T4 autoantibodies (T4AA) are falsely NORMALIZING TT4. T4AAs bind T4 in the non-dialysis assay → measured as T4 → inflates the result. fT4 by EQUILIBRIUM DIALYSIS physically removes T4AAs before measuring → reveals the true low value. TSH is elevated because the pituitary correctly senses inadequate T4. Pattern: Normal TT4 + Low fT4-ED + High TSH = hypothyroid with T4AA interference.
HYPOTHYROID · EUTHYROID SICK
A dog with severe aspiration pneumonia: TT4 = 8 nmol/L (LOW), fT4-ED = 12 pmol/L (normal), TSH = 18 mU/L (normal). Should you diagnose and treat hypothyroidism?
↻ flip
ANSWER
NO — this is euthyroid sick syndrome. Pattern: low TT4 + normal fT4-ED + normal TSH. Non-thyroidal illness suppresses TT4 production; fT4-ED is preserved (less affected); TSH remains normal because the pituitary senses adequate free T4. Do NOT diagnose hypothyroidism in sick patients. Treat the illness and retest when stable.
HAC · ALP MECHANISM
A 10yo Mini Poodle has ALP = 2100 IU/L, ALT = 180 IU/L. Bilirubin normal; not icteric. What is causing the massively elevated ALP and why doesn't it correlate with severity?
↻ flip
ANSWER
Steroid-induced ALP isoenzyme (C-ALP) from HAC. Cortisol induces production of this dog-specific isoenzyme in massive quantities — CATS LACK THIS ISOENZYME, so feline HAC does not cause this pattern. The enzyme is synthesized pharmacologically under cortisol influence regardless of actual hepatocyte damage. ALP does NOT correlate with disease severity or predict treatment response. Normal bilirubin + only mild ALT = no true liver damage.
HAC · UCCR PITFALL
A UCCR collected in-clinic = 28 (RI <15–20). Your colleague wants to proceed to LDDST. Why might this result be unreliable?
↻ flip
ANSWER
The UCCR is likely falsely elevated from STRESS CORTISOL secreted during the clinic visit. UCCR reflects ALL cortisol production, including stress-induced. A vet visit, car ride, handling, and unfamiliar environment trigger HPA axis activation → legitimate cortisol secretion → elevated UCCR even in normal dogs. Sample MUST be collected at HOME as a first-morning void, ideally 2+ days after any clinic visit. In-clinic UCCR is essentially uninterpretable. UCCR is only useful to RULE OUT HAC when collected correctly at home.
HAC · LDDST DISCRIMINATION
A dog's LDDST: resting 2.3 µg/dL → 4-hr <1.0 µg/dL → 8-hr 1.9 µg/dL. What type of HAC does this support?
↻ flip
ANSWER
PDH (pituitary-dependent HAC). 8-hr cortisol 1.9 > 1.4 µg/dL = POSITIVE for HAC. Then 4-hr <1.4 µg/dL = suppression = PDH pattern. Transient suppression at 4 hours (pituitary tumor partially suppressible with low-dose dex) followed by escape at 8 hours = hallmark PDH pattern. ADH (adrenal tumor) would show NO suppression at either time point because ACTH is already suppressed — the tumor secretes cortisol autonomously regardless of dex.
ADDISON'S · ELECTROLYTES
A 4yo female Standard Poodle collapses: Na 128, K 7.2, Na:K = 17.8, HR = 48 bpm, BUN 65, Cr 2.1, USG 1.018. The attending diagnoses "prerenal renal failure." What is the critical problem with this assessment?
↻ flip
ANSWER
This is Addison's masquerading as renal failure. Two red flags: (1) BRADYCARDIA in a hypovolemic patient — hypovolemia should cause tachycardia. Bradycardia = hyperkalemia suppressing cardiac conduction = Addison's. (2) Na:K = 17.8 — critically low (normal 27–40). The azotemia is pre-renal. Give 0.9% NaCl (NOT K-containing fluids!) + glucocorticoids + confirm with ACTH stim. NOT LRS (has K). NOT dextrose saline (dilutes Na further).
ADDISON'S · ATYPICAL
A 5yo Bearded Collie has 6 months of episodic vomiting and lethargy. Na:K = 30.6 (NORMAL). Can you rule out Addison's?
↻ flip
ANSWER
NO. Atypical (glucocorticoid-deficient) Addison's presents with normal electrolytes because aldosterone-producing cells (zona glomerulosa) are partially spared — mineralocorticoid function intact. Cortisol deficiency alone causes the waxing/waning GI signs, stress intolerance, and response to fluids/steroids. Most atypical dogs have low or undetectable aldosterone on testing despite normal serum electrolytes. Many develop classic electrolyte abnormalities over time. Test: ACTH stim — post-stim cortisol <2 µg/dL confirms adrenocortical insufficiency regardless of normal electrolytes.
DM · SODIUM
An 8yo Samoyed with DM has glucose = 380 mg/dL. Na = 142 mEq/L (RI 140–155 — "normal"). Why is normal sodium actually concerning in this patient?
↻ flip
ANSWER
Normal Na with marked chronic hyperglycemia = clinically significant DEHYDRATION. Glucose is an active osmol — it pulls water from interstitial/intracellular spaces into the vascular compartment → dilutional hyponatremia is EXPECTED at glucose 380 (expect ~↓1.6 mEq/L Na per 100 mg/dL glucose above 100). A "normal" sodium means the dilutional effect is being counteracted by hemoconcentration from severe dehydration — this dog is more dehydrated than clinical signs suggest.
DKA · POTASSIUM
A diabetic cat in DKA has serum K = 4.8 mEq/L (normal). You start IV insulin. What will happen to serum K and what must you do?
↻ flip
ANSWER
Serum K will DROP RAPIDLY. Even though serum K is currently normal, TOTAL BODY K stores are depleted. K has been shifted from intracellular to extracellular space by acidosis and hyperosmolality — masking the deficit. When insulin is given, it drives K back into cells → serum K can drop precipitously to dangerous hypokalemic levels → cardiac arrhythmias. ALWAYS add KCl to IV fluids before or with insulin. Monitor K every 4–6 hours throughout treatment.
DKA · PHOSPHORUS
During DKA treatment, serum P drops from 1.8 to 0.8 mg/dL 8 hours after starting insulin. The patient develops pale mucous membranes and pink plasma. What happened?
↻ flip
ANSWER
Intravascular hemolysis from severe hypophosphatemia. P dropped below the critical threshold of 1 mg/dL. Phosphorus is essential for RBC membrane integrity (2,3-DPG synthesis + ATP production). Severe hypophosphatemia → RBC membrane fragility → intravascular hemolysis → hemoglobinemia (pink plasma) + anemia (pale MM). Preventable by monitoring P every 4–6 hours during insulin therapy and supplementing KPO₄ in IV fluids when P approaches the critical threshold.
FELINE HAC
A 12yo neutered DSH is uncontrolled on escalating insulin doses (fasting glucose 400–500 mg/dL consistently). Skin tears during examination. ALP is mildly elevated. What disease should you screen for, and why is ALP not massively elevated like in dogs?
↻ flip
ANSWER
Feline HAC. Poorly controlled DM despite appropriate insulin + fragile/easily torn skin = screen for feline HAC. ALP is only mildly elevated because CATS LACK THE STEROID ALP ISOENZYME. In dogs, cortisol induces production of a dog-specific corticosteroid ALP isoenzyme → 10–100× increases. This isoenzyme does not exist in cats → feline HAC causes only modest ALP changes. Test: ACTH stimulation or LDDST. Also screen for acromegaly (IGF-1).
FELINE ACROMEGALY
An 11yo male cat has been diabetic for 2 years. Now requires 12 units BID (started at 2 units). He is gaining weight despite uncontrolled DM. Nose looks wider than in old photos. IGF-1 = 115 nmol/L. Phosphorus = 5.4 without azotemia. Interpret.
↻ flip
ANSWER
Feline acromegaly. IGF-1 >100 nmol/L = positive (70–100 = grey zone; <70 = negative). Weight gain DESPITE uncontrolled DM + extreme insulin resistance + facial changes (widened nose) = classic acromegaly. Hyperphosphatemia WITHOUT azotemia is GH-specific — GH promotes phosphate retention independent of kidney function. Normal BUN/Cr rules out renal hyperphosphatemia. Next step: pituitary MRI or CT to confirm somatotropic adenoma.
INSULINOMA · FERRET
A 5yo ferret has episodic staring and hind limb weakness. Blood glucose during an episode = 35 mg/dL. Immunoreactive insulin = 18 µIU/mL (RI 5–20 — within RI). Is the insulin level diagnostic?
↻ flip
ANSWER
YES — the insulin is diagnostically INAPPROPRIATELY HIGH. Insulin must ALWAYS be interpreted relative to concurrent glucose. At a glucose of 35 mg/dL, the normal pancreatic response is to secrete essentially ZERO insulin. Any measurable insulin at this level = autonomously secreted = insulinoma. The numerical RI assumes NORMAL glucose. At glucose 35, even 5 µIU/mL would be "inappropriately high." Whipple's Triad is satisfied: episodic signs + glucose 35 + (presumably) resolves with glucose. Diagnose insulinoma.
IATROGENIC HAC
A dog on long-term prednisone has PU/PD, pot-belly, elevated ALP, alopecia. ACTH stim: baseline 0.8 µg/dL, post-stim 0.9 µg/dL (flat). What type of HAC and what are the adrenal glands doing?
↻ flip
ANSWER
Iatrogenic HAC. Exogenous prednisone → negative feedback on hypothalamus/pituitary → suppressed endogenous ACTH → bilateral adrenal cortical ATROPHY. The adrenal glands cannot respond to exogenous ACTH stim because they are atrophied. Flat ACTH stim in this context = iatrogenic, not primary Addison's. Clinical signs are Cushingoid (from excess exogenous steroid). If prednisone is abruptly withdrawn → iatrogenic Addisonian crisis (must taper slowly).

📊 Endocrine Disease Quick-Pattern Summary

DiseaseNa:K / ElectrolytesGlucoseALPKey CBCUSG
Feline hyperthyroidNormalMild ↑ (22%)↑ALT > ALPStress leukogram; mild erythrocytosisVariable; ~50% >1.035
Canine hypothyroidNormalNormalMild ↑Mild normocytic non-regen anemia; occasional target cellsNo consistent change
HAC (dog)Normal electrolytesMild ↑; frank DM in 5%↑↑↑ >1000 (C-ALP)Stress leukogram; thrombocytosis<1.015–1.020 (85%)
Addison'sNa:K <27:1
↓Na, ↑K		↓ mild (20%)Mild ↑ (hypoperfusion)NO stress leukogram; eosinophilia/lymphocytosis in sick dog<1.030 in 88% (medullary washout)
DM (uncomplicated)Mild ↓Na (osmotic dilution)↑↑↑ persistentMild ↑Often unremarkable; leukocytosis if UTI1.025–1.035 (glucosuria)
DKA↓Na; total body K ALWAYS low (serum K variable)↑↑ (~500 mg/dL)VariableInflammatory leukogram if trigger presentUnreliable (glucosuria inflates)
Feline HACNormal↑ (poorly controlled DM)NO steroid isoenzyme — mild ↑ onlyStress leukogramVariable
Feline acromegalyMild ↑P without azotemia↑↑ (extreme insulin resistance)No major riseMild erythrocytosis; hyperglobulinemiaVariable
InsulinomaNormal↓↓ 30–50 mg/dLNormalNormal (unremarkable)Normal

🔬 Thyroid Test Decision Framework

Cats — Hyperthyroidism

  • Screen with TT4 only
  • Elevated TT4 in right context = diagnostic
  • High-normal TT4 → add fT4 (look at both together)
  • Elevated fT4 alone = NON-DIAGNOSTIC
  • Monitoring: use ONE assay consistently (each has its own RI)

Dogs — Hypothyroidism

  • Normal TT4 rules OUT hypothyroidism (high sensitivity)
  • Low TT4 alone does NOT confirm it (NTI, drugs cause false lows)
  • Add fT4-ED + TSH when TT4 is low or suspicious
  • Classic: ↓TT4 + ↓fT4-ED + ↑TSH
  • Euthyroid sick: ↓TT4 + normal fT4-ED + normal TSH
  • T4AA interference: normal TT4 + ↓fT4-ED + ↑TSH → use dialysis assay
  • NEVER test thyroid function in an acutely ill patient

🏥 HAC Screening & Addison's Diagnosis — When to Use Which Test

TestUse forCANNOT use for
UCCRRULE OUT HAC only (home sample). Normal = HAC unlikely.Diagnosing HAC (too many false positives); in-clinic sample (stress artifact)
LDDSTScreening + discrimination (PDH vs ADH). Most sensitive.Iatrogenic HAC; Addison's; treatment monitoring; acutely ill dogs (false positives)
ACTH stimONLY test for iatrogenic HAC. ONLY test for Addison's. Treatment monitoring. Shorter protocol.Differentiating PDH from ADH; patients recently administered steroids (cross-reacts)
Resting cortisolAddison's screen: >2 µg/dL rules OUT. <2 µg/dL → proceed to ACTH stim.HAC workup
eACTHPDH vs ADH discrimination (HIGH in PDH; LOW in ADH)Labile: chilled EDTA tube, plastic, no gel, spin + freeze immediately
Abdominal USPDH (bilateral adrenomegaly) vs ADH (unilateral mass + contralateral atrophy). First-line per ACVIM.Functionally differentiating — anatomy only, not physiology

Atypical HAC — Sex Hormone / Cortisol Precursor Excess

  • Controversial diagnosis — adrenal overproduction of sex hormones and/or cortisol precursors without cortisol elevation
  • Clinical signs identical to classic HAC (bilaterally symmetrical alopecia, thin skin, PU/PD, pot belly, calcinosis cutis)
  • Cortisol is normal → routine LDDST, UCCR, and standard ACTH stim tests are all NEGATIVE
  • Diagnosis: Extended ACTH stim panel measuring intermediate cortisol products AND sex hormones (progesterone, 17-hydroxyprogesterone, estradiol, androstenedione)
  • Available through University of Tennessee CVM (UT Panel)
  • Specificity questioned — some clinically normal dogs also show elevated values
⚠️ Clinical approach: Consider atypical HAC in dogs with classic HAC clinical signs but repeatedly normal LDDST and ACTH stim tests. Rule out other causes of alopecia first. Send UT Panel if index of suspicion remains high after standard testing negative.
🔑 Key contrast: Classic HAC = ↑cortisol (detected by LDDST/ACTH stim). Atypical HAC = cortisol normal, sex hormones/precursors ↑ (only detected by extended panel). Both can look identical clinically.

Primary Hyperaldosteronism (Conn's Syndrome)

Pathophysiology

  • Adrenal adenoma/carcinoma → autonomous aldosterone excess → Na retention + K wasting
  • Expands ECF volume → hypertension + suppression of RAAS (↓ renin)
  • Cats > dogs in frequency

Lab findings

TestFinding
K⁺Low — aldosterone drives renal K wasting
Na⁺High-normal to mildly elevated
CK↑ >1000 IU/L — hypokalemic polymyopathy
Aldosterone↑↑ markedly elevated
Renin↓ suppressed — confirms primary (autonomous)

Clinical signs

  • Hypokalemia: weakness, polymyopathy, collapse, cervical ventroflexion (cats), paresis
  • Hypertension: acute blindness (retinal detachment), hemorrhage

Diagnosis

  • ↑↑ aldosterone + ↓ renin (rules out secondary)
  • If renin unavailable: ↑ aldosterone + adrenal mass on US + exclude secondary
⚠️ Primary vs secondary: Secondary HA (CHF, CKD, liver failure) = aldosterone ↑ AND renin ↑. Primary HA = aldosterone ↑, renin ↓ (autonomous secretion suppresses RAAS).

Pheochromocytoma — Adrenal Medullary Catecholamine Tumor

Key features

  • Chromaffin cell tumor → excess epinephrine + norepinephrine; primarily dogs
  • Locally invasive; ~30% metastasis; catecholamine release episodic and unpredictable
  • Clinical signs: collapsing episodes, weakness, panting, PU/PD, severe hypertension → retinal detachment

Lab findings (non-specific)

  • No consistent specific CBC/chemistry changes
  • Mild hyperglycemia (usually below renal threshold); UA: hyposthenuria, proteinuria

Diagnosis

  • Adrenal mass on US + non-azotemic hypertension + clinical signs → tentative Dx
  • Must rule out HAC first (functional cortical mass)
  • Serum inhibin UNDETECTABLE → supports pheo; PDH and adrenocortical tumors → ↑ inhibin
  • Urinary catecholamines/metabolites — elevated levels strengthen presumptive diagnosis
🔑 Key discriminator: Inhibin is the best serum test to distinguish pheochromocytoma (undetectable) from cortical adrenal tumors and PDH (both elevated). Histology with chromogranin A staining confirms chromaffin cell origin.
GI Toxicology
☠️ Toxicology GIT #1–4: Seven topics — Iron · Urea · Salt · Zinc · Copper · NSAIDs (GI/renal/CNS dose-dependent toxicity) · GI Plants (Araceae, Amaryllidaceae, Pokeweed, Nightshades/Solanine, Castor Bean/Toxalbumins). Each entry covers mechanism, species susceptibility, clinical signs, lab findings, and treatment.

Iron — Sources & Toxicity Thresholds

Common sources

SourceNotes
MultivitaminsVarious iron salts: 12–48% elemental iron. Most common source — especially dogs
Oxygen absorber packetsRare ingestion
MRE heater packsRare ingestion
Injectable supplementsOccasional problem in horses, livestock, and other species

Toxicity thresholds (elemental iron, dog/most mammals)

DoseEffect
5–20 mg Fe/kgMild effects
20–60 mg Fe/kgModerate effects
>60 mg Fe/kgSevere effects

Pharmacokinetics

PhaseKey details
AbsorptionSmall intestine; saturable carrier-mediated (DMT-1); ~10–20% absorbed from diet; tightly regulated; GI storage as ferritin
DistributionExtensive; transferrin transports iron in blood
StorageStored as ferritin and hemosiderin
ExcretionNo active excretory process for iron once it enters the bloodstream — accumulation risk!

Iron Poisoning — Phases of Toxicity

Mode of action: (1) Direct irritant effects on GI mucosa. (2) Direct mitochondrial poison. (3) Induces oxidative stress — labile iron (Fe II/III/IV) acts as free radical catalytic center → lipid peroxidation → molecular damage (DNA, sugars, lipids, proteins) → ferroptosis → cell death. A vicious cycle: cell damage releases more stored iron from ferritin/hemosiderin → amplifies toxicity.
Phase I — 0–6 hours
  • Vomiting and diarrhea ± blood (hallmark)
  • Hypovolemic shock
  • CNS depression
  • Fever
  • Metabolic acidosis
Phase II — hours 6–24

Asymptomatic period possible — patient may appear to improve. Do NOT be reassured — liver failure is coming.

Phase III — 12–24 hours after Phase I
  • Liver failure (ALT ↑↑, bilirubin ↑)
  • Oliguria and anuria (shock-induced renal failure)

Diagnosis

TestFinding
Serum iron↑
Transferrin saturation↑
Serum ferritin↑
Hepatic iron conc.↑
RadiographsIron pills radiopaque — may be negative if liquid/chewable
HistopathPrussian blue stain (+) in liver
Serum Fe >500 µg/dL associated with significant toxicosis (metabolic acidosis, shock). Chelation threshold: serum Fe >300 µg/dL OR greater than total iron binding capacity → deferoxamine. Peak serum iron: 2–6 hr post-ingestion. Measure at 2–6 hr and again at 8–10 hr.

Iron Poisoning in Horses

Historically common prior to the 1980s — ferrous fumarate was routinely administered to neonatal foals. Hepatic failure was documented following oral administration of approximately 16 mg/kg body weight ferrous fumarate.

Clinical presentation (horses)

  • GI signs
  • Bloody diarrhea
  • Metabolic acidosis
  • Shock and coma
  • Multi-organ failure
Lethal exposures from injectable iron supplements have also occurred in horses. Management follows the same principles as in dogs: GI decontamination, deferoxamine chelation, and supportive care.

Iron — Management & Chelation

Thresholds for treatment

Dose ingestedAction
>20 mg Fe/kg + signs beyond mild vomitingOral decontamination, GI protectants, milk of magnesia (complexes Fe), abdominal radiographs, supportive care + IV fluids
>60 mg/kg elemental ironMonitor serum Fe + TIBC at 3 hr and 8–10 hr post-exposure; chelation if Fe >300 µg/dL or Fe > TIBC

Deferoxamine — chelation of choice

FeatureDetail
Dose40 mg/kg IM every 4–8 hr for 5–7 days
MechanismChelates free iron → ferrioxamine complex excreted in urine
Monitoring sign"Vin rosé urine" — pinkish-red discoloration of urine confirms active chelation
Side effectsInjection site pain/infection, fever, nausea, abdominal pain
RareAcute renal failure
Other chelating agents: deferasirox, deferiprone (less commonly used in veterinary practice).

Urea Toxicosis — Overview

Species: Ruminants primarily (cattle, sheep, goats). Urea is used as a non-protein nitrogen (NPN) source in feed to supplement dietary protein. Toxic doses occur when the rumen is overwhelmed by ammonia production.

Pathophysiology — step by step

Urea + H₂O → 2NH₃ + CO₂ (bacterial urease in rumen)
↓
Rumen pH ≥ 7.5–8 → more NH₃ (vs NH₄⁺) in alkaline environment → more crosses GIT membrane
↓
NH₃ absorbed into blood → hyperammonemia
↓
Brain: astrocyte swelling → cerebral edema → encephalopathy

Risk factors / conditions of poisoning

  • Sudden access to urea supplement
  • Poor mixing of feed
  • Errors in calculating urea amount
  • Accidental overconsumption
  • Low carbohydrate rations (less NH₃ incorporated into amino acids)
  • Contamination of water
Toxic dose: 0.25–0.5 g/kg (cow). Potentially fatal: 1–1.5 g/kg.

Urea Toxicosis — Clinical Signs, Diagnosis & Treatment

Clinical signs (ruminants)

  • Twitching of ears and facial muscles
  • Grinding teeth (bruxism)
  • Frothy salivation
  • Bloat + abdominal pain
  • Frequent urination
  • Forced rapid breathing
  • Staggering → violent struggling and bellowing
  • Terminal spasms
  • "Sudden death" near the urea source

Diagnosis

  • History of urea access + clinical signs
  • ↑ Rumen ammonia levels
  • Marked ammonia smell when rumen opened at necropsy
  • ↑ Rumen pH (≥7.5)
  • Inflammatory changes in rumen mucosa
  • Bloat + generalized congestion + pulmonary edema + fluid in pericardial sac
  • No characteristic gross microscopic lesions

Treatment

10 gallons cold water + 1 gallon vinegar
Cold water: ↓ urease enzyme activity + dilutes urea.
Vinegar (acetic acid = H⁺): NH₃ + H⁺ → NH₄⁺ (ammonium ion). NH₄⁺ cannot cross GIT membranes → trapped in rumen → not absorbed.

Prevention

  • Limit urea in diet; avoid overconsumption of palatable liquid supplements
  • Slowly adapt animals to urea → allows rumen flora to adapt
  • Once adapted, continue feeding urea consistently (interruption → loss of tolerance)

Salt Poisoning / Water Deprivation — Overview

Agents: NaCl (table salt, rock salt/halite), sodium propionate/acetate/carbonate. All cause hypernatremia (serum Na >145 mmol/L). Sodium is an effective osmole agent — drives water movement across cell membranes. Most common in swine, cattle, dogs, and poultry.

Direct vs. Indirect Poisoning

TypeMechanismExamples
DirectAcute salt intake overwhelms excretion mechanismsBrackish/salt water; table salt as emetic; salt-flour mixtures; deicers; milk replacer errors; hypertonic saline administration
IndirectHigh salt → water deprivation → sudden rehydration → cerebral osmotic crisisMost common in veterinary cases. Fish waste, whey, potato chips, baker's dough → then water withheld (frozen, mechanical failure, overcrowding)
Direct poisoning less likely if salt-regulating mechanisms are intact and fresh water is available.

Toxic doses

SpeciesToxic doseLethal dose
Dogs2–3 g/kg table salt4 g/kg table salt
Clinical signs (dogs)Serum Na >170 mEq/L
General (acute LD50)1–6 g/kg across species

Pathophysiology — the 4-step osmotic trap

Normal
ECF: 140 mEq Na⁺ / 290 mOsm
ICF: 290 mOsm
→
Salt poisoning
ECF: 160 mEq Na⁺ / 320 mOsm
Water leaves cells → cell shrinkage
→
Compensation
Brain cells produce organic osmolytes
ICF osmolality rises to 315 mOsm
→
Rehydration (rapid)
ECF drops to 280 mOsm
ICF still 300 mOsm → water rushes IN → cell swelling, damage, death
The danger is rehydration. Brain cells made organic osmolytes during the hypernatremic state. Rapid correction of Na⁺ → ECF becomes hypotonic relative to ICF → water floods into brain cells → cerebral edema → encephalopathy → death. Always correct hypernatremia SLOWLY.

Clinical Signs by Species

General (all species)

  • Thirst (early)
  • GI signs: vomiting, diarrhea, colic, bloat
  • Neurologic: tremors, seizures, coma

Pigs (most classic / high-yield)

StageSigns
Early↑ thirst, pruritus, constipation, anorexia, ataxia, blindness or deafness, circling around a single limb
Delayed (1–5 days)Seizures, opisthotonos, coma, sitting on haunches, goose stepping

Diagnosis

TestFinding
Serum Na>160 mEq/L diagnostic
CSF Na> serum sodium (CSF lags)
Brain sodium (fresh tissue)>1,800 ppm wet weight
Gross pathologyCerebral edema/necrosis; inflamed/congested GI mucosa; gastric ulcers; hydropericardium (poultry)
Histopath — PIGSEosinophilic cuffs around vessels in cerebral cortex and adjacent meninges (pathognomonic in pigs). Absent in cattle.
Histopath — allCerebral edema or necrosis, neuronal necrosis and spongiosis

Treatment & Prognosis

Treatment

⚠️ NEVER rehydrate rapidly. Rapid correction of hypernatremia = cerebral edema = death. Water correction must be SLOW and controlled.
  • Remove offending feed or water
  • Provide fresh water initially in small amounts
  • Oral water: 0.5% of body weight per hour
  • May need to correct acidosis

Acute hypernatremia (Na >160 mmol/L)

  • Start D5W at 4 ml/kg/hr to decrease Na⁺
  • If hypernatremia + volume deficit together → hypotonic saline (not pure D5W)
  • Furosemide 2.2 mg/kg IV once
  • Diazepam for agitation or seizures
  • Antiemetic for vomiting (dogs)

Prognosis

Hypernatremia significantly worsens prognosis. Mortality rate may be >50% in affected animals.
  • Changes in the eye and blindness may be irreversible
  • Vascular damage
  • Retinal and vitreous hemorrhage
  • Posterior vitreal and retinal detachment

Toxicology Quick-Compare: Iron vs. Urea vs. Salt vs. Zinc vs. Copper

FeatureIronUreaSaltZincCopper
SpeciesDogs/cats primarily; anyRuminants onlySwine, cattle, dogs, poultryAll (dogs, birds, ruminants)Sheep esp.; dogs (storage disease)
Key mechanismFree radicals → hepatic necrosisRumen NH₃ → hyperammonemiaHypernatremia → osmolytes → cerebral edema on rehydrationOxidative damage to RBC membrane → hemolysis; GI irritationHepatic Cu accumulation → hemolytic crisis; oxidative stress
Hallmark signBloody vomiting/diarrhea → liver failure"Sudden death" near urea sourceNeurologic signs; pig eosinophilic cuffsHeinz body hemolytic anemia; icterusGunmetal kidneys; port-wine urine; hemoglobinuria
Key lab finding↑ serum Fe, ↑ transferrin sat; "vin rosé urine" with treatment↑ rumen NH₃, ↑ rumen pH, hyperammonemiaSerum Na >160 mEq/L; brain Na >1800 ppm↑ serum Zn >2 µg/mL; Heinz bodies; ↑ AMS/LPS; hemoglobinuriaLiver Cu ≥150 ppm (sheep); blood Cu ≥5–10 µg/mL; ↑ ALT/AST
Pathognomonic lesionPrussian blue stain (+) in liverNo characteristic lesion; ammonia smell from rumenEosinophilic cuffs (pigs only)Radiopaque metal on abdominal radiograph; Heinz bodies on blood smearSwollen gunmetal-colored kidneys; centrilobular hepatic necrosis
Specific treatmentDeferoxamine (chelation)Cold water + vinegar → NH₄⁺Slow rehydration (0.5% BW/hr)Remove metal (endoscopy); CaNa2EDTA or D-penicillamine chelationD-penicillamine or ammonium tetrathiomolybdate; poor prognosis

Zinc Toxicosis — Overview & Sources

Key concept: Zinc is an essential trace metal. Toxicosis occurs from ingestion of zinc-containing metal objects. The hallmark is Heinz body hemolytic anemia from oxidative damage to RBC membranes (glutathione depletion). All species are susceptible.

Common sources

SourceNotes
Pennies minted after 198397.5% zinc core — most common source in dogs
Zipper pulls, bra clips, leash hooks, screws, nutsHousehold metal objects
Zinc oxide (diaper rash cream)GI irritation; less systemic toxicity
Zinc nanoparticles (sunscreen)Low dermal absorption; mild GI effects only
Vitamin/mineral supplementsMultivitamins: iron usually greater concern than zinc

Pharmacokinetics

ProcessDetail
AbsorptionSmall intestine; passive diffusion + saturable carrier (~25% absorbed)
DistributionAlbumin, α₂-macroglobulin, transferrin as transporters
StorageMetallothionein (inducible zinc-binding protein)
ExcretionFecal >> pancreatic > urinary
Mechanism: Direct GI mucosal irritation + oxidative damage to RBC membrane (NOT to hemoglobin directly) → glutathione depletion → intravascular hemolysis → Heinz bodies.

Zinc — Clinical Signs by Species & Duration

Small animals — acute exposure

  • Vomiting and anorexia
  • Diarrhea ± hematochezia
  • Heinz body hemolytic anemia (hallmark)
  • Pale mucous membranes
  • Icterus (pre-hepatic hemolysis)
  • Coma

Birds — acute exposure

  • Lethargy, anorexia, regurgitation
  • Diarrhea ± hematochezia
  • Polyuria and polydipsia
  • Seizures, muscle wasting
  • Hemolytic anemia

Prolonged exposure (all species)

  • Slow release of zinc from retained metal
  • Anorexia, weight loss, depression
  • Pancreatitis — especially in birds
⚠️ Zinc-induced pancreatitis in birds is a distinct presentation of chronic/prolonged zinc exposure — the same organism can present acutely (hemolysis) or chronically (pancreatitis) depending on rate of zinc release from retained metal.

Zinc — Diagnosis & Management

Diagnosis

TestFinding
Serum zinc>2 µg/mL (normal 0.7–2 µg/mL)
Abdominal radiographRadiopaque metallic foreign body in GIT
CBCHeinz body hemolytic anemia, ↓ PCV
ChemistryHypernatremia; ↑ AMS/LPS (pancreatitis); azotemia (glomerular damage + renal tubular necrosis); hemoglobinuria
Coagulation↑ PT and aPTT (prolonged)

Management

  • Remove metal object — endoscopy preferred; surgery if needed (increased post-op complications)
  • Proton pump inhibitors / H₂-blockers — decrease gastric acid formation of zinc salts
  • Gastroprotectants (e.g., sucralfate) for gastric ulceration
  • IV diuresis for nephrotoxicity
  • Blood transfusions for severe hemolytic anemia

Chelation agents

AgentDose
CaNa₂EDTA100 mg/kg/day IV or SC × 3 days (divided into 4 doses)
D-penicillamine110 mg/kg/day PO ÷ q8h × 1 week
Dimercaprol (BAL)Alternative
DMSA (succimer)Alternative oral chelator
Prognosis: Can be favorable depending on duration and aggressiveness of treatment. Prevention: reduce exposure to metal objects/toys; annual radiographs in zoo animals.

Copper Toxicosis — Overview

Key concept: Copper is an essential element under tight regulatory control. Toxicosis occurs via two routes: (1) storage disease — genetic or dietary accumulation in liver → hemolytic crisis; (2) acute poisoning — overwhelming GI dose. Ruminants (especially sheep) and certain dog breeds are most susceptible.

Normal copper physiology

  • Absorbed in gut via high-affinity copper transporter CTR-1 (SLC31A1)
  • Binds metallothionein in enterocytes; exits via copper-transporting ATPase
  • Stored in liver and bone
  • Excreted in bile (primary route)
  • Copper deficiency → hypochromic, microcytic anemia

Storage disease — species

SpeciesDisease/Breed
HumansWilson's disease (ATP7B mutation)
DogsBedlington Terriers, Labrador Retrievers, others — hepatic copper accumulation
SheepMost susceptible ruminant — low excretory capacity

Sources — acute poisoning

  • Anthelmintic drenches containing copper sulfate
  • Mineral mixes with excess copper
  • Improperly formulated rations
  • Young calves or sheep injected with soluble copper
  • Copper water pipes

Mechanism

  • Incompletely understood
  • Direct GI mucosal irritation
  • Oxidative stress → hepatic accumulation → liver failure
  • When liver storage capacity exceeded → copper released into blood → lipid peroxidation → intravascular hemolysis
Chronic poisoning more likely with low dietary molybdenum and sulfur — Mo and S form thiomolybdates that bind copper and reduce absorption. This is a worldwide concern in sheep grazing.

Chronic Copper Poisoning: Sheep (High-Yield)

Sheep accumulate copper subclinically for weeks to months. Liver capacity is eventually exceeded → sudden release of copper into blood → hemolytic crisis. Triggered by stressors.

Risk factors for triggering hemolytic crisis

  • Transportation / handling stress
  • Lactation
  • Strenuous exercise
  • Deteriorating plane of nutrition
  • Ingestion of hepatotoxic plants or cattle feed (high copper)
  • Copper water pipes

Prognosis

Herd morbidity often <5%, but >75% of affected animals die. Losses may continue for up to 2 months after the dietary problem is corrected (copper remains stored in survivors' livers).

Clinical signs (sheep)

  • CNS depression, weakness, recumbency
  • Hemolysis, methemoglobinemia
  • Hemoglobinuria (port-wine–colored urine)
  • ↓ PCV
  • ↑ liver enzymes (ALT, AST) → jaundice

Pathognomonic lesions

  • Swollen, gunmetal-colored kidneys (copper-laden)
  • Port-wine–colored urine
  • Icterus and methemoglobin
  • Enlarged spleen and liver
  • Centrilobular hepatic necrosis + renal tubular necrosis

Acute vs. Chronic Copper Poisoning — Comparison

FeatureAcute PoisoningChronic Poisoning (Sheep)
OnsetHours to days after high-dose exposureWeeks to months of subclinical accumulation → sudden hemolytic crisis
Primary presentationSevere gastroenteritis: abdominal pain, diarrhea, shockHemolysis, hemoglobinuria, jaundice, recumbency
GI signsProminent (vomiting, diarrhea, dehydration)Less prominent
HemolysisPossible (within 1–3 days)Hallmark — intravascular hemolysis is the crisis event
LiverCentrilobular hepatic necrosis + icterusHepatic necrosis + massive enzyme elevation
KidneyRenal failureGunmetal kidneys (copper deposits) + renal tubular necrosis
DeathMay occur within daysOften peracute once crisis starts; >75% die

Copper — Diagnosis & Treatment

Diagnostic copper concentrations

SampleDiagnostic threshold
Liver≥150 ppm (sheep); consistent with copper poisoning most species
Kidney≥15 ppm
Blood≥5–10 µg/mL (normal ~1 µg/mL)

Other lab findings

  • ↑ ALT, AST (hepatocellular necrosis)
  • Hemolysis indicators: ↓ PCV, hemoglobinuria, methemoglobinemia
  • Azotemia (renal tubular necrosis)
  • Icterus (bilirubinemia)

Treatment

Prognosis is often very poor once the hemolytic crisis has started. Treatment is largely supportive.
AgentUse
D-penicillamineChelation — binds copper for urinary excretion
Calcium versenate (CaNa₂EDTA)Chelation
Ammonium tetrathiomolybdatePreferred in ruminants — forms stable Cu-Mo complexes; used orally to reduce absorption
Molybdenum + sulfur supplementationPrevention in sheep at-risk flocks
In dogs with hereditary copper storage disease (Bedlington Terriers), long-term D-penicillamine or zinc supplementation (competes for Cu absorption) is used for maintenance.

NSAIDs — Overview & Pharmacokinetics

Core mechanism: NSAIDs inhibit cyclooxygenase enzymes (COX-1 and/or COX-2) → reduce prostaglandin synthesis → interfere with normal prostaglandin-mediated cytoprotection of GI mucosa and renal perfusion. Aspirin irreversibly inhibits COX; most others reversibly inhibit COX.

Chemical classes

ClassExample
SalicylatesAspirin (nonselective COX inhibitor)
Propionic acidIbuprofen, Naproxen (nonselective)
Phenylacetic acidDiclofenac (nonselective)
IndolesIndomethacin (nonselective)
OxicamsPiroxicam, Meloxicam
PyrazolesPhenylbutazone (horses)
SulfonanilidesNimesulide
COX-2 preferential/selectiveCarprofen (preferential), Celecoxib, Deracoxib

Pharmacokinetics

PhaseKey points
AbsorptionWell absorbed from GI; food can enhance bioavailability
DistributionRapid (hours); highly protein-bound (albumin); cross placenta; ion trapping in alkaline horse urine
MetabolismExtensive hepatic (CYP450 + glucuronidation); cats especially sensitive — poor glucuronidation; prodrugs (sulindac, parecoxib) require hepatic activation
ExcretionBiliary excretion + reabsorption; renal excretion of unchanged drug <5–10%; excreted into milk
⚠️ Cats are especially sensitive to NSAID toxicosis due to deficient glucuronidation — require approximately half the dose to cause toxicosis compared to dogs.

NSAID Toxicity — Dose-Response (Ibuprofen, Dog)

Clinical signs often develop within 4–12 hours of ingestion. Toxicity is dose-dependent and can produce GI, renal, and CNS effects.
Dose (mg/kg/day)DaysEffects
8–1630Vomiting, diarrhea, nausea, abdominal pain, anorexia; gastric ulceration/erosions
100–1251Gastric ulceration
175–3001Renal failure
>4001Seizures, ataxia, coma
≥6001Death
Cats are susceptible at approximately half the doses required to cause toxicosis in dogs.

Carprofen — Oral Toxicity Data

SpeciesDose (mg/kg/day)DaysEffect
Cat11–5Therapeutic
Cat41Gastric ulceration/erosions
Cat81Renal failure
Dog2.2–4.41–5Therapeutic
Dog201Gastric ulceration/erosions
Dog401Renal dysfunction
⚠️ Even at therapeutic levels, some dogs have exhibited an idiosyncratic hepatocellular toxicosis with carprofen.

Clinical Signs by Species

Dog

  • Gastric ulcers: anorexia, vomiting, diarrhea, melena, abdominal pain
  • Acute kidney injury: polyuria/polydipsia, azotemia, dose-dependent renal tubular degeneration, renal papillary necrosis
  • Hepatotoxicity (idiosyncratic; rare)
  • Abdominal radiographs may show pneumoperitoneum and loss of serosal detail
  • Clinical signs often develop in 4–12 hr

Horse

  • Gastric ulcers: anorexia, diarrhea, melena, abdominal pain, colic, secondary endotoxemia
  • Protein-losing enteropathy (PLE): hypoproteinemia, ventral edema
  • Esophageal ulcers/ulcerative stomatitis: difficult prehension, excessive salivation, painful swallowing
  • Renal papillary necrosis (phenylbutazone): hyponatremia, hypochloremia, coagulative necrosis of renal crest
  • Higher incidence in foals and dehydrated horses

NSAID Management

Decrease GI absorption (<2 hr)

  • Emetic (often not very effective)
  • Activated charcoal ± saline/osmotic cathartic; repeat AC may be needed (indomethacin, sulindac, piroxicam undergo enterohepatic recirculation)
  • Gastric lavage ("concretions" may form)

Gastric ulcer management

DrugDoseNotes
SucralfateDog: 0.5–1 g q8–12h PO; Cat: 0.25 g q8–12h POUse with active ulcers
Omeprazole (PPI)0.7 mg/kg q24h PO (dog)More effective than H2-blockers
Cimetidine (H2)10 mg/kg q8h IM/IV/POH2-receptor antagonist
Ranitidine (H2)Dog: 2 mg/kg q8h; Cat: 2.5 mg/kg q12h IV or 3.5 mg/kg q12h POH2-receptor antagonist
Misoprostol3 µg/kg PO q8hSynthetic PGE1 analog; prevents aspirin-induced ulcers; NOT recommended after ulcer formation; may not prevent renal effects
Metoclopramide0.2–0.4 mg/kg q6–8h PO or SQControl of vomiting

Symptomatic & supportive

  • Assisted ventilation ± oxygen
  • Control seizures
  • Control hypotension and hemorrhage
  • IV fluids: 0.45% saline + 2.5% dextrose
  • Dopamine (2.5 µg/kg/min) or dobutamine (5–20 µg/kg/min) — questionable efficacy
  • Peritoneal dialysis may be needed

Special considerations

NSAIDs are highly protein-bound → hemodialysis may be ineffective. Consider: therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE), plasmapheresis, charcoal hemoperfusion, or intralipid therapy.

GI Plants — Overview

Vomiting and diarrhea are common signs of many plant intoxications. It is somewhat arbitrary to classify plants as having "predominantly GI effects" — many also have systemic effects (e.g., cardiac glycosides cause GI + CVS signs). Severity ranges from simple vomiting to severe gastroenteritis with systemic involvement.

MILD — Vomiting only

Araceae (Philodendron, Dieffenbachia, Caladium)

MODERATE — Vomiting/Diarrhea ± systemic

Amaryllidaceae (Narcissus, Daffodil, Amaryllis)

SEVERE — Gastroenteritis + systemic

Pokeweed, Nightshades (Solanum spp.), Castor bean (Ricinus communis), Abrus precatorius

Other plants associated with GI signs: Marsh marigold (Caltha), Christmas Rose (Helleborus niger), Mistletoe (Phoradendron spp.), Saponaria spp., Sesbania spp.

Oral Irritants: Araceae Family

Family: >1800 species including Philodendron spp., Dieffenbachia spp. (dumbcane), Caladium spp. Toxic principle: calcium oxalate crystals — needle-like raphide crystals that cause mechanical damage to oral and pharyngeal mucosa.

Clinical signs

  • Salivation, pawing at mouth
  • Vomiting, dyspnea
  • Immediate pain may limit amount consumed
  • Skin irritant — blistering from sap
  • Renal lesions occasionally reported
  • Death from choking (very rare)

Mechanism & Treatment

Calcium oxalate raphide crystals → needle-like mechanical damage to mucosa. Milk provides Ca²⁺ that binds free oxalate → reduces mucosal irritation.
  • Give milk following oral exposure
  • Antihistamine
  • Symptomatic and supportive

Potent Emetic Plants: Amaryllidaceae Family

Plants: Narcissus spp. (narcissus, jonquil, daffodil), Amaryllis. Potent emetics — elicit vomiting without prominent diarrhea. Exposure commonly occurs from indoor plants or during planting season.

Clinical signs

  • Vomiting (predominant sign)
  • Oral irritation, abdominal pain
  • Diarrhea (less prominent than vomiting)
  • Vomiting often resolves within 24–36 hours

Treatment

  • Symptomatic and supportive
  • Emetics: generally NOT needed — plant is already a potent emetic
  • Activated charcoal
  • Avoid cathartic

Pokeweed (Phytolacca americana)

Toxic principle: Saponins (steroid or triterpene glycosides) — dissolve cell membranes; bitter-tasting; present in all plant parts. Toxicity of berries decreases as they mature. Common in eastern USA.

Toxicity facts

  • All species susceptible; all parts toxic
  • Humans: 10 raw berries → serious toxicosis; 2–3 uncooked berries may be fatal to a child
  • Ruminants less affected — rumen dilution/degradation
  • Fatality reported from root consumption

Lesions

  • Mild to severe gastroenteritis; hemorrhagic/ulcerative gastritis (worst lesion)
  • Liver: congestion, dark discoloration, swelling
  • Peripheral blood plasmacytosis reported

Clinical signs

  • 2–3 hour latent period
  • Nausea, vomiting, hemorrhagic diarrhea
  • Cholinergic signs reported in people
  • Recovery generally within 24–48 hours

Treatment

  • Activated charcoal
  • Generally avoid cathartics
  • Anti-emetics; control dehydration; supportive care

Nightshades (Solanum spp.) — Solanine

Family: Solanaceae (>1500 Solanum species). Includes S. tuberosum (potato), S. lycopersicum (tomato), S. melongena (eggplant), S. dulcamara (bittersweet), S. nigrum (black nightshade). Toxic principle: Solanine (glycoalkaloid + other alkaloids) — found in all plant parts; concentrates in green berries.

Mechanisms of action

  • Inhibit cholinesterase
  • Disrupt cell membranes
  • Inhibit mitochondria (opens mPTP → Ca²⁺ release → apoptosis)
  • Potassium channel effects

Toxicity thresholds (solanine)

SpeciesDose
Humans — toxic3 mg/kg
Humans — estimated lethal5–10 mg/kg
Rodents/rabbits — lethal20–35 mg/kg
Sheep — toxic (not lethal)225 mg/kg

Clinical signs — Dogs

  • GI: bloat, anorexia, vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhea, fever
  • CNS/CVS: respiratory and CNS depression, mydriasis, muscle tremors, incoordination, tachycardia or bradycardia, dyspnea

Clinical signs — Livestock

  • GI: bloat/ileus, anorexia, colic, diarrhea; head/facial subcutaneous edema; urinary retention
  • Purkinje cell degeneration (chronic): ataxia, opisthotonos, nystagmus — notable with S. viarium (tropical soda apple)

Treatment

  • Symptomatic and supportive; fluids/electrolytes
  • Nausea control; GI prokinetics
  • Atropine (control bradycardia from cholinesterase inhibition)
  • Activated charcoal

Toxalbumins — Castor Bean & Related Plants

Toxalbumins are toxic plant proteins that inhibit protein synthesis by blocking ribosomal function. They contain a lectin B-chain that binds the cell surface and promotes toxin entry, and a catalytic A-chain that destroys ribosomal function.

Toxalbumin sources

PlantToxalbumin
Ricinus communis (Castor bean)Ricin
Robinia pseudoacacia (Black locust)Robin
Abrus precatorius (Jequirity/Rosary pea)Abrin
Jatropha curcas (Barbados/Purging Nut)Curcin

Mechanism — Ricin

  • 66 kDa glycoprotein; two subunits (A and B chains)
  • B-chain (lectin): binds galactose residues on cell surface → endocytosis
  • A-chain: eliminates adenine base from 28S rRNA of large ribosomal subunit → blocks GTP hydrolysis and protein translation → cell death

Ricin toxicity

RouteLD₅₀
Inhalation / IV / IP~5–25 µg/kg
Oral~20 mg/kg; ~4 seeds/kg
Hard seed coat slows digestion. More toxic if seed is pierced, broken, or chewed.

Clinical signs (ingestion)

  • GI irritation, ulceration, hemorrhage — rapid onset within 1–2 hr
  • Absorbed within 2 hr via intestinal blood vessels and lymphatics
  • Accumulates in liver and spleen → necrosis of liver, spleen, kidneys
  • Hypoglycemia; leukocytosis commonly observed
  • Death within 1–3 days

Treatment

  • No specific antidote
  • Activated charcoal; emetics/gastric lavage
  • Fluids, vasopressor therapy, electrolyte support
  • Research: anti-ricin IgG inhalation; formalin-killed toxoid immunization protocols

GI Plants Quick-Compare

PlantFamilyToxic PrincipleSeverityKey SignsTreatment Highlights
Philodendron, Dieffenbachia, CaladiumAraceaeCalcium oxalate raphide crystals (mechanical)MildSalivation, pawing at mouth, vomiting; pain limits ingestionMilk, antihistamine, supportive
Narcissus, Daffodil, AmaryllisAmaryllidaceaeAlkaloids (galantamine, lycorine)Mild–ModPredominantly vomiting; resolves 24–36 hrSupportive; NO additional emetic; AC; avoid cathartic
Phytolacca americana (Pokeweed)PhytolaccaceaeSaponins (steroid/triterpene glycosides)Severe2–3 hr latency; hemorrhagic diarrhea; cholinergic signs; liver congestionAC; avoid cathartics; anti-emetics; fluids
Solanum spp. (Nightshades)SolanaceaeSolanine (glycoalkaloid) — cholinesterase inhibitor + membrane disruptorSevereGI + CNS/CVS (mydriasis, tremors, bradycardia/tachycardia); livestock: Purkinje cell degeneration, ataxiaAC; atropine (bradycardia); GI prokinetics; supportive
Ricinus communis + Abrus, Robinia, JatrophaVariousToxalbumins (Ricin, Abrin, Robin, Curcin) — block ribosomal protein synthesisSevereGI hemorrhage 1–2 hr; liver/spleen/kidney necrosis; hypoglycemia; death 1–3 daysNo antidote; AC; gastric lavage; fluids; vasopressors
Liver Toxicology
💊 Acetaminophen (APAP / paracetamol): Most commonly used analgesic worldwide. Present in hundreds of OTC products. Cats are exquisitely sensitive due to glucuronidation deficiency. Primary toxic metabolite = NAPQI. Treatment = N-acetylcysteine (NAC).

Sources & Uses

Sources

  • Tylenol (brand) + generic formulations
  • Hundreds of OTC preparations (cold/flu, PM sleep, combination analgesics)
  • Also known as paracetamol (international name)
  • Important: Many combination products (Nyquil, Theraflu, Percocet) contain hidden APAP — owner may not realize they gave it

Uses

  • Analgesic; antipyretic; weak anti-inflammatory
  • Most commonly used analgesic worldwide
  • Safe in humans at therapeutic doses via glucuronidation/sulfation pathways

Pharmacokinetics & Metabolic Pathways

  • Rapid oral absorption (peak 30–60 min)
  • Well-distributed to brain, liver, and other tissues
  • Hepatic metabolism via CYP450 → then glucuronidation or sulfation → safe metabolites
  • Critical side reaction: CYP450 → NAPQI (N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine) — the toxic metabolite (5–15% of dose normally)
⚙️
SLIDE 4 — APAP METABOLIC PATHWAYS (DIAGRAM)
Three pathways: (1) UDP-glucuronosyltransferase (40–67%) → glucuronic acid conjugate [SAFE]; (2) Phenolsulfotransferase (20–49%) → sulphuric acid conjugate [SAFE]; (3) CYP450 (5–15%) → NAPQI [TOXIC]. NAPQI + glutathione → non-toxic (normal doses). NAPQI + cell macromolecules → protein adducts + cell death (overdose/cat).
CYP450 → NAPQI = the toxic branch (5–15% of dose normally)
⚠️ Why 5–15% matters in cats: Cats have dramatically deficient glucuronidation (UGT enzyme deficiency) — more drug is shunted to the CYP450 → NAPQI pathway. NAPQI is generated faster than glutathione can neutralize it → toxicity at doses safe in dogs/humans.
⚙️ NAPQI mechanism: When NAPQI production exceeds glutathione reserves (~70% depletion threshold), NAPQI covalently binds to critical cell proteins → protein adducts → cell death. The liver is the primary target because CYP450 metabolism occurs there.

NAPQI Mechanism of Hepatotoxicity

APAP ingested
→
CYP450 2E1
(hepatic)
→
NAPQI
Reactive metabolite
→
+ Glutathione
(adequate)
→ Non-toxic conjugates
OR
Glutathione DEPLETED
(>70%)
→ Protein adducts
→ Cell death
ConditionNAPQI fateResult
Normal therapeutic dose, normal animalNAPQI + glutathione → cysteine/mercapturic acid conjugates → urineSafe — no toxicity
Overdose, normal animalGlutathione depleted (>70%) → NAPQI binds proteins/DNACentrilobular hepatic necrosis
Normal dose, CATUGT deficiency → more APAP shunted to CYP450 → NAPQI excess → glutathione depleted rapidlyToxicity at lower doses; methemoglobinemia prominent

Why Cats Are Most Susceptible — Species Comparison

SpeciesGlucuronidationNAPQI generationClinical outcomeToxic dose
🐱 CatSEVERELY DEFICIENT — UGT1A6/1A9 mutations↑↑ (more drug → CYP450 → NAPQI)Methemoglobinemia dominant + facial/paw edema; liver failure less prominent<50–60 mg/kg
🐕 DogNormalNormal (at therapeutic doses)Liver failure dominant (icterus, hepatic necrosis); MetHb less prominent≥200 mg/kg
🧑 HumanNormalNormalLiver failure (overdose); MetHb not prominent~150 mg/kg
⚠️ Cats also lack adequate RBC methemoglobin reductase → MetHb persists longer → chocolate/brown MM, Heinz bodies, hemolytic anemia is MORE PROMINENT in cats than hepatic failure. Dogs show liver failure first (icterus at 24–36 hr). Cats show MetHb signs within hours.

Clinical Signs — Species Comparison

SignCats (dominant)Dogs (dominant)Timing
GI signsVomiting, anorexiaVomiting, nauseaEarly (hours)
MethemoglobinemiaPROMINENT — cyanosis, blue-grey/chocolate MM, tachycardia, increased respiration rate, weaknessPresent but less prominentWithin hours of ingestion
Facial & paw edemaProminent and characteristic — swollen muzzle, swollen paws; occurs within hoursLess commonHours
Liver failure signsLess prominent (MetHb kills before liver fails)PROMINENT — icterus, CNS depression, weakness, hypothermia24–36 hr post-ingestion (dogs)
HemoglobinuriaProminent (hemolysis)Can occurWith MetHb/Heinz body crisis
🐕
SLIDE 8 — METHEMOGLOBINEMIA (DOG)
Blue-grey mucous membranes and tongue, pronounced facial edema. Chocolate-brown discoloration from methemoglobin (Fe³⁺ — cannot carry O₂). Dog is weak and tachycardic.
Blue-grey or chocolate MM = MetHb
🐱
SLIDE 9 — FACIAL/PAW EDEMA (CAT)
Cat with severe facial edema — swollen muzzle, periorbital edema. Also shown: swollen paws. These signs occur within hours of APAP ingestion in cats. More characteristic of feline toxicosis.
Facial + paw edema within hours = classic feline APAP toxicosis

Clinical Pathology Findings

Hematologic

  • Heinz body anemia
  • Methemoglobinemia (measured as % MetHb)
  • Hemoglobinuria (dark brown urine)
  • Thrombocytopenia

Biochemistry

  • ALT + AST ↑↑ dramatically (hepatocellular leakage)
  • Hyperbilirubinemia (liver failure + hemolysis)
  • Hypoalbuminemia; hypocholesterolemia (liver failure)
  • Azotemia (renal injury from MetHb + hypoxia)

Timeline — Dogs

TimePhaseFindings
2–24 hrInitial phaseAnorexia, vomiting, nausea; GI signs predominate
~24–48 hrAsymptomatic periodMay appear to improve; liver enzymes begin rising
3–5 daysHepatic necrosis phaseALT/AST dramatically elevated; jaundice; liver failure signs
7–8 daysRecovery or deathSurvivors begin to improve; untreated → progressive liver failure

Cats: MetHb signs within hours — may not reach the "asymptomatic period" seen in dogs.

Diagnosis

  • History of exposure — most important clue; owner may not realize product contained APAP
  • Clinical signs: facial/paw edema (cats), MetHb (chocolate MM), Heinz body anemia, liver failure (icterus in dogs)
  • Measure acetaminophen in blood and tissues
  • Clinical pathology: MetHb %, Heinz bodies on blood smear, ALT ↑↑, AST ↑↑

Histopathology

  • Centrilobular to midzonal hepatic necrosis — Zone 3 most affected (highest CYP450 2E1 activity)
  • Edema of the portal connective tissue
  • Dilated lymphatics
  • Hemorrhage within necrotic areas
  • Minimal inflammation in purely toxic injury
🔬
SLIDE 12 — HEPATIC HISTO
Centrilobular necrosis — pale/eosinophilic areas around central veins (Zone 3). Portal triads (Zone 1) at periphery relatively preserved. Sharp demarcation between necrotic Zone 3 and viable Zone 1.
Centrilobular necrosis = Zone 3 = highest CYP450 2E1 activity

Prognosis

  • Good prognosis with early treatment (within 16 hr of ingestion)
  • High mortality with delayed presentation in animals already in liver failure
  • Chronic liver failure can occur as a sequela
  • Always evaluate the animal for underlying disease that prompted the owner to give a pain reliever
💉 Treatment priority: Early decontamination + N-acetylcysteine (NAC) = cornerstone. NAC works best within 16 hr of ingestion. Ascorbic acid addresses MetHb. SAMe supports hepatic glutathione long-term.

Complete Treatment Protocol

StepTreatmentDose / DetailsRationale
1 — Decontamination
(<2 hr post-ingestion)		Emesis + Activated charcoalAC: 2 g/kg PO with saline or osmotic cathartic. AC does NOT reduce NAC efficacy (give AC first).Reduce further absorption. Limit total APAP load → less NAPQI generated.
2 — NAC
(most critical)		N-acetylcysteineLoading dose: 140 mg/kg PO, then 70 mg/kg PO q6h × 7 additional doses. Most effective if given within 16 hr. IV formulation available.Provides cysteine → replenishes hepatic glutathione → NAPQI neutralized. Also direct free radical scavenger.
3 — Ascorbic acidVitamin C20 mg/kg PO or parenteral, up to QIDReduces methemoglobin back to oxyhemoglobin (Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺). Especially important in cats.
4 — Supportive careIV fluids + Blood transfusionAs needed based on clinical status, PCV, TPMaintain perfusion; address anemia from hemolysis
5 — SAMeS-Adenosyl methionine18 mg/kg PO for 1–3 monthsHepatoprotective; supports hepatic glutathione long-term
6 — MonitoringLiver enzymes at 24 and 48 hrALT, AST, bilirubin, albumin, PT/aPTTAssess severity; guide further treatment decisions
🔗 Why cimetidine does NOT help: Cimetidine + NAC showed NO increased benefit over NAC alone in clinical studies. Do not add cimetidine to the protocol. Use NAC + ascorbic acid + SAMe instead.
🍄 Aflatoxins: Mycotoxins produced by Aspergillus flavus and A. parasiticus contaminating stored grains. Aflatoxin B1 = most potent natural hepatocarcinogen known. Affects all mammals. Dogs and pigs most susceptible. Chronic exposure → cirrhosis + hepatocellular carcinoma.

Aflatoxin Sources & Types

Producing Organisms

SpeciesDistributionTypes produced
A. flavusGlobalB1, B2 only
A. parasiticusAfrica & AmericasB1, B2, G1, G2
