~17 fungal metabolite types total. B1 is most common and most toxic.

Common contaminated feedstuffs

  • Cereals: corn (primary), rice, wheat
  • Nuts: peanuts, almonds, walnuts, coconut
  • Spices: paprika, black pepper, coriander, ginger
  • Animal products: milk, cheese, meat, eggs (carry-over)
⚠️ Food recalls are common. FDA monitors and enforces action levels.

Pre- & Post-harvest Risk Factors

Pre-harvest

  • High temperatures; chronic drought stress
  • Heavy rains; crop insect damage (entry points)
  • Weed competition; high crop densities
  • High soil organic content and moisture

Post-harvest

  • High temperatures during storage
  • High humidity in grain storage
  • Delayed crop drying after harvest

Aflatoxin B1 Metabolism & Mechanism

Aflatoxin B1
ingested
→
CYP450
hepatic bioactivation
→
Aflatoxin epoxide
Reactive electrophile
→
+ DNA
N7-guanine adduct
(mutations → cancer)
OR
+ Protein
Lysine adduct
(cell dysfunction)

Key fact: Only 1–3% of ingested aflatoxins irreversibly bind to proteins and DNA bases to form adducts — but even this small fraction causes significant hepatotoxic, mutagenic, and carcinogenic effects with repeated exposure.

Carcinogenesis

  • Positive carcinogenicity studies in: rats, mice, hamsters, salmon, trout, ducks, tree shrews, woodchucks, monkeys
  • Human epidemiologic studies: significant cause of HCC
  • May cause 4.6–28.2% of all global HCC cases
  • Hepatitis B virus infection is a synergistic risk factor — HBV + aflatoxin dramatically increases HCC risk vs. either alone
  • Mechanism: aflatoxin epoxide creates G→T transversions at codon 249 of the TP53 tumor suppressor gene → specific p53 mutation associated with aflatoxin-related HCC
⚠️ Aflatoxin B1 = most potent natural hepatocarcinogen known. The HBV + aflatoxin synergy is especially high-yield. Aflatoxin IS NOT a "just mold on corn" problem — it is a global liver cancer risk factor.

Clinical Signs in Mammals

Early / Subclinical Signs

  • Decreased feed efficiency and growth rate
  • Reduced feed intake
  • Rough, dull hair coat
  • Increased prevalence of infectious disease — aflatoxin is immunosuppressive (↓ T-cell function, ↓ macrophage activity)

Acute High-Dose Signs

  • "Sudden death" (massive hepatic necrosis)
  • Vomiting and diarrhea
  • Jaundice + ascites (liver failure)
  • Hemorrhage (coagulopathy from ↓ clotting factor synthesis)

Pathology

Exposure typeGross liver findingsHistologic findings
Acute / high-doseEnlarged, pale, yellow-orange (necrosis + lipidosis). Hemorrhage.Periportal (Zone 1) necrosis primarily — aflatoxin enters via portal blood. Hepatocyte swelling + lipid vacuolation.
Chronic / low-doseFirm, nodular, tawny yellow. Small and irregular capsule (cirrhosis). ± Neoplastic nodules (HCC).Megalocytosis (large hepatocytes with giant nuclei — DNA crosslinking prevents mitosis), biliary hyperplasia, fibrosis, cirrhosis → HCC
⚠️ Aflatoxin causes PERIPORTAL (Zone 1) necrosis — different from most hepatotoxins (Zone 3/centrilobular). Reason: aflatoxin enters via portal blood → Zone 1 sees highest concentration before detoxification. Compare: APAP = Zone 3. Aflatoxin = Zone 1. This is an important histologic distinction.

Diagnosis of Aflatoxicosis

MethodDetailsLimitations
History of exposureRecall information, batch number of feed, lot contaminationMost important starting point
Black light (UV) screening of feedUV fluorescence detects kojic acid (fungal metabolite, not aflatoxin itself)Many false positives (kojic acid ≠ aflatoxin) but few false negatives — good rapid screen, poor confirmation
Confirmatory lab analysisELISA (screening) + GC-MS or HPLC-MS (confirmation)Gold standard; quantifies specific aflatoxin types and levels

FDA Action Levels for Aflatoxins in Animal Feed

LevelFeed type / intended animal
20 ppbCorn and other grains for immature animals (including immature poultry) and dairy animals, or when destination unknown
20 ppbAnimal feeds other than corn or cottonseed meal (general feed)
100 ppbCorn and grains for breeding beef cattle, breeding swine, or mature poultry
200 ppbCorn and grains for finishing swine ≥100 lbs
300 ppbCorn and grains for finishing (feedlot) beef cattle + cottonseed meal for beef, swine, or poultry
🔗 Milk carryover: Aflatoxin B1 in feed → metabolized to Aflatoxin M1 in ruminants → excreted in MILK. FDA action level for aflatoxin M1 in milk = 0.5 ppb. This is why dairy animals have the strictest feed limits (20 ppb) — to protect human milk consumers.
⚡ 6 clinical vignettes — APAP mechanism, APAP cats vs dogs, NAC treatment, aflatoxin sources, aflatoxin mechanism, regulatory limits. Click to flip.
VIGNETTE · APAP · CAT
A cat presents 3 hours after its owner gave it "half a Tylenol" for apparent pain. Clinical signs: chocolate-brown mucous membranes, tachycardia, weakness, swollen muzzle. Why is this cat so severely affected, and what is the immediate treatment?
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
Acetaminophen toxicosis — cats are severely UGT-deficient. Cats lack adequate UGT1A6/1A9 enzymes → cannot glucuronidate APAP → more drug shunted to CYP450 → NAPQI → rapidly depletes glutathione → NAPQI oxidizes hemoglobin → MetHb (chocolate MM). Facial edema = characteristic. Treatment: (1) Emesis if <2 hr; (2) Activated charcoal 2 g/kg; (3) N-acetylcysteine 140 mg/kg loading dose then 70 mg/kg q6h × 7 doses; (4) Ascorbic acid 20 mg/kg QID → reduces MetHb back to OxyHb. Most effective within 16 hr.
VIGNETTE · APAP · DOG
A dog presents 2 days after ingesting ~300 mg/kg of acetaminophen. Now: profoundly icteric, vomiting, weak. Labs: ALT 3400 U/L, AST 2800 U/L, bilirubin ↑↑↑, PT prolonged. No facial edema. Histology: centrilobular necrosis. Why are these signs different from a cat, and what zone and why?
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
APAP hepatotoxicity in dogs — liver failure dominant. Dogs have adequate glucuronidation → MetHb is less prominent. At overdose doses, NAPQI overwhelms glutathione → covalent protein binding → Zone 3 (centrilobular) hepatic necrosis. Zone 3 = highest CYP450 2E1 activity + lowest O₂ tension. Liver failure signs at 24–36 hr: icterus, coagulopathy (PT prolonged), hypoalbuminemia. NAC still indicated + fluids + liver monitoring at 24 and 48 hr. Prognosis poor at this stage — delayed presentation with liver failure = high mortality.
VIGNETTE · NAC MECHANISM
Why does N-acetylcysteine work for APAP toxicosis? What is the loading dose, what is the maintenance dose, and why does giving activated charcoal first NOT reduce NAC efficacy?
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
NAC = glutathione precursor. NAC provides cysteine → used by hepatocytes to synthesize glutathione → glutathione neutralizes NAPQI → prevents covalent binding to critical cell proteins. Also direct free radical scavenger. Loading dose: 140 mg/kg PO (or IV). Maintenance: 70 mg/kg PO q6h × 7 doses. AC does NOT reduce NAC efficacy because: (1) AC binds GI tract contents; (2) NAC is given after AC is largely cleared; (3) NAC acts within hepatocytes — a separate mechanism from GI absorption.
VIGNETTE · AFLATOXIN · SOURCES
A kennel purchases a corn-based dog food. Several young dogs develop decreased appetite, dull haircoats, and elevated liver enzymes over 3 weeks. The food tests positive on black light screening. What are the two producing organisms, which aflatoxin type is most toxic, and why is black light not definitive?
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
Aflatoxicosis from contaminated dog food. Organisms: (1) A. flavus = global; produces B-class only (B1, B2). (2) A. parasiticus = Africa + Americas; produces B and G classes (B1, B2, G1, G2). Most toxic = Aflatoxin B1 (most common AND most toxic). Black light: positive screening indicates presence of fungal contamination (kojic acid fluoresces) but has significant false positives — non-toxic fungal metabolites fluoresce too. Confirm with ELISA then GC-MS/HPLC-MS. Remove contaminated feed immediately.
VIGNETTE · AFLATOXIN · MECHANISM
Why is aflatoxin B1 the most potent natural hepatocarcinogen? Describe the metabolic activation and specific DNA lesion it creates. What percentage of ingested aflatoxin forms these lesions, and how does HBV infection alter risk?
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
AFB1 → CYP450 → aflatoxin-8,9-epoxide → reactive electrophile → binds N7 position of guanine in DNA → N7-guanine adduct → G→T transversion mutations in TP53 (codon 249) → loss of tumor suppressor function → HCC. Also forms lysine adducts on proteins → cell dysfunction. Only 1–3% of ingested aflatoxins form adducts — but this 1–3% causes cumulative mutagenic damage. HBV synergy: HBV causes chronic hepatocellular injury/regeneration while aflatoxin provides the mutagenic driver → dramatically elevated HCC risk. AFB1 may cause 4.6–28.2% of global HCC.
VIGNETTE · AFLATOXIN · REGULATION
A feed mill finds corn tests at 150 ppb total aflatoxin. Can this corn be legally fed to: (A) a lactating dairy herd, (B) breeding swine, (C) finishing beef cattle (feedlot)? Give the FDA action levels.
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
A) Dairy: NO. FDA limit = 20 ppb for dairy animals. 150 ppb far exceeds this. Dairy cows are most restricted because AFB1 → metabolized to AFM1 in ruminants → excreted in milk → human exposure. B) Breeding swine: YES. FDA allows up to 100 ppb for breeding swine. 150 ppb > 100 ppb — actually NO, this is too high. Wait — 150 ppb exceeds the 100 ppb limit for breeding swine. Breeding swine = 100 ppb. C) Finishing feedlot beef cattle: YES. FDA allows up to 300 ppb for feedlot finishing beef cattle. 150 ppb < 300 ppb = permissible. Summary: 20 (dairy/immature) < 100 (breeding) < 200 (finishing swine) < 300 (feedlot beef).
🍄 Aflatoxins (Tox 2 continued): CYP450 bioactivation → epoxide intermediate → irreversible DNA/protein adducts → hepatotoxicity, immunosuppression, carcinogenesis. Species susceptibility, dose-dependent outcomes, and regulatory limits reviewed here with additional detail from Tox 2.

Aflatoxin Types — Recap + Additional Detail

Two producing species

SpeciesDistributionTypes produced
A. flavusGlobalB1, B2 only
A. parasiticusAfrica & AmericasB1, B2, G1, G2

Fluorescence under UV: B types fluoresce blue, G types fluoresce green.

Species Susceptibility

SpeciesSusceptibility
Rabbits, ducks, catsVery high
Pigs, dogs, calves, horsesHigh
Cattle, sheep, goatsModerate
Poultry (chickens)Moderate
🔑 Pigs are most susceptible among domestic livestock.

Metabolism Pathway — Detailed

Ingested
Aflatoxin B1
→
CYP450
Hepatic bioactivation
(microsomes)
→
Aflatoxin epoxide
Reactive intermediate
(electrophile)
→
Adduct formation
N7-guanine on DNA
Lysine on proteins
→
Cell injury
Necrosis · Mutagenesis
Carcinogenesis
⚠️ Only 1–3% of ingested aflatoxins irreversibly bind to proteins and DNA — but even this small fraction causes significant cumulative damage with repeated exposure.

Dose-Dependent Outcomes

Exposure typeDurationPrimary outcomeKey lesion
Acute high doseSingle large exposureAcute hepatic necrosis + liver failureCentrilobular to massive hepatocellular necrosis; hemorrhage
Chronic low doseWeeks to monthsMegalocytosis + fibrosis + biliary hyperplasia → cirrhosisGiant hepatocytes (DNA crosslinking prevents normal mitosis); portal fibrosis
Long-term exposureMonths to yearsHepatocellular carcinoma or cholangiocarcinomaAflatoxin B1 = most potent natural hepatocarcinogen known. HBV co-infection amplifies risk.

Early / Subclinical Signs — The Economic Impact

  • Decreased feed efficiency and growth rate
  • Reduced feed intake / anorexia
  • Rough, dull hair or coat
  • Increased prevalence of infectious disease (immunosuppression — ↓ T-cell function, ↓ macrophage activity)
⚠️ Immunosuppression as a sentinel sign: Animals on contaminated feed will show increased susceptibility to respiratory and enteric infections — a herd outbreak of infectious disease on a grain-based diet warrants feed testing.

Diagnosis

MethodDetailsCaveats
History of exposureContaminated grain source; feed lot history; recent diet changeMay be the only initial clue
Clinical signs + pathologyHepatomegaly, icterus, coagulopathy; megalocytosis on histoNot pathognomonic alone
Black light screeningUV fluorescence detects kojic acid (fungal metabolite, not aflatoxin itself)Many false positives but few false negatives — good screen, poor confirmation
Confirmatory lab analysisELISA (screening) + GC-MS or HPLC-MS (confirmation) of feed samplesGold standard; quantifies specific aflatoxin types

FDA Action Levels — Full Table

LevelFeed type / intended use
20 ppbCorn/grains for immature animals, dairy animals, or when destination is unknown
100 ppbCorn/grains for breeding beef cattle, breeding swine, or mature poultry
200 ppbCorn/grains for finishing swine ≥100 lbs
300 ppbCorn/grains for finishing (feedlot) beef cattle + cottonseed meal for beef, swine, or poultry
⚠️ Exam high-yield: Dairy = LOWEST (20 ppb) because AFB1 → AFM1 in ruminants → excreted in milk → human exposure (FDA limit for AFM1 in milk = 0.5 ppb). Feedlot cattle = HIGHEST (300 ppb) — large, mature ruminants, product not entering milk supply.
☀️ Photosensitization: Skin damage from heightened reactivity following sunlight exposure. Most veterinary cases are secondary (hepatogenous) — liver injury prevents detoxification of phylloerythrin, which accumulates in skin and reacts with UV light. Most severe in non-pigmented skin.

Two Types — The Core Distinction

Primary photosensitization

  • Animal ingests a photodynamic agent directly
  • No liver injury required
  • The photosensitizer circulates to the skin intact
  • Less common than secondary form
  • Examples: St. John's wort, buckwheat, phenothiazine

Secondary (hepatogenous) photosensitization

  • Liver injury MUST be present
  • Damaged liver cannot detoxify phylloerythrin
  • Phylloerythrin accumulates in plasma → reaches skin → UV light activation → tissue damage
  • More common than primary form
  • Treating requires treating the underlying hepatic disease

Phylloerythrin — The Key Molecule in Secondary Photosensitization

Dietary chlorophyll
Normal feed ingestion
→
GI bacteria
Metabolize → phylloerythrin
(porphyrin compound)
→
Absorbed
Into portal circulation → liver
→
Healthy liver
Detoxifies → biliary excretion (normal)
Damaged liver
CANNOT excrete phylloerythrin
→
Plasma accumulation
Normal sheep: <0.01 mM/L
Affected: >0.03 mM/L
→
Reaches skin
Especially non-pigmented areas
→
UV excitation
Free radical generation → skin necrosis
🔑 Threshold: Plasma phylloerythrin >0.03 mM/L in sheep is associated with photosensitization. Normal plasma is <0.01 mM/L.
☀️
SLIDE 15 — PHOTOSENSITIZATION IN SHEEP (GROSS)
Sheep with severe facial swelling, crusting, and sloughing of non-pigmented skin (face, ears). Lesions absent on dark-pigmented areas. Classic secondary (hepatogenous) presentation. Seeks shade; head shaking; pain around coronary bands.
Non-pigmented skin only — diagnostic clue
🔬
SLIDE 18 — PHYLLOERYTHRIN STRUCTURE
Phylloerythrin is a porphyrin-type compound derived from chlorophyll breakdown by gut bacteria. Polycyclic ring structure absorbs UV light → excited-state molecule → generates reactive oxygen species and free radicals → oxidative skin damage.
Porphyrin + UV → ROS → skin necrosis

Clinical Signs — Progression

Early signs

  • Erythema (redness of exposed skin)
  • Pruritis (itching)
  • Shaking of head and ears
  • Pain around coronary bands (hooves/horns)
  • Seeks shade — behavioral change from photophobia

Late signs

  • Swelling and edema of ears and other exposed skin surfaces
  • Cracking of skin with serum exudation
  • Secondary bacterial infections (skin barrier loss)
  • Corneal opacity (eye involvement)
  • Genital region involvement → decreased reproductive performance
  • Udder lesions → decreased nursing → reduced rate of gain in nursing animals

Treatment Principles

  • Primary: Remove the photosensitizing plant or drug; move animal out of sunlight; supportive care for skin lesions
  • Secondary: Must address underlying hepatic disease first; remove from sun; treat liver cause (remove toxin source, support liver function)
  • Neither form will resolve if sunlight exposure continues
  • Secondary form will recur if liver disease is not treated — skin treatment alone is insufficient

Plants Associated with Primary Photosensitization

Scientific nameCommon nameKey notes
Hypericum perforatumSt. John's wortMost well-known primary photosensitizer; hypericin = photosensitizing pigment
Polygonium fagopyrumBuckwheatFagopyrin = photosensitizing compound
Ammi majusBishop's weedFurocoumarins; also used in human phototherapy (PUVA)
Cooperia pedunculataRain lilyAffects cattle and sheep in endemic areas
Cymopterus watsoniiDesert parsleyFurocoumarins
Thamnosma texanaDutchman's breechesSouthwest US; sheep and cattle

Plants Associated with Hepatogenous (Secondary) Photosensitization

Scientific nameCommon nameHepatotoxic mechanism
Lantana spp.LantanaTriterpenes (lantadenes) → cholestasis + canalicular damage → impaired biliary phylloerythrin excretion
Agave lecheguillaLecheguillaSaponins → liver and kidney injury; sheep most severely affected
Ageratina altissimaWhite snakerootTremetone → hepatic injury; historically caused "milk sickness" in humans via cattle
Nolina texanaSacahuistaCholestatic liver injury → phylloerythrin accumulation
⚠️ Lantana causes BOTH primary hepatotoxicity (megalocytosis, canalicular cholestasis, focal necrosis) AND secondary photosensitization. When you see a question about photosensitization in cattle grazing ornamental landscaping plants — think Lantana.
🌊 Blue-Green Algae (Cyanobacteria): Not true algae — prokaryotic bacteria that form dense blooms in warm, nutrient-rich, stagnant water. Two major toxin categories: neurotoxins (rapid death — anatoxins, saxitoxin) and hepatotoxins (liver failure — microcystins, cylindrospermopsin, nodularins). Mixed exposures are common.

Cyanobacteria Ecology & Bloom Risk Factors

Biology

  • Present in most waterbodies — fresh, brackish, and marine
  • Can form dense surface blooms (visible green, blue-green, or reddish mats)
  • Considered HABs (Harmful Algal Blooms) — major animal and public health issue

Risk factors for bloom formation

  • Increasing water temperatures (warming climate = more blooms)
  • High nutrient concentrations (eutrophication — agricultural runoff)
  • Drought — less water volume = higher nutrient concentration
  • Low water flow / stagnant conditions

Toxin Categories — The Two-Branch Framework

Neurotoxins → Neurological signs

  • Anatoxin-a — nicotinic AChR agonist
  • Anatoxin-a(s) — acetylcholinesterase inhibitor
  • Saxitoxin — sodium channel blocker
  • BMAA (β-methylamino-L-alanine)

Onset: RAPID — convulsions, tremors, paralysis, respiratory arrest. Sudden death common.

Hepatotoxins → Liver failure

  • Microcystin — most important, most studied
  • Cylindrospermopsin
  • Nodularins

Onset: hours to days — liver failure, hemorrhage, hepatomegaly.

🌊 Mixed exposure: A single bloom event commonly produces both neurotoxins and hepatotoxins. Animals may die rapidly (neurotoxins) OR develop liver failure over hours to days (hepatotoxins) OR show both simultaneously.
🌊
SLIDE 27 — CYANOBACTERIAL BLOOM (GROSS)
Dense blue-green surface bloom on a lake — paint-like mat on water surface. Dogs that swim in or drink from bloom-affected water are at high risk. The bloom concentrates downwind and at water margins.
Green "paint" on water = potential bloom

Cyanobacterial Neurotoxins — Four Key Agents

ToxinMechanismClinical result
Anatoxin-aNicotinic acetylcholine receptor agonist — binds nAChR but cannot be hydrolyzed by AChE → persistent depolarizationMuscle tremors, rigidity, cyanosis, convulsions, respiratory arrest, death
Anatoxin-a(s)Organophosphate-like — inhibits acetylcholinesterase → ACh accumulates at synapsesSLUD signs (salivation, lacrimation, urination, defecation) + seizures; slower onset than anatoxin-a
SaxitoxinBlocks voltage-gated sodium channels → prevents action potential propagationParalytic shellfish poisoning equivalent; rapid paralysis, respiratory arrest
BMAAGlutamate receptor agonist; may be misincorporated into proteinsChronic neurodegeneration in long-term exposure; possible link to ALS-like disease
⚠️ Anatoxin-a vs Anatoxin-a(s): Anatoxin-a is a receptor agonist (mimics ACh at nicotinic receptor), while anatoxin-a(s) is an enzyme inhibitor (like an organophosphate). Atropine (muscarinic blocker) can partially treat anticholinesterase effects of anatoxin-a(s) but will NOT treat anatoxin-a poisoning (nicotinic receptor agonism). This distinction is clinically and exam-relevant.

Microcystins — Mechanism & Pathophysiology

  • Released when Microcystis cells die (lysis) — dead algae cells = more toxin released
  • Ingestion of water or algal cells; adverse effects in fish, dogs, cats, livestock, and humans
  • Mechanism: inhibit cytoplasmic protein phosphatases (PP1 and PP2A) → disrupts hepatocyte cytoskeleton → cell rounding, dissociation, and death
  • Hepatocytes are the primary target (active uptake via organic anion transporters)
🔑 MOA distinction: Microcystins inhibit protein phosphatase (unlike aflatoxin which uses CYP450 epoxide, or Amanita which inhibits RNA polymerase II). Mechanism = phosphorylation cascade dysregulation → cytoskeletal collapse.

Clinical Signs

  • Sudden death within hours to days
  • Watery or bloody diarrhea
  • Death may be preceded by: coma, muscle tremors, paddling, dyspnea

Clinical Pathology

  • Rapid rise (15–30 min) in serum bile acids, ALP, GGT, AST
  • Increased WBC count (neutrophilia)
  • Prolonged clotting times (coagulopathy)

Gross & Histopathology

Gross pathology

  • Hepatomegaly (enlarged liver)
  • Intrahepatic hemorrhage — dark red-black liver
  • Cyanobacteria visible in stomach and GI tract (blue-green ingesta)
  • Greenish (algal) stains on mouth, nose, legs, and feet

Histopathology

  • Centrilobular hepatic necrosis (early) → periportal necrosis (later, severe)
  • Hepatocytes are dissociated and rounded (cytoskeletal collapse) — hallmark
  • Eosinophilic cellular and karyorrhectic debris in necrotic zones
  • Hepatocyte debris found in pulmonary vessels and kidneys (emboli)
⚠️ Centrilobular → periportal progression: Microcystin necrosis begins in Zone 3 and with massive exposure progresses to Zone 1. Unlike most hepatotoxins that stay centrilobular. This zonal progression may be tested on exams. Hallmark histo = rounded, dissociated hepatocytes from cytoskeletal collapse.

Treatment of Cyanobacterial Toxicosis

Immediate decontamination

  • Remove animal from contaminated water immediately
  • Dermal decontamination — bathe skin thoroughly to remove cyanobacteria
  • Gastrointestinal decontamination: activated charcoal or cholestyramine

Specific / experimental treatments

  • Bile acid transport blockers (inhibit hepatocyte uptake of microcystin)
  • Pre-treatment with cyclosporin A, rifampin, or silymarin (milk thistle) — experimental models only
  • Atropine: for muscarinic effects of anatoxin-a(s) ONLY — does NOT work for anatoxin-a

Prevention Strategies

StrategyDetails
Restrict water accessPrevent animals from accessing affected waterways; fence off ponds/lakes during bloom season
Copper-based algicidal treatmentsCopper sulfate kills cyanobacteria; rapid cell death releases stored toxins — toxin levels can temporarily increase immediately after treatment
Phosphorus removalLime, aluminum sulfate, ferric chloride, clay particles — bind and precipitate phosphorus from eutrophic waters
Monitoring programsState/local HAB monitoring; public warning signs; water testing during warm months
⚠️ Copper algicidal treatment caveat: Adding copper-based algicide kills cyanobacterial cells rapidly → mass cell lysis → release of intracellular toxins into the water. Toxin levels can temporarily increase immediately after treatment. Animals should be kept away from treated water for several days after algicide application.
⚡ 6 clinical vignettes — aflatoxins, photosensitization, and blue-green algae. Click to flip.
VIGNETTE · AFLATOXIN · DOGS
A kennel of 15 dogs develops jaundice, bloody diarrhea, and hemorrhage over 3 days. Three die acutely. Labs: markedly elevated ALT and bilirubin, prolonged PT, leukopenia. A new batch of commercial dry food was introduced 2 weeks ago. What is the diagnosis and what lab test confirms it?
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
Aflatoxicosis from contaminated commercial dog food. Dogs are highly susceptible. Aflatoxin B1 → CYP450 → epoxide → hepatocellular necrosis + coagulopathy (clotting factor synthesis failure). Immunosuppression (leukopenia) is an early and characteristic feature. Confirmation: ELISA screen of food → GC-MS or HPLC-MS for definitive aflatoxin quantification. Black-light screening of the food has many false positives but few false negatives. Recover unsold food from the same lot number. Check FDA recall database.
VIGNETTE · AFLATOXIN · REGULATORY
A feed mill tests a batch of corn and finds 85 ppb total aflatoxin. Which of the following animals can legally receive this corn as feed? (A) Lactating dairy cows, (B) Breeding swine, (C) Nursing piglets, (D) Feedlot finishing beef cattle
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
(D) Feedlot finishing beef cattle only. FDA action levels: Dairy animals and immature animals (including nursing piglets) = 20 ppb → A and C excluded. Breeding swine = 100 ppb → 85 ppb IS within 100 ppb limit, so B is technically permissible too. Feedlot finishing beef cattle = 300 ppb → easily within limit. D = YES. B = YES. A and C = NO. Summary: 20 (dairy/immature) < 100 (breeding) < 200 (finishing swine) < 300 (feedlot beef). Lower action level = more sensitive/vulnerable population.
VIGNETTE · PHOTOSENSITIZATION
A 4yo Hereford cow develops swelling and ulceration of white-haired skin on her face, muzzle, and udder but dark-pigmented areas are unaffected. She is seeking shade and head-shaking. GGT is markedly elevated. What type of photosensitization is this and what is the key pathophysiologic molecule?
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
Secondary (hepatogenous) photosensitization. GGT elevation = hepatic/biliary injury. The damaged liver cannot conjugate and excrete phylloerythrin (a porphyrin produced from chlorophyll by gut bacteria). Phylloerythrin accumulates in plasma → distributes to skin → UV light excites it → free radical generation → oxidative skin necrosis. Only non-pigmented skin is affected because melanin blocks UV penetration. Normal plasma phylloerythrin <0.01 mM/L; affected animals >0.03 mM/L. Must treat underlying liver disease — skin treatment alone insufficient.
VIGNETTE · BLUE-GREEN ALGAE · NEUROTOXIN
A 2yo Labrador swam in a lake with a thick green surface bloom. Within 20 minutes he developed muscle tremors, rigidity, salivation, and respiratory distress and died before reaching the veterinary clinic. What toxin is most likely responsible and what is its mechanism?
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
Anatoxin-a (most likely given rapid onset and neuromuscular signs). Mechanism: nicotinic acetylcholine receptor (nAChR) agonist — mimics ACh but cannot be hydrolyzed by AChE → persistent receptor depolarization → neuromuscular paralysis → respiratory arrest. Onset in minutes. Key distinction: atropine helps for anatoxin-a(s) (AChE inhibitor — muscarinic SLUD signs) but NOT for anatoxin-a (nicotinic agonist — atropine does nothing at nicotinic receptors). Both are neurotoxins from cyanobacteria, not the hepatotoxic microcystin.
VIGNETTE · MICROCYSTIN · PATHOLOGY
Necropsy of a cow found dead near a bloom-affected pond: enlarged dark hemorrhagic liver, green-stained ingesta in stomach, rounded and dissociated hepatocytes on histo with centrilobular necrosis. What toxin, what mechanism, and what is the unique histologic finding that distinguishes this from other hepatotoxins?
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
Microcystin hepatotoxicosis. Mechanism: inhibition of protein phosphatases PP1 and PP2A → disruption of hepatocyte cytoskeleton → cell rounding and dissociation. This cytoskeletal collapse is the hallmark — hepatocytes become rounded and dissociated from each other, unlike most hepatotoxins where cells undergo typical coagulative necrosis in place. Green stain in stomach = algal cells ingested. Centrilobular necrosis → may progress periportally with massive exposure. Hepatocyte debris emboli can be found in pulmonary vessels and kidneys.
VIGNETTE · MECHANISM COMPARE
Match the hepatotoxin to its specific mechanism: (1) Aflatoxin B1, (2) Microcystin-LR, (3) α-Amanitin (Amanita mushroom). Mechanisms: (A) inhibits RNA polymerase II, (B) inhibits protein phosphatase PP1/PP2A, (C) CYP450 → epoxide → N7-guanine DNA adduct.
↻ Click to flip
ANSWER
1→C, 2→B, 3→A. Aflatoxin B1: CYP450 bioactivation → aflatoxin epoxide → irreversible N7-guanine DNA adducts + lysine protein adducts → genotoxicity + hepatocyte death. Microcystin-LR: inhibits cytoplasmic protein phosphatases PP1 and PP2A → cytoskeletal disruption → hepatocyte rounding and dissociation. α-Amanitin (Amanita phalloides): inhibits RNA polymerase II → blocks mRNA synthesis → protein synthesis failure → cell death. All three cause hepatic necrosis but via completely different targets. High-yield mechanism triad.
Reproductive Toxicology
🌿 Reproductive Toxicology: Teratogenesis principles · Veratrum californicum (cyclopamine, sheep) · Tall Fescue / Endophyte (ergovaline, horses & cattle). Covers critical windows, mechanisms, clinical syndromes, and management.

🧬 Teratogenesis — Principles

TermDefinition
TeratogenAgent or factor that causes malformation of an embryo
TeratogenesisProcess by which congenital malformations are produced in an embryo or fetus
OrganogenesisPeriod when organs develop — most sensitive window for structural defects

Gestational Timing → Outcome

Exposure TimingLikely Outcome
Pre-implantationEmbryo loss / abortion (all-or-nothing effect)
OrganogenesisStructural malformations (teratogenic responses)
Fetal periodGrowth restriction, functional deficits, weight loss

Teratogenic Plants — Summary Table

PlantSpecies AffectedPrimary Teratogenic Effect
Veratrum spp.Sheep, cattleCyclopia, arthrogryposis
Nicotiana spp.Swine, cattleArthrogryposis, torticollis
Conium spp.Swine, cattleArthrogryposis, cleft palate
Lupinus spp.Cattle, sheepArthrogryposis, torticollis
Astragalus spp.Herbivores, sheepArthrogryposis, contracted tendons
Sorghum spp.HerbivoresAnkylosis, contracted tendons
Lathyrus spp.HerbivoresSkeletal deformities

🌿 Veratrum californicum (Corn Lily / False Hellebore)

Key Facts

FeatureDetail
Primary species at riskSheep (most studied); cattle, llamas, alpacas rarely eat it; horses rarely affected
Risk conditionEarly spring grazing — plant is palatable before grasses emerge
Toxic syndrome (adults)Hypotension, bradycardia (affects humans and ruminants)
Mechanism (teratogenesis)Alkaloids (cyclopamine/jervine) inhibit Sonic Hedgehog (SHH) signaling pathway → disrupts midline craniofacial development
When malformations appearOften observed in early Autumn (lambs born with defects after spring exposure)

⚠️ Critical Windows of Susceptibility — Ewes (HIGH-YIELD)

Gestational DaysEffect
0–10 daysNo obvious structural defect — may cause loss of implantation / early abortion
12–16 daysCyclopian-type malformations (single midline eye, proboscis)
12–34 daysMotor nerve paralysis
24–30 daysCleft palate
28–33 daysHypoplasia of metacarpals and metatarsals
🧠 Mnemonic: "12–16 → Cyclops" — the ewe grazes Veratrum at day 12–16 of gestation and delivers a cyclopian lamb in the fall.

🌾 Tall Fescue Toxicosis

Background

FeatureDetail
GrassLolium arundinaceum (= Festuca arundinacea) — cool-season perennial; ~35 million acres in US
EndophyteEpichloë coenophiala (= Neotyphodium coenophialum) — intercellular, symbiotic; improves drought/insect resistance
Primary toxicantErgovaline — accounts for 84–97% of ergopeptine content of infected fescue
Other alkaloidsErgotamine, ergosine, indole alkaloids; peramines and lolines (nontoxic)
Toxic thresholdErgovaline >200 ppb toxic for cattle; total ergopeptines >200 ppb toxic to mares
Risk factorsSpring endophyte proliferation; drought stress; nitrogen fertilization; high ambient temperatures

Mechanism of Action

PathwayEffect
Vasoconstriction (α-adrenergic agonism)Peripheral vasoconstriction → reduced blood flow → fescue foot, thickened placentas
Dopamine D2 receptor agonismSuppresses prolactin → agalactia, delayed parturition, reproductive failure
Rumen metabolismCattle may be more resistant than horses due to ruminal detoxification

🐴 Horses — Clinical Signs & Critical Window

FeatureDetail
Critical exposure windowLast 30 days of gestation (gestation day ~300+); removal before day 300 → normal foaling
Delayed parturitionProlonged by 20–27 days; higher incidence of dystocia and uterine trauma
Placental changesThickened, edematous; hyperemic/hemorrhagic chorioallantois; retained placenta
AgalactiaDecreased / absent colostrum → immune compromise in foals
Dysmature foalsWeak or stillborn; long/lanky, prominent skeleton, fine hair coat, overgrown hooves, irregular incisors, may be hypothyroid
TreatmentRemove from fescue ASAP; domperidone (1.1 mg/kg PO q12–24h) — dopamine D2 antagonist; alternative colostrum/plasma for foals

🐄 Ruminants — Clinical Syndromes

SyndromeSeasonKey Features
Summer Syndrome ("Summer Slump")Warm months (any season)Most common; reduced weight gain, decreased milk, poor appetite, retained winter coat, fever, ± diarrhea, low prolactin; animals seek shade/water; signs within 1–2 wk
Fescue FootFall/winterPeripheral vasoconstriction → lameness; pastured animals or fescue hay
Fat NecrosisAnyLipomatous masses in omentum/mesentery; can cause anorexia, CNS depression, uremia, hydroureter, hydronephrosis, dystocia
Reproductive effectsAnyReduced LH, prolactin, progesterone; decreased pregnancy rates; decreased placental and birth weight

Diagnosis & Management

StepDetail
Serum hormonesLow prolactin concentration supports diagnosis
Pasture testingMultiple samples required; evaluate for endophyte presence or measure ergovaline; also measurable in urine
Management optionsSwitch to endophyte-free fescue; diversify pasture with other forages; remove livestock after seed heads appear
Nontoxic endophytesEndophyte-positive fescue producing peramines and lolines but NOT ergot alkaloids — nontoxic for cattle, sheep, and horses
All 18 cases from lectures 1–6. Click a sub-tab to filter by system, then click any case to expand. Try to interpret the data before revealing the answer.
Liver Cases (1–6)
🐴 Case 1 — 9-year-old Arabian mare
Acute hepatic disease · SDH 12×, GGT 40×, bilirubin 10.8, BUN ↓, albumin ↓
▼
AnalyteResultRI
BUN57–25
Albumin2.72.8–3.5
Bilirubin10.80.4–2.5
SDH168.56.3–13.8 (12×)
ALP62566–181 (3.5×)
AST628202–338 (2×)
GGT7554–19 (40×!)
CK149117–564 (normal)
Pattern: Hepatocellular damage + cholestasis + early functional loss

SDH 12× = massive hepatocellular damage (liver-specific in horses — not AST which is non-specific). GGT 40× = severe cholestasis (preferred cholestasis marker in large animals). CK normal = not muscle disease. BUN ↓, albumin borderline ↓ = beginning to lose functional hepatic mass. Bilirubin 10.8 reflects both cholestasis and anorexia-related UCB rise in this species. Most likely: severe hepatic disease (hepatitis, toxic liver disease, or cholangiohepatitis).

🐕 Case 2 — Maximillion, 3yo MC Labrador
Xylitol ingestion · ALT 9×, ALP normal, GGT normal
▼
AnalyteResultRI
ALT~90010–100 (9×)
ALPNormal20–150
GGTNormal2–10
BilirubinNormal0.1–0.4
Category 1: Isolated hepatocellular damage

ALT 9× URL with completely normal ALP and GGT = pure hepatocellular leakage without cholestasis. No induction of biliary enzymes. Xylitol causes direct hepatotoxicity in dogs (also triggers massive insulin release → hypoglycemia — check glucose urgently). The pattern (leakage enzyme up, induction enzymes normal) is the hallmark of Category 1 hepatocellular damage.

🐱 Case 3 — Galileo, 8yo MC DSH cat
Obese, anorexic 5 days, icteric · ALP 5.5×, GGT normal — reverse ratio
▼
AnalyteResultRI
ALP~5606–102 (5.5×)
GGTNormal / minimally ↑1–10
BilirubinElevated<0.4
ALTMildly ↑10–100
Diagnosis: Hepatic lipidosis — the ALP:GGT reverse ratio

ALP markedly elevated + GGT normal = hallmark of feline hepatic lipidosis. Obese cat + anorexia → fat mobilisation → hepatocyte triglyceride engorgement → intrahepatic cholestasis → ALP induction. If GGT were elevated more than ALP, think cholangitis/cholangiohepatitis instead (the "reverse-reverse" ratio). Treatment: aggressive nutritional support via feeding tube — do NOT allow continued anorexia. Icterus confirms cholestasis is clinically significant.

🐕 Case 4 — Artie, 5yo MC Bichon
Acute abdomen · ALP 47×, GGT 48×, Lipase 9×, thrombocytopenic, anemic
▼
AnalyteResultRI
ALP~7,05020–150 (47×)
GGT~4802–10 (48×)
ALTElevated10–100
Lipase9× URL—
BilirubinElevated<0.4
PlateletsThrombocytopenic200–400K
PCVAnemic37–55%
Primary: Pancreatitis → extrahepatic cholestasis + secondary hepatocellular damage + DIC

Lipase 9× + acute abdomen = pancreatitis. Inflamed pancreatic head compresses common bile duct → extrahepatic cholestasis → ALP 47×, GGT 48× (massive biliary enzyme induction). ALT ↑ = secondary hepatocellular damage from bile acid detergent effect + ischaemia. Thrombocytopenia + anaemia = DIC — pancreatitis is a major DIC trigger (systemic coagulation cascade activation). Check PT, aPTT, D-dimers, fibrinogen urgently.

🐕 Case 5 — Tiny, 6mo teacup Yorkie
Ataxia, head pressing · Ammonia 45 µmol/L (RI 10–30) · Not icteric
▼
AnalyteResultRI
Ammonia45 µmol/L10–30
ALT / ALPMinimal elevation—
BilirubinNormal—
AlbuminLow-normal2.6–3.8

Which is NOT a possible diagnosis? A. PSS  B. Hepatic encephalopathy  C. Liver failure  D. Disc disease

NOT possible: D — Disc disease

Disc disease = spinal cord compression = neurologic signs WITHOUT hyperammonaemia. Young Yorkie + hyperammonaemia + minimal liver enzymes + not icteric = congenital portosystemic shunt (PSS). Portal blood bypasses liver → ammonia not cleared → encephalopathy. Enzymes near-normal because hepatocytes are atrophied (underperfused), not damaged. Hallmark labs: bile acids markedly elevated postprandially, ammonium biurate crystals in urine. Confirm with Doppler US or portography.

🐕 Case 6 — Westie, 15yo MC · Head pressing, ascites, icteric
Albumin 1.3 ↓↓ · BUN 3 ↓ · Glucose 60 ↓ · Bilirubin 15 ↑↑↑
▼
AnalyteResultRI
Albumin1.3 g/dL2.6–3.8
BUN3 mg/dL7–25
Glucose60 mg/dL70–120
Bilirubin15 mg/dL0.1–0.4
CholesterolNormal—
Pattern: End-stage liver disease — functional failure + cholestasis

Functional mass loss (>70% hepatocytes gone): albumin ↓↓ (liver is sole source), BUN ↓ (cannot convert NH₃ to urea), glucose ↓ (no gluconeogenesis or glycogen storage). Cholestasis: bilirubin 15. Why is cholesterol paradoxically NORMAL? Synthesis ↓ (lowers it) + cholestasis (raises it) cancel out exactly. Head pressing + ascites = hepatic encephalopathy (NH₃ accumulation) + portal hypertension combined with hypoalbuminaemia driving fluid into abdomen.

GI & Exocrine Pancreas Cases (7–8)
🐕 Case 7 — Caesar, 3yo MC Golden Retriever
Vomiting · Glucose 546 ↑↑↑ · AMS 3×, LPS 4× · Calcium ↓ · Bicarb ↓
▼
AnalyteResultRI
Glucose546 mg/dL70–120
Amylase3.1× URL—
Lipase4× URL—
ALPElevated20–150
ALTElevated10–100
BilirubinElevated<0.4
CalciumLOW ↓8.8–11.0
BicarbonateLOW ↓18–26
Acute severe pancreatitis with concurrent diabetes mellitus

AMS 3×, LPS 4× = pancreatitis. Glucose 546 = concurrent DM — NOT stress hyperglycaemia (rarely exceeds 300 mg/dL in dogs; confirm with glucosuria on UA). Pancreatitis → islet beta-cell destruction → permanent insulin deficiency. Hypocalcaemia = fat saponification (lipase releases FFAs → bind calcium in peripancreatic fat). ALP + bilirubin ↑ = bile duct compression → extrahepatic cholestasis. Bicarb ↓ = metabolic acidosis. This patient needs insulin therapy alongside pancreatitis management.

🐕 Case 8 — Tapioca, 9yo FS mixed breed
Vomiting, PU/PD · BUN 125 ↑↑ · Cr 4.8 ↑↑ · AMS 1.2×, LPS 2.2× · USG 1.008
▼
AnalyteResultRI
BUN125 mg/dL7–25
Creatinine4.8 mg/dL0.5–1.5
Amylase1.2× URL—
Lipase2.2× URL—
USG1.008>1.030
CKD with AMS/LPS elevated from ↓ renal clearance — NOT primary pancreatitis

BUN 125, Cr 4.8, USG 1.008 = renal azotaemia (tubules unable to concentrate). AMS/LPS 1.2–2.2× = mild elevation fully explained by ↓ GFR — these enzymes are renally cleared. Without CKD, AMS/LPS would still be elevated from reduced clearance alone. Correct test in this patient: cPLI — pancreas-specific, not affected by GFR. Key principle: always assess renal function before interpreting amylase and lipase.

Muscle Cases (9–11)
🐕 Case 9 — 8yo FS Boxer
Meningioma + cluster seizures last night · AST 430 ↑↑ · CK 5750 ↑↑ · ALT mildly ↑
▼
AnalyteResultRI
CK5,750 IU/L10–200 (~29×)
AST430 IU/L10–55 (~8×)
ALTMildly ↑10–100
ALPNormal20–150
AST elevation: skeletal muscle damage from seizures, confirmed by CK ↑↑

CK + AST both markedly elevated = current muscle damage. Seizures → intense skeletal muscle contractions → myofibre disruption → CK and AST leak. ALT only mildly elevated, ALP normal = liver is NOT the primary source. If this were primary hepatocellular disease, ALT would dominate and CK would be normal. CK half-life 2–4 hr; AST 7–8 days — CK peaking confirms damage is acute and ongoing. Cause: meningioma → seizures → muscle damage. Do not misattribute to liver disease.

🐴 Case 10 — 2yo SB gelding
Shipped to event, sweating, stiff hindquarters, dark urine · CK 78,050 (~123×) · AST 1500
▼
AnalyteResultRI
CK78,050 IU/L100–400 (~123×)
AST1,500 IU/L200–500 (3×)
UrineDark brown/redYellow
Exertional rhabdomyolysis ("tying up")

CK 123× URL = catastrophic acute skeletal muscle destruction. Young Thoroughbred + shipping stress + exertion + sweating + hindquarter stiffness = classic presentation. Dark urine = myoglobinuria — myoglobin released from destroyed myofibres is freely filtered by kidneys. Critical risk: myoglobin is nephrotoxic → acute tubular necrosis → AKI. Monitor urine output and creatinine closely. Treatment: aggressive IV fluid diuresis to flush kidneys, rest, analgesia. Investigate cause: PSSM, RER syndrome, electrolyte imbalance, selenium deficiency.

🐷 Case 11 — 2yo FS pig
Dog attack, extensive bite wounds · CK 486,400 (~760×) · AST 1258 · Inflammatory leukogram
▼
AnalyteResultRI
CK486,400 IU/L~640 (~760×)
AST1,258 IU/L~45–80
WBCMarked leukocytosis—
Catastrophic skeletal muscle destruction from trauma + inflammatory leukogram

CK 760× URL from massive myofibre disruption by bite injury. Inflammatory leukogram (neutrophilia ± left shift ± toxic change) reflects wound infection and systemic inflammatory response. Same myoglobinuria/AKI risk as Case 10 — monitor urine colour, BUN, creatinine closely. Treatment: debridement, IV antibiotics, aggressive IV fluid diuresis, analgesia. Myoglobinuria → acute tubular necrosis if not flushed promptly.

Ca / P / Mg Cases (12–14)
🐕 Case 12 — 1yo Yorkie
Ate 9 Vitamin D capsules (1,000 IU each) · Ca >16 ↑↑ · P 4.7 ↑ · Both elevated
▼
AnalyteResultRI
Calcium (total)>16 mg/dL8.8–11.0
Phosphorus4.7 mg/dL2.5–5.5
PTHSuppressed—
Vitamin D toxicosis — Ca × P >70 → soft tissue mineralisation risk

↑Ca AND ↑P together = vitamin D toxicity pattern. Distinguishes from primary hyperparathyroidism (↑Ca + ↓P). Vitamin D increases intestinal absorption of BOTH Ca and P + mobilises both from bone. Ca × P product: >16 × 4.7 = >75 — above critical threshold of 70 mg²/dL² → risk of: nephrocalcinosis (most serious → AKI), vascular calcification, gastric mucosal mineralisation, pulmonary calcification. Treatment: IV saline diuresis, furosemide, glucocorticoids (↓ intestinal absorption), ± bisphosphonates. Monitor urine output and renal values closely.

🐴 Case 13 — 11yo Quarter Horse mare
Anorexia, weight loss, fever · Ca 15.8 ↑↑ · P 1.8 ↓ · PTHrp 13.3 ↑↑↑ (RI 0–1)
▼
AnalyteResultRI
Calcium15.8 mg/dL10.6–13.4
Phosphorus1.8 mg/dL1.9–6.0
PTHrp13.3 pmol/L0–1 (13× URL)
PTHSuppressed—
Hypercalcaemia of malignancy (HCM) — PTHrp-secreting neoplasm

↑Ca + ↓P + ↑↑↑ PTHrp + suppressed PTH = classic HCM pattern. PTHrp (parathyroid hormone-related protein) mimics PTH → increases bone Ca resorption + renal Ca retention + renal phosphate wasting (→ ↓P). PTH suppressed because the normal parathyroid gland correctly senses hypercalcaemia — the tumour ignores feedback. In horses: lymphoma or squamous cell carcinoma are most common PTHrp-secreting tumours. Treatment: address primary tumour, IV saline diuresis, furosemide, ± glucocorticoids, ± bisphosphonates.

🐄 Case 14 — Holstein, 5yo, 2 days post-calving
Cannot rise · Ca 5.3 ↓↓ · P 3.6 ↓ · Mg 3.1 ↑ · AST 491 ↑ · Hypothermic
▼
AnalyteResultRI
Calcium5.3 mg/dL8.0–10.4
Phosphorus3.6 mg/dL3.6–8.3
Magnesium3.1 mg/dL1.8–2.3
AST491 IU/L~45–100
Milk fever (parturient paresis / bovine hypocalcaemia)

Ca 5.3 = severe hypocalcaemia. Peak colostrum demand 1–4 days post-calving exceeds PTH-mediated Ca mobilisation capacity. Calcium is essential for muscle contraction — loss causes: recumbency (skeletal muscle), rumen atony (smooth muscle), hypothermia (thermogenic muscle paralysis), bradycardia (cardiac conduction). AST ↑ = recumbency myopathy ± hepatic lipidosis from negative energy balance. Treatment: slow IV calcium borogluconate — monitor ECG during infusion (too fast → ventricular fibrillation). Prevention: low-calcium (anionic) diet pre-calving to activate PTH axis before parturition.

Endocrine Cases (15–18)
🐕 Case 15 — Jazzi, 11yo FS Shih Tzu
PU/PD, alopecia, calcinosis cutis on ear · ALP 1047 (7×) · GGT 94 ↑↑ · USG 1.010 · UPCR 2.2
▼
CBCResultFlag
WBC20.52HIGH ↑
Neutrophils18.72HIGH ↑
Lymphocytes0.45LOW ↓
Monocytes1.13HIGH ↑
Platelets626KHIGH ↑
ChemistryResultFlag
ALP1,047HIGH (~7×)
ALT323HIGH ↑
GGT94HIGH ↑↑
Cholesterol363HIGH ↑
Creatinine0.4LOW ↓
BUN16Low-normal
USG1.010Markedly dilute
UPCR2.2HIGH (RI <0.5)
Diagnosis: Canine HAC (Cushing's Syndrome)

Stress leukogram (mature neutrophilia + lymphopenia + monocytosis + thrombocytosis) + ALP 1,047 (C-ALP isoenzyme) + GGT 94 (vacuolar hepatopathy) + hypercholesterolaemia + low creatinine (muscle wasting → ↓Cr production) + markedly dilute urine (cortisol antagonises ADH) + significant proteinuria (glomerulopathy from hypertension). Calcinosis cutis on the ear is nearly pathognomonic for HAC — cortisol → abnormal Ca-P metabolism → calcium salts precipitate in dermis. Next steps: LDDST or ACTH stim, abdominal US, urine culture (40–50% have occult UTI — always culture), blood pressure measurement.

🐕 Case 16 — Sidney, 10yo F Min Pin · Known DM + HAC on lysodren
Missed medications · Bradycardia + sinus pauses · K 9.3 ↑↑↑ · Na:K 13.6 · Glucose 607 ↑↑↑
▼
Key LabsResultFlag
Glucose607 mg/dLHIGH ↑↑↑
Sodium127 mEq/LLOW ↓↓
Potassium9.3 mEq/LHIGH ↑↑↑ LIFE-THREATENING
Na:K ratio13.6CRITICALLY LOW (RI 27.7–35.9)
Bicarbonate15 mEq/LLOW — metabolic acidosis
Calcium12.2 mg/dLHypercalcaemia (~30% Addisonian)
UA glucose4+Severe glucosuria (uncontrolled DM)
UA ketonesNEGATIVENot DKA
Diagnosis: Iatrogenic Addisonian crisis (lysodren over-suppression) + uncontrolled DM

Two simultaneous crises: (1) Electrolyte collapse — lysodren over-suppressed adrenal cortex → aldosterone deficiency → Na lost, K retained → Na:K 13.6. K 9.3 depolarises cardiac conduction → bradycardia + sinus pauses (life-threatening). (2) Hyperglycaemia 607 from missed insulin — NOT DKA (ketones negative). Emergency priorities: ① ECG + cardiac monitor ② IV 0.9% NaCl (NOT LRS — contains K) ③ IV dextrose (drives K into cells) ④ IV dexamethasone (will not interfere with ACTH stim) ⑤ ACTH stimulation test ⑥ Resume insulin only after K stabilises (insulin also shifts K intracellularly).

🐕 Case 17 — Yogi
Episodic weakness after running, worse post-exercise · Glucose 44 ↓↓ · All else near-normal
▼
AnalyteResultFlag
Glucose44 mg/dLLOW ↓↓ (critical)
ALP272Mildly HIGH (~2× — red herring)
ALT35Normal
GGT<3Low-normal
Na:K ratio~35Normal — not Addison's
Cholesterol279Normal — not hypothyroid
Lipase45Normal — not pancreatitis
Albumin4.0Normal
Diagnosis: Insulinoma — until proven otherwise

Glucose 44 mg/dL is the critical finding. Mild ALP 272 is a red herring — no GGT elevation, no stress leukogram, no dilute urine = not HAC. Na:K normal = not Addison's. Cholesterol normal = not hypothyroid. No pancreatitis. Why exercise worsens it: exercise depletes hepatic glycogen → glucagon normally raises glucose. With insulinoma, autonomous insulin secretion continues regardless → glucose falls further. Whipple's Triad: ✓ Episodic weakness · ✓ Glucose 44 mg/dL · ✓ Resolution with glucose. Next: measure immunoreactive insulin SIMULTANEOUSLY with documented hypoglycaemia — at glucose <50 mg/dL, any detectable insulin = inappropriately secreted = insulinoma.

🐕 Case 18 — Sally, young female WHWT
Episodic vomiting, weakness, collapse · Na ↓ · K ↑ · Na:K critically low · Mimics renal failure
▼
AnalyteResultFlag
Sodium~128 mEq/LLOW ↓↓
Potassium~7.1 mEq/LHIGH ↑↑
Na:K ratio~18CRITICALLY LOW (<27)
BUNMildly elevatedPre-renal azotaemia
CreatinineMildly elevatedPre-renal
USG<1.030Should be concentrated if pre-renal only
Eosinophils / LymphsNot decreasedKEY: no stress response in a sick dog
Diagnosis: Hypoadrenocorticism (Addison's disease) — "The Great Pretender"

Na:K <27 = cardinal finding. Aldosterone deficiency → Na wasting + K retention. Critical teaching points: (1) USG <1.030 despite azotaemia and dehydration = medullary washout from chronic Na wasting → mimics renal failure. (2) No stress leukogram in a collapsed dog = RED FLAG. Cortisol deficiency → no eosinopenia/lymphopenia. (3) Bradycardia in a hypovolaemic patient = hyperkalemia suppressing conduction (hypovolaemia should cause tachycardia). ALWAYS rule out Addison's before diagnosing renal failure in any young dog with electrolyte abnormalities. Diagnosis: ACTH stim — post-stim cortisol <2 µg/dL confirms. Treatment: IV 0.9% NaCl + dexamethasone IV → ACTH stim → confirm → DOCP + prednisone long-term.

Thyroid Worksheet Cases (T1–T4)
🐕 T1 — Bailey, 10yo F/S Shetland Sheepdog
Diffuse hair thinning, alopecia, poor coat · T4 <0.500 ↓↓ · TSH 1.12 ↑
▼
TestResultRI
T4 (in-house)<0.500 µg/dL1.2–5.2 µg/dL
TSH1.12 ng/mL0–0.50 ng/mL
Diagnosis: Canine Hypothyroidism (primary)

T4 <0.500 (markedly low) = below the lower limit of RI — consistent with hypothyroidism. Could also be suppressed by non-thyroidal illness (euthyroid sick syndrome from concurrent allergic skin disease) or medications. TSH 1.12 (elevated) = pituitary sensing inadequate T4 and secreting more TSH to stimulate the thyroid. Elevated TSH supports true hypothyroidism over euthyroid sick syndrome (which typically has normal TSH). Classic pattern: low T4 + high TSH = primary hypothyroidism. Diffuse hair thinning, alopecia, and poor coat quality are consistent cutaneous signs. Treat with L-thyroxine supplementation.

🐕 T2 — Kayla, 12yo F/S Maltese
Chronically elevated liver enzymes, history iatrogenic hypoadrenocorticism · TT4 0.54 ↓ · fT4-ED 12 (normal) · TSH 17 (normal)
▼
In-House TT4ResultRI
TT40.54 µg/dL1.2–5.2 µg/dL
MSU Full PanelResultRI
TT49 nmol/L11–60 nmol/L
fT4-ED12 pmol/L6–42 pmol/L
TSH17 mU/L0–30 mU/L
T4AA12%0–20%
T3AA6%0–10%
TGAA1%<20% = Negative
Diagnosis: Euthyroid Sick Syndrome — NOT hypothyroidism

TT4 low (9 nmol/L) — consistent with hypothyroidism, euthyroid sick, or drug suppression. fT4-ED normal (12 pmol/L) — this is the KEY finding. fT4-ED by equilibrium dialysis is resistant to non-thyroidal illness suppression; a normal fT4-ED argues strongly against true hypothyroidism. TSH normal (17 mU/L) — the pituitary is NOT sensing thyroid hormone deficiency; if truly hypothyroid, TSH would be elevated. All autoantibodies negative. Pattern = euthyroid sick syndrome (NTI suppressing TT4 while fT4-ED and TSH remain normal). Concurrent conditions (iatrogenic hypoadrenocorticism, chronic liver disease) explain the suppressed TT4. Do NOT start L-thyroxine — treat the underlying disease and reassess thyroid function when the patient is stable.

🐕 T3 — Rusty, 18mo M Golden Retriever
Recurrent pyoderma, lethargy, weight gain · TT4 17 (normal) · fT4-ED 0 ↓↓ · TSH 75 ↑↑ · T4AA ↑, TGAA positive
▼
Lipid PanelResultRI
Triglycerides318 mg/dL28–303 mg/dL
MSU Thyroid PanelResultRI
TT417 nmol/L11–60 (normal)
fT4-ED0 pmol/L6–42 pmol/L ↓↓
TSH75 mU/L0–30 mU/L ↑↑
T4AA31%0–20%
T3AA57%0–10%
TGAA362%>35% = Positive
Diagnosis: Hypothyroidism with T4 Autoantibody Interference — Lymphocytic Thyroiditis

TT4 17 (falsely normal) — T4AA (31%) is being measured AS T4 in the standard assay → artificially inflates the result, masking true deficiency. fT4-ED essentially 0 (critically low) — equilibrium dialysis removes T4AAs before measuring, revealing the true (severely deficient) free T4. TSH 75 (markedly elevated) — pituitary maximally stimulating the thyroid = the highest-yield finding confirming true hypothyroidism. TGAA positive (362%) = lymphocytic thyroiditis is the underlying cause. Classic "discordant" pattern: TT4 misleads, fT4-ED + TSH reveal the truth. Treatment: L-thyroxine supplementation. Hypertriglyceridemia (318) resolves with treatment.

🐱 T4 — Simba, 18yo F/S DSH cat
Very thin (BCS 3/9), HR 240 bpm, muscle wasting, small kidneys · TT4 6.77 ↑↑ · BUN 44 ↑ · PHOS 6.2 ↑ · ALT 161 ↑
▼
ChemistryResultRI
BUN44 mg/dL15–37
Creatinine1.7 mg/dL0.7–1.9 (upper end)
Phosphorus6.2 mg/dL2.4–5.7
ALT161 IU/L27–95
ALP57 IU/L11–58 (within)
GGT<3 IU/L0–3
UA & ThyroidResultRI
USG1.017—
TT46.77 µg/dL0.78–4.27
Diagnosis: Feline Hyperthyroidism masking concurrent CKD

TT4 6.77 (elevated; RI 0.78–4.27) = diagnostic for feline hyperthyroidism in the right signalment (18yo cat). Tachycardia (HR 240) = thyroid hormone-driven cardiac stimulation. ALT mildly elevated = hyperthyroid hepatopathy (resolves with treatment). Critical issue: BUN 44 + Cr 1.7 (upper end) + USG 1.017 + small palpable kidneys = CKD masked by hyperthyroidism. Hyperthyroidism ↑GFR → dilutes creatinine into near-normal range → CKD appears less severe than it is. After treating hyperthyroidism, GFR normalizes → creatinine will rise → CKD unmasked. Hyperphosphatemia (6.2) supports reduced renal phosphate excretion = CKD. Management: methimazole trial before permanent treatment to allow renal reassessment. If creatinine rises significantly after treatment, consider lower target TT4.

Adrenal Worksheet Cases (A1–A5)
🐕 A1 — Katy, 13yo F/S Terrier mix
Chronic skin infections × 3 years, generalized erythema and pustules · ACTH stim negative · LDDST negative
▼
ACTH StimResultRI
COR0 (baseline)7.8 µg/dL1–4.5 µg/dL ↑
COR1 (post-ACTH)12.1 µg/dL7–20 µg/dL (normal)
LDDSTResultRI
COR0 (baseline)1.2 µg/dL1–4.5 µg/dL
COR4 (4-hour)<1.00 µg/dL<1.4 = suppressed ✓
COR8 (8-hour)<1.00 µg/dL<1.4 = suppressed ✓
Diagnosis: HAC not confirmed — both screening tests negative

ACTH stim: COR1 = 12.1 (RI 7–20) = NEGATIVE — normal stimulation response. Does not support HAC. Note: ACTH stim sensitivity for PDH = 60–70%, so up to 40% of PDH dogs can test negative. LDDST: both COR4 and COR8 <1.00 = NEGATIVE — normal suppression. The elevated baseline (COR0 7.8) before LDDST is notable but the suppression pattern is normal, reflecting a stressed patient at start. Both screening tests negative = HAC is unlikely but not excluded given sensitivity limitations. Clinical signs (chronic skin infections, immunosuppression) could reflect other causes. Consider UCCR collected at home as a low-stress screen, or re-evaluate in 3–6 months if signs progress.

🐕 A2 — Morgan, 10yo F/S Labrador mix
Difficult-to-control DM · LDDST: COR4 suppressed ↓, COR8 3.2 ↑ (positive) — PDH pattern
▼
LDDSTResultRI / Cutoff
COR0 (baseline)3.1 µg/dL1–4.5 µg/dL (normal)
COR4 (4-hour)<1.00 µg/dL<1.4 µg/dL — suppressed ✓
COR8 (8-hour)3.2 µg/dL<1.4 µg/dL — NOT suppressed ✗
Diagnosis: HAC confirmed (PDH pattern) — Concurrent with DM

COR8 3.2 > 1.4 = LDDST POSITIVE — cortisol not suppressed at 8 hours = HAC confirmed. Reason to screen: difficult-to-control DM is a major trigger — cortisol causes insulin resistance, promoting gluconeogenesis and antagonizing insulin at receptor level. PDH discrimination: COR4 <1.00 (<1.4) = suppression criterion met → supports PDH (pituitary tumor partially suppressible with low-dose dex but escapes by 8 hours). Confirm with abdominal US (bilateral adrenomegaly expected for PDH). Treatment: trilostane or mitotane for HAC; expect insulin requirements to decrease once cortisol is controlled.

🐕 A3 — Makoa, 12yo M/C German Spitz
1.5yr elevated liver enzymes, PU/PD, polyphagia · LDDST: elevated baseline + NO suppression at 4 or 8 hr
▼
LDDSTResultRI / Cutoff
COR0 (baseline)7.0 µg/dL1–4.5 µg/dL — ELEVATED ↑
COR4 (4-hour)5.2 µg/dL<1.4 µg/dL — NOT suppressed ✗
COR8 (8-hour)5.6 µg/dL<1.4 µg/dL — NOT suppressed ✗
Diagnosis: HAC confirmed — PDH vs ADH indeterminate by LDDST alone

COR8 5.6 > 1.4 = LDDST POSITIVE — HAC confirmed. Elevated baseline (7.0) suggests baseline hypercortisolemia. No suppression at 4 or 8 hr, and neither <50% of baseline (COR4/baseline = 74%; COR8/baseline = 80%) — zero PDH suppression criteria met. Cannot discriminate PDH vs ADH by LDDST suppression pattern alone when no criteria are met. Next steps: (1) Abdominal ultrasound — bilateral adrenomegaly = PDH; unilateral mass + contralateral atrophy = ADH. (2) Endogenous ACTH (eACTH) — high = PDH; low/undetectable = ADH. Both tests required for definitive discrimination.

🐕 A4 — Annabelle, 13yo F/S Beagle
PU/PD, urinary incontinence, bilateral adrenomegaly on US · ACTH stim gray zone · LDDST positive, no PDH suppression
▼
ACTH StimResultRI
COR03.2 µg/dL1–4.5 µg/dL
COR1 (post-ACTH)19.5 µg/dL7–20 µg/dL (gray zone)
LDDSTResultRI / Cutoff
COR04.6 µg/dL1–4.5 µg/dL ↑
COR42.6 µg/dL<1.4 — NOT suppressed ✗
COR82.9 µg/dL<1.4 — NOT suppressed ✗
Diagnosis: PDH — confirmed by LDDST positive + bilateral adrenomegaly on US

ACTH stim COR1 = 19.5 (gray zone 17–20) = NEGATIVE/gray zone for HAC. ACTH stim sensitivity for PDH only 60–70% — expected that many PDH dogs test negative or gray zone. Do NOT use alone to rule out HAC. LDDST COR8 = 2.9 > 1.4 = POSITIVE — HAC confirmed; LDDST is the more sensitive test. No PDH suppression criteria met (COR4/baseline = 57%, COR8/baseline = 63% — neither <50%). Discrimination: bilateral adrenomegaly on US = bilateral stimulation = PDH (pituitary ACTH driving both adrenals). Combination of LDDST positive + bilateral adrenomegaly = PDH most likely diagnosis. Treatment: trilostane or mitotane; monitor with ACTH stim tests.

🐕 A5 — Sadie, 11yo F/S Shih Tzu
Hair thinning, pyoderma, accidents indoors · on topical triamcinolone · ACTH stim: baseline 1.6, post-ACTH 2.6 ↓↓ (flat)
▼
ACTH StimulationResultRI
COR0 (baseline)1.6 µg/dL1–4.5 µg/dL (low-normal)
COR1 (post-ACTH)2.6 µg/dL7–20 µg/dL — MARKEDLY LOW ↓↓

Current medications: Topical triamcinolone as needed for allergic dermatitis

Diagnosis: Iatrogenic HAC with secondary HPA axis suppression from topical triamcinolone

COR1 = 2.6 (RI 7–20) = MARKEDLY LOW / FLAT RESPONSE = inadequate stimulation. The adrenal glands cannot respond to exogenous ACTH. Critical history: topical triamcinolone — a potent synthetic glucocorticoid absorbed systemically → suppresses HPA axis via negative feedback → adrenal cortex atrophies from chronic ACTH suppression → adrenals fail to respond when challenged. This is iatrogenic HAC, not spontaneous. LDDST is useless here (exogenous steroids independently suppress the HPA axis). Management: gradually withdraw triamcinolone (do NOT stop abruptly — risk of iatrogenic Addisonian crisis). Switch to less potent topical steroid or alternative anti-pruritic (oclacitinib). Supplement glucocorticoids during stress until adrenal recovery confirmed on repeat ACTH stim.

Official study questions from lectures — organized by body system. Click a tab to navigate.
🫀 Liver · Biochem · Exocrine Pancreas
Q1. Which enzymes detect hepatocellular injury in (a) small animals and (b) large animals?
▼
(a) Small animals: ALT (primary), AST · (b) Large animals: SDH (primary), AST

ALT is liver-specific in SA. In large animals, liver ALT activity is too low — SDH is the enzyme of choice. AST used in both but non-specific (muscle source).

Q2. Rank these enzymes by half-life, shortest to longest: AST, ALT, ALP, CK
▼
CK (2–4 hr) → AST (~12 hr) → ALT (~60 hr) → ALP (~72 hr)
Q3. Classic lab pattern of end-stage liver disease?
▼
Synthesis ↓: albumin ↓, BUN ↓, glucose ↓, clotting factors ↓. Clearance ↓: bile acids ↑, ammonia ↑, bilirubin ↑. Enzymes paradoxically low (nothing left to leak).
Q4. Bilirubin is 18 mg/dL — is bile acids or ammonia the better liver function test?
▼
Ammonia — bile acids add no information when bilirubin is already markedly elevated (cholestasis/liver disease is already confirmed). Ammonia tests the urea cycle independently.
Rev Q15. List 3 chemistry values that increase with cholestasis.
▼
ALP · GGT · Bilirubin (conjugated/direct)

ALP: markedly induced by bile acids backing up into hepatocytes → new ALP synthesis. GGT: biliary epithelial enzyme, very sensitive for cholestasis in cats (more specific than ALP in cats). Conjugated bilirubin: accumulates when bile flow is obstructed (bile duct obstruction, intrahepatic cholestasis). Note: bilirubinuria appears BEFORE serum bilirubin rises — sensitive early cholestasis marker. ALT and AST may also rise secondary to cholestatic hepatocellular injury.

Rev Q16. What is the most common chemistry abnormality in hyperthyroid cats?
▼
Elevated ALP (and often ALT) — hepatopathy secondary to hyperthyroidism

Thyroid hormone excess causes direct hepatocellular injury (increased metabolic demand, hepatic congestion, malnutrition) → ALP and ALT elevation. This is so common that elevated liver enzymes in a middle-aged/older cat should always prompt T4 testing. Other common findings: elevated BUN (increased muscle catabolism = increased protein turnover → more nitrogen), stress leukogram, erythrocytosis (↑ EPO stimulation), hyperphosphatemia. Concurrent CKD is common and may be "unmasked" after treatment.

🔬 GI, Exocrine Pancreas & Muscle
GI and exocrine pancreas questions are covered in the clinical cases and the Game tab (GI & Pancreas section). Use the 📋 Cases tab for case-based GI practice.
💪 Muscle
Muscle-specific study questions are integrated with liver biochemistry above. Key muscle markers: CK (half-life 2–4 hr) · AST (non-specific) · Troponin I (cardiac). See the 🫀 Liver & Biochem tab.
🦴 Ca / P / Mg
CaP Q1. What effect does hypoalbuminemia have on total Ca vs. ionized Ca?
▼
↓ total Ca (protein-bound fraction ↓) but iCa is typically NORMAL — pseudohypocalcemia. Rarely causes clinical signs alone.
CaP Q2. Effect of metabolic acidosis on ionized calcium?
▼
Acidemia → ↑ iCa. H⁺ displaces Ca²⁺ from albumin binding → more free Ca. Alkalemia → ↓ iCa (Ca binds albumin more avidly).
Rev Q13. What is the mnemonic for hypercalcemia? List all differentials.
▼
GOSHDARNIT: Granulomatous disease · Osteolysis · Spurious/lab error · Hypoadrenocorticism · D vitamin toxicosis · Alimentary (GI absorption) · Renal disease (early) · Neoplasia (most common) · Idiopathic (cats) · Temperature/sample artifact

Most common causes: Neoplasia (#1 in dogs — lymphoma, AGASACA, myeloma via PTHrP), Hypoadrenocorticism (#2 in young dogs), Vitamin D rodenticide toxicosis, primary hyperparathyroidism (older dogs). In cats: idiopathic is #1. Always check ionized Ca to confirm true hypercalcemia (not just protein-bound artifact). PTH and PTHrP help differentiate: PTH high = primary HPT; PTHrP high = humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy.

Rev Q14. What is the most expected Ca and P pattern with renal disease in small animals?
▼
Hyperphosphatemia + normal-to-low calcium (Ca × P product elevated)

Renal disease → ↓ GFR → phosphate retention (hyperphosphatemia). Hyperphosphatemia → phosphate binds Ca²⁺ → reciprocal ↓ in ionized Ca. ↓ renal 1α-hydroxylase → ↓ calcitriol → ↓ intestinal Ca absorption → further hypocalcemia. ↓ iCa stimulates PTH → secondary renal hyperparathyroidism → bone resorption (fibrous osteodystrophy, "rubber jaw"). Elevated Ca × P product (>55–70 in dogs) → soft tissue mineralization risk (metastatic calcification of kidneys, lungs, vessels).

🩸 Hematology
Heme Q1. Compare the leukocyte stress response (SSMILED) vs. epinephrine response (ELSI). What is the key difference in time course and mechanism?
▼
Epinephrine (ELSI): immediate, short-lived (~30 min), redistribution of neutrophils from MNP→CNP + lymphocytosis. Stress (SSMILED): delayed (hours), longer lasting (~24 hr), neutrophilia + monocytosis + lymphopenia + eosinopenia from corticosteroid effects.

Epinephrine response: due to acute catecholamine release — redistribution, no new cell production; 30-minute duration. Classic in stressed cats (neutrophils up to 39,000, lymphs up to 35,000).
Corticosteroid/stress response: requires endogenous cortisol (pain, illness, Cushing's, exogenous steroids). Develops over hours, peaks 4–8 hr. Neutrophilia from adhesion molecule down-regulation shifting MNP→CNP. Lymphopenia from redistribution to lymphoid tissues ± lymphotoxic effect. Monocytosis from redistribution. Eosinopenia the most consistent finding in cattle.

Heme Q2. A dog has a normocytic/normochromic anemia with reticulocyte count of 45,000/µL. Is this regenerative? What should you do next?
▼
Non-regenerative (inadequate) — canine threshold for any regeneration is >95,000/µL. However, do not stop here.

Consider three important caveats: (1) Mild anemia may not provide enough hypoxic stress to stimulate retic release. (2) If the patient is in the 2–3 day pre-regenerative window after an acute event, it will appear non-regenerative. (3) Very severe anemias (Hct <10%) may have a marginal initial response. Serial PCVs over 3–5 days are the most reliable assessment tool — a rising PCV confirms regeneration even when retics appear inadequate. Also evaluate RDW, blood smear for polychromasia, and reticulocyte count methodology (laser vs. manual give different numbers).

Heme Q3. Describe the fluid analysis expected with FIP effusion vs. septic exudate vs. pure transudate.
▼
FeaturePure TransudateFIP EffusionSeptic Exudate
AppearanceClear, colorlessViscous, yellow, high fibrinTurbid, yellow-white
Protein (g/dL)<2.5Very high (often >3.5–5)>4.0
NCC (/µL)<500Low to moderate (often <5,000)Very high (>5,000, often >10,000)
Cell typesRare meso cells, few lymphsMacrophages, non-degenerate neuts, some lymphsDegenerate neutrophils ± intracellular bacteria
Key featureLow protein, low cells = ↓ oncotic pressure (hypoalbuminemia)High protein:low cell ratio is characteristic; gamma globulin often dominantBacteria intracellular in neuts; degenerate (pyknotic) neutrophils = toxic local environment
Rev Q1. What disease should you suspect in older dogs with persistent and progressive lymphocytosis?
▼
Chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL)

CLL is a neoplasm of mature-appearing lymphocytes that typically presents as a slowly progressive, sustained lymphocytosis in older dogs. Unlike reactive lymphocytosis (transient, usually <20,000/µL), CLL shows persistent counts that may exceed 30,000–100,000/µL over months. Diagnosis requires immunophenotyping (B-cell CLL most common in dogs) ± PARR. CLL is generally a low-grade disease with prolonged survival compared to high-grade lymphoma.

Rev Q2. In which species is a neutrophil count of 30,000/µL considered a marked to extreme neutrophilia?
▼
Cats — the normal neutrophil RI in cats is 2,500–12,500/µL, so 30,000 = marked to extreme neutrophilia.

In dogs, the RI is 3,000–11,500/µL so 30,000 is a moderate neutrophilia. In cats, 30,000 exceeds the RI by ~2.4× the upper limit, placing it in the marked-to-extreme range. Species-specific reference intervals are essential — the same absolute number can have very different clinical significance.

Rev Q3. What pattern of MCV and MCHC is considered classic for iron deficiency?
▼
Microcytic (↓ MCV) + hypochromic (↓ MCHC) anemia

Iron is required for heme synthesis. Without sufficient iron, RBCs are produced with less hemoglobin → smaller (microcytic) and paler (hypochromic) cells. On blood smear: small RBCs with increased central pallor, poikilocytosis (schistocytes, keratocytes), thrombocytosis (reactive). Iron panel: ↓ serum iron, ↓ ferritin, ↑ TIBC, ↓ transferrin saturation. Most common in young animals with heavy parasitism (fleas, hookworms) or chronic blood loss.

Rev Q4. What morphology finding is highly suggestive of snake envenomation?
▼
Schistocytes (fragmented RBCs) — combined with a consumptive coagulopathy (DIC pattern)

Pit viper venom contains proteases and phospholipases that directly fragment RBCs (producing schistocytes) and activate coagulation → DIC. Lab pattern: thrombocytopenia, prolonged PT/aPTT, low fibrinogen, elevated FDPs/D-dimers + schistocytes on smear. The combination of schistocytes + DIC in a dog with a bite wound is highly suggestive of crotalid (rattlesnake, copperhead, cottonmouth) envenomation.

🩺 Hemostasis & Coagulation
Rev Q5. What blood collection tube is required for a coagulation panel?
▼
Blue-top tube (sodium citrate, 3.2%) — filled to exactly the line (9:1 blood:anticoagulant ratio)

Sodium citrate chelates calcium, reversibly inhibiting coagulation. The 9:1 ratio is critical — underfilling (too much citrate relative to blood) artificially prolongs PT/aPTT. EDTA (purple) and heparin (green) are NOT acceptable — EDTA permanently chelates calcium, heparin directly inhibits thrombin. Process within 30–60 min for accurate results.

Rev Q6. Which coagulation factors are vitamin K dependent?
▼
Factors II (prothrombin), VII, IX, X — and Proteins C and S (anticoagulants)

Mnemonic: 1972 (factors II, VII, IX, X) — or "2, 7, 9, 10." Vitamin K is a cofactor for γ-carboxylation of glutamate residues on these factors, required for their calcium-binding and activation. Anticoagulant rodenticides (brodifacoum, warfarin) block vitamin K epoxide reductase → these factors cannot be activated → bleeding. Factor VII has the shortest half-life (~6 hr) → PT prolongs first, then aPTT.

Rev Q7. A 1-year-old male dog bleeds excessively after a toenail trim. Platelet count and coagulation times are within RI. What is the most likely diagnosis?
▼
von Willebrand disease (vWD) — the most common inherited coagulopathy in dogs

Normal platelet count + normal PT/aPTT + mucosal/surface bleeding = primary hemostasis problem despite normal counts, pointing to platelet function disorder. vWD causes failure of platelet adhesion (vWF bridges GP Ib receptor to subendothelial collagen). Standard coagulation tests do NOT measure platelet function — buccal mucosal bleeding time (BMBT) is prolonged. Confirm with vWF antigen assay. Breeds: Dobermans (Type 1 most common), Scottish Terriers (Type 3 = most severe).

🫘 Kidney & UA
Rev Q8. What are the 4 main causes of an increased anion gap?
▼
KULD: Ketoacidosis · Uremia · Lactic acidosis · Drugs/toxins (e.g., ethylene glycol)

Anion gap = (Na + K) − (Cl + HCO₃) — normal ~12–24 mEq/L. An elevated AG = unmeasured anions present. (1) Ketoacidosis (DKA, starvation): β-hydroxybutyrate + acetoacetate. (2) Uremia: phosphate, sulfate, organic acids accumulate. (3) Lactic acidosis: shock, hypoperfusion, severe anemia. (4) Toxins: ethylene glycol → glycolic acid; salicylates; methanol → formic acid. High-AG metabolic acidosis requires searching for the underlying cause.

Rev Q9. List the 3 differentials for a positive occult blood test on a urine dipstick.
▼
1. Hematuria · 2. Hemoglobinuria · 3. Myoglobinuria

The dipstick peroxidase reaction detects heme in all three. Differentiate: Hematuria — RBCs in sediment, urine clears on centrifugation, plasma clear. Hemoglobinuria — no RBCs in sediment, urine stays red/brown after spin, PINK plasma (intravascular hemolysis). Myoglobinuria — no RBCs, urine stays discolored, CLEAR plasma, CK markedly elevated (rhabdomyolysis).

Rev Q10. List the components of nephrotic syndrome.
▼
Proteinuria + Hypoalbuminemia + Edema/ascites + Hypercholesterolemia

Nephrotic syndrome = glomerular disease severe enough to cause massive protein loss. Mechanism: glomerular damage → albumin leaks → hypoalbuminemia → ↓ plasma oncotic pressure → edema/ascites/pleural effusion + compensatory hepatic lipoprotein synthesis → hypercholesterolemia. UPC >2.0 in dogs/cats = significant proteinuria. Causes: glomerulonephritis (immune complex deposition), amyloidosis, hereditary nephropathy. Patients at risk for thromboembolism (loss of antithrombin III in urine).

Rev Q11. A cat has marked titrational metabolic acidosis, hypocalcemia, azotemia, dilute urine, and glucosuria. What is your top differential?
▼
Ethylene glycol (antifreeze) toxicity

The combination is pathognomonic: EG is metabolized to oxalic acid → chelates Ca²⁺ → hypocalcemia + calcium oxalate monohydrate crystals in urine → AKI → azotemia + isosthenuria (tubular damage = loss of concentrating ability). Glucosuria without hyperglycemia = tubular damage (Fanconi-like pattern from tubular necrosis). Marked high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis from glycolic acid accumulation. Cats develop signs faster (3 hr post-ingestion) than dogs (6 hr). Treatment window is critical — fomepizole (less effective in cats vs dogs; ethanol is alternative), IV fluids, bicarbonate, calcium gluconate.

Rev Q12. A 7-month-old Yorkie presents for altered mentation. Abdominal US reveals a PSS. Which crystal might you see in the urine?
▼
Ammonium biurate crystals (thorn-apple / spiky sphere shape)

PSS → blood bypasses liver → ammonia not converted to urea → hyperammonemia (explains CNS signs). Uric acid also accumulates (not converted to allantoin by liver). Ammonia + uric acid → ammonium biurate crystals in alkaline to neutral urine. Yellow-brown, spiky sphere ("thorn apple"). Also seen with severe hepatic failure and in normal Dalmatians (breed-specific uric acid transport defect). PSS confirmation: markedly elevated post-prandial bile acids, hypoalbuminemia, low BUN, hypoglycemia.

⚗️ Electrolytes & Acid-Base
Electrolyte and acid-base study questions are integrated in the Kidney & UA tab (anion gap, occult blood, nephrotic syndrome, EG toxicity). For deeper practice use the ⚗️ Electrolytes section of the 🎮 Game tab.
☠️ Toxicology
Tox Q1. Describe the three phases of iron poisoning and what organ is primarily affected in Phase III.
▼
Phase I (0–6 hr): GI — bloody vomiting/diarrhea, hypovolemic shock, metabolic acidosis. Phase II: Apparent recovery (asymptomatic). Phase III (12–24 hr post-Phase I): Liver failure (hepatocellular necrosis) ± oliguria/anuria from shock-induced renal failure.

The Phase II "calm" is deceptive — do not discharge the patient. Liver failure develops because iron deposits in hepatocytes produce free radicals (Fenton reaction) → lipid peroxidation → hepatic necrosis.

Tox Q2. What is the specific treatment for iron poisoning and what monitoring sign confirms it is working?
▼
Deferoxamine (40 mg/kg IM q4–8 hr × 5–7 days). Monitoring sign: "vin rosé urine" — pinkish-red discoloration = ferrioxamine being excreted in urine, confirming active chelation.

Chelation is indicated when serum Fe >300 µg/dL OR serum Fe exceeds total iron binding capacity. Milk of magnesia is used early (complexes iron in GIT to decrease absorption).

Tox Q3. Why does urea poisoning only affect ruminants, and what is the mechanism of toxicity?
▼
Ruminants have bacterial urease in the rumen that converts urea → NH₃ + CO₂. Monogastrics lack this fermentative compartment, so dietary urea is absorbed and excreted without generating toxic ammonia loads.

Mechanism: NH₃ production exceeds liver urea-cycle clearance → hyperammonemia → astrocyte swelling → cerebral edema → encephalopathy. Alkaline rumen pH (≥7.5) worsens this by shifting NH₄⁺ ⇌ NH₃ equilibrium toward the membrane-permeable NH₃ form.

Tox Q4. Explain the vinegar + cold water treatment for urea poisoning at the molecular level.
▼
Cold water: ↓ urease enzyme activity (temperature-sensitive) + dilutes urea substrate. Vinegar (acetic acid): provides H⁺ → NH₃ + H⁺ → NH₄⁺ (ammonium ion). NH₄⁺ is ionized and cannot cross GIT membranes → trapped in rumen → not absorbed into blood.
Tox Q5. Why is rapid rehydration dangerous in salt poisoning, and what is the pathognomonic lesion in pigs?
▼
During hypernatremia, brain cells produce organic osmolytes to equalize osmolality. If Na⁺ is corrected rapidly, ECF becomes hypotonic relative to ICF (which still has high osmolytes) → water rushes into cells → cerebral edema → death. Pathognomonic lesion in pigs: eosinophilic perivascular cuffs in the cerebral cortex and adjacent meninges (absent in cattle).

Treatment: Provide water at only 0.5% BW/hr orally. For IV correction: D5W at 4 ml/kg/hr or hypotonic saline if volume deficit is also present.

Tox Q6. What is the hallmark hematologic finding in zinc toxicosis, and what is the underlying mechanism?
▼
Heinz body hemolytic anemia — caused by oxidative damage to the RBC membrane (not directly to hemoglobin), with glutathione depletion as the key mechanism.

Zinc causes intravascular hemolysis by oxidizing the erythrocyte membrane rather than hemoglobin itself. Glutathione (the cell's antioxidant buffer) is depleted → oxidative stress → Heinz body formation → membrane fragility → hemolysis. Clinically: pale mucous membranes, icterus (pre-hepatic), hemoglobinuria. The most common source in dogs is pennies minted after 1983 (97.5% zinc).

Tox Q7. Explain the pathophysiology of chronic copper poisoning in sheep, including why the hemolytic crisis occurs suddenly after weeks of subclinical accumulation.
▼
Sheep have a limited ability to excrete copper in bile. Copper accumulates silently in hepatocytes for weeks to months (subclinical phase). When hepatic storage capacity is finally exceeded — often triggered by a physiologic stressor — copper is suddenly released into the blood → ↑ blood copper → lipid peroxidation → intravascular hemolysis → hemolytic crisis.

Triggers for crisis: transportation stress, lactation, strenuous exercise, deteriorating nutrition, ingestion of hepatotoxic plants. Pathognomonic lesions: swollen gunmetal-colored kidneys + port-wine urine + centrilobular hepatic necrosis. Despite low herd morbidity (<5%), >75% of affected animals die. Losses can continue 2 months after the dietary source is corrected because copper remains stored in other animals' livers. Treatment: ammonium tetrathiomolybdate or D-penicillamine; prognosis is very poor once crisis starts.

🦋 Endocrine
Rev Q17. If a LDDST is positive for Cushing's, the next step is looking for evidence of suppression. List the 3 criteria used.
▼
3 criteria for differentiation (LDDST / HDDST): (1) 4-hr cortisol <50% baseline · (2) 4-hr cortisol <1.4 µg/dL · (3) 8-hr cortisol <1.4 µg/dL

These criteria suggest PDH (pituitary-dependent hyperadrenocorticism) rather than ADH (adrenal-dependent): the pituitary retains some dexamethasone sensitivity, so at least partial suppression can occur. If ANY one criterion is met on LDDST → suggests PDH. Adrenal tumor: cortisol autonomously secreted → NO suppression at any point. If LDDST doesn't differentiate, HDDST (0.1 mg/kg dex) or endogenous ACTH measurement is next.

Rev Q18. When treating a DKA patient with insulin, what 2 chemistry parameters must be closely monitored due to life-threatening risk?
▼
Potassium (K⁺) and Glucose

Potassium: DKA patients are total body K⁺ depleted (osmotic diuresis losses) but may appear normokalemic or hyperkalemic on presentation (acidosis shifts K⁺ out of cells). Insulin drives K⁺ back intracellularly → rapid hypokalemia → life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias and respiratory muscle paralysis. Must supplement K⁺ aggressively as insulin is started.
