Glucose: Insulin rapidly lowers blood glucose → hypoglycemia if insulin rate not adjusted. Monitor glucose q1–2 hr. When glucose reaches ~250 mg/dL → add dextrose to IV fluids and continue low-dose insulin to clear ketones without causing hypoglycemia.

🔬 Thyroid & Adrenal Worksheet
Thyroid and adrenal worksheet cases with full Q&A are in the 📋 Cases tab under "🔬 Thyroid & Adrenal (T1–T4, A1–A5)". Endocrine review questions (LDDST, DKA monitoring) are in the 🦋 Endocrine tab above.
🧫 Cytology
Cyt Q1. What is the key difference between squash prep and splat smear? Which is diagnostic?
▼
Squash prep ✅ — press gently then pull horizontally → well-spread, diagnostic. Splat smear ❌ — press and immediately lift → thick clumps, non-diagnostic.

Squash prep is the standard for FNA samples. Splat smear is the most common reason cytology is non-diagnostic in practice. DiffQuik stains granules poorly in mast cell tumors — send for Wright-Giemsa if you suspect MCT but see no granules.

Cyt Q2. For which tumor categories are criteria of malignancy (CoM) reliable vs unreliable?
▼
Reliable: Epithelial and Mesenchymal. Unreliable: Round cell and Neuroendocrine tumors — diagnose by morphology, not CoM.

Round cell tumors (LYMPH mnemonic: Lymphoma, TVT, MCT, Plasmacytoma, Histiocytoma) are identified by their unique morphologic features. Neuroendocrine tumors show "free nuclei in a sea of cytoplasm" — malignant ones can look bland. Always check for concurrent inflammation before calling malignancy (inflammation causes dysplastic change).

Cyt Q3. What is the LYMPH mnemonic for round cell tumors? Give the key cytologic feature of each.
▼
L=Lymphoma (monomorphic large lymphocytes), Y=TVT (round clear cytoplasmic vacuoles), M=MCT (purple granules — use WG stain), P=Plasmacytoma (perinuclear clear zone + soccer ball chromatin), H=Histiocytoma (fried egg, spontaneous regression)

For lymphoma: monomorphism (uniformity of the large cell population) is the key — not just cell size. Reactive lymph nodes still have >50% small lymphocytes. TVT cytoplasmic vacuoles are pathognomonic. MCT: DiffQuik often fails to stain granules → send Wright-Giemsa. Plasmacytoma: perinuclear clear zone = Golgi apparatus.

Cyt Q4. What does finding degenerate neutrophils in a tissue aspirate mean? How does this differ from toxic change in blood?
▼
Degenerate neutrophils (tissue): nuclear swelling/loss of segmentation = bacterial infection → BUG HUNT! Toxic change (blood): cytoplasmic changes (basophilia, vacuoles, Döhle bodies) from rapid marrow release.

Degenerate change is a TISSUE-only finding caused by local bacterial toxins and acidic pH. It is NOT seen in peripheral blood. Toxic change IS seen in blood during severe systemic inflammation. Finding degenerate neutrophils + intracellular bacteria = septic suppurative inflammation confirmed.

Cyt Q5. How do you classify effusions? What is the hallmark of FIP effusion vs septic exudate?
▼
FIP: VERY HIGH protein + LOW NCC (high protein:low cell count ratio). Septic exudate: degenerate neutrophils ± intracellular bacteria. Both are "exudate-range" protein but opposite cell counts.

Effusion classification: Pure transudate (protein <2.5 g/dL, NCC <1500) → Modified transudate (protein >2.5, NCC <5000) → Exudate (protein >2.5, NCC >5000). FIP vasculitis leaks protein without recruiting cells → high protein:low cell ratio is pathognomonic. Confirm FIP: Rivalta test, PCR on fluid, A:G ratio <0.6.

Cyt Q6. Differentiate granulomatous, pyogranulomatous, and eosinophilic inflammation by cell thresholds and causes.
▼
Granulomatous (>50% macrophages): infectious (fungi, Mycobacterium, Leishmania, protozoa) or non-infectious (foreign body, acral lick). Pyogranulomatous (50–70% neuts + 30–50% mononuclear): foreign body or fungal. Eosinophilic (>10% eos): parasites, oomycetes, allergy, arthropod bite.

Key fungi: Blastomyces (broad-based budding), Cryptococcus (narrow-based budding + capsule), Histoplasma (intracellular, 2–5 µm), Coccidioides (spherules, no budding). All cause granulomatous to pyogranulomatous inflammation.

🧪 Urinalysis
UA Q1. List the 3 differentials for a positive urine occult blood dipstick and how to differentiate them.
▼
1. Hematuria (RBCs in sediment, urine clears on spin, clear plasma) · 2. Hemoglobinuria (no RBCs, stays red, PINK plasma — intravascular hemolysis) · 3. Myoglobinuria (no RBCs, stays discolored, CLEAR plasma, CK markedly elevated)

All three produce a positive dipstick peroxidase reaction because heme is present in all forms. Key: check plasma color first (pink = Hgb), then sediment for RBCs, then CK if plasma clear.

UA Q2. What are the 4 main causes of an elevated anion gap? Give the mnemonic.
▼
KULD: Ketoacidosis · Uremia · Lactic acidosis · Drugs/toxins (especially ethylene glycol → glycolic acid)

AG = (Na+K) − (Cl+HCO₃), normal 12–24 mEq/L. Elevated AG = unmeasured anions. DKA ketones (β-OHB + acetoacetate), uremia (phosphate, sulfate), lactic acidosis (shock), EG (glycolic acid). High AG = find the source.

UA Q3. List the components of nephrotic syndrome and the life-threatening complication.
▼
Proteinuria + hypoalbuminemia + edema/ascites + hypercholesterolemia. Life-threatening: thromboembolism (loss of antithrombin III in urine).

Glomerular disease → massive protein loss → low oncotic pressure → third-space fluid. Compensatory hepatic lipoprotein synthesis → hypercholesterolemia. ATIII lost → hypercoagulable → pulmonary thromboembolism. Confirm: UPC >2.0 in dogs = severe; renal biopsy for cause.

UA Q4. A cat has marked high-AG metabolic acidosis, hypocalcemia, azotemia, dilute urine, and glucosuria. Top differential?
▼
Ethylene glycol (antifreeze) toxicity — treat immediately with fomepizole or ethanol

EG → glycolic acid (high AG) → oxalic acid → chelates Ca (hypocalcemia) → CaOx monohydrate crystals in renal tubules → AKI (azotemia, isosthenuria). Glucosuria without hyperglycemia = Fanconi-like tubular damage. Cats develop signs at 3 hr (dogs 6 hr). Every hour of delay = more tubular necrosis. Treat: fomepizole IV, fluids, bicarbonate, calcium gluconate.

UA Q5. A 7-month-old Yorkie has altered mentation and ammonium biurate crystals on UA. What is the underlying condition?
▼
Portosystemic shunt (PSS) — ammonium biurate = ammonia + uric acid accumulating when liver bypassed

PSS → ammonia not converted to urea + uric acid not converted to allantoin → ammonium biurate crystals (thorn apple, brown spiky spheres) in alkaline urine. CNS signs from hyperammonemia post-meal. Confirmatory: markedly elevated post-prandial bile acids, low BUN, hypoalbuminemia, hypoglycemia. Small breeds (Yorkies, Maltese) most common.

❤️ Systemic Hypertension: IRIS/ACVIM staging by systolic BP, target organ damage (retinal, cardiac, renal, CNS), causes (CKD most common), measurement, and antihypertensive treatment (amlodipine, benazepril, telmisartan).

IRIS/ACVIM Blood Pressure Staging

StageSystolic BPRisk of TODAction
Normotensive<140 mmHgMinimalMonitor
Pre-hypertensive140–159 mmHgLowTreat underlying cause; recheck
Hypertensive160–179 mmHgModerateAntihypertensive therapy indicated
Severely hypertensive≥180 mmHgHighImmediate antihypertensive treatment
⚠️ Diagnosis requires ≥2 readings on ≥2 separate occasions (unless acute emergency with TOD). "White coat" effect is real — indirect Doppler measurement in calm environment.

Target Organ Damage (TOD)

👁 Ocular (most visible)

  • Retinal hemorrhage
  • Retinal detachment (sudden blindness)
  • Vitreal hemorrhage
  • Hyphema
  • Tortuous retinal vessels
  • Papilledema
Hypertensive blindness = emergency. Cats: most dramatic ocular changes.

❤️ Cardiac

  • Left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH)
  • Hypertensive cardiomyopathy (cats)
  • Systolic murmur
  • Gallop rhythm (S3 or S4)
  • Dyspnea from CHF

🫘 Renal & CNS

  • Glomerular injury → proteinuria → CKD progression
  • Hematuria
  • CNS: seizures, altered mentation, vestibular signs, intracranial hemorrhage
  • Epistaxis (nasal hemorrhage)

Causes of Systemic Hypertension

Secondary (most common in veterinary medicine)

CauseNotes
CKDMost common overall; 20–65% of CKD patients are hypertensive. Substaging criterion in IRIS.
Hyperthyroidism (cats)↑ cardiac output + direct vasoconstriction. Resolves with treatment of hyperthyroidism.
HyperadrenocorticismCortisol enhances vascular responsiveness. Up to 60% of Cushings dogs hypertensive.
Primary hyperaldosteronismAldosterone → Na/water retention → volume overload. Cats most common. Hypertensive blindness.
PheochromocytomaEpisodic catecholamine release → episodic severe hypertension. Episodes triggered by stress/manipulation.
Diabetes mellitusOsmotic effects, nephropathy, endothelial dysfunction.
Polycythemia↑ blood viscosity → ↑ BP. Treat with phlebotomy.

Antihypertensive Treatment

DrugClassUse
AmlodipineCalcium channel blockerFirst-line cats; very effective. Also dogs (esp. CKD-related).
Benazepril / EnalaprilACE inhibitorDogs: first-line for proteinuric CKD hypertension. Reduces GFR slightly but reduces proteinuria.
TelmisartanARB (angiotensin receptor blocker)Cats: FDA approved for hypertension + proteinuria. Alternative to ACE-i.
Prazosin / phenoxybenzamineAlpha-1 blockerPheochromocytoma — pre-operative; blocks catecholamine effects.
Target: systolic BP <160 mmHg. Avoid reducing too rapidly — end-organ hypoperfusion risk. In cats: amlodipine is far more effective than ACE-inhibitors alone.

Measurement & Interpretation Tips

  • Indirect Doppler preferred in small animals. Oscillometric less accurate in cats.
  • Measure cuff width = 30–40% of limb circumference. Too narrow → falsely high reading.
  • Allow 5–10 min acclimatization in quiet room. Take 5–7 consecutive readings, discard first, average remainder.
  • Record: method used, limb site, cuff width, patient demeanor.
  • Cats normal: <140 mmHg systolic. Dogs: <150 mmHg systolic (some use <160).
  • CKD substaging uses BP to assess TOD risk (IRIS 2019 guidelines).
👁 Ophthalmology & Ocular Clinpath: Ocular manifestations of systemic disease — hypertensive retinopathy, hyphema, retinal detachment, uveitis-associated lab findings, and aqueous humor analysis.

Ocular Signs of Systemic Disease — Key Associations

Ocular FindingSystemic CauseLab Clue
Retinal detachment (sudden blindness)Systemic hypertension (most common), primary hyperaldosteronism, pheochromocytoma, CKD, hyperthyroidism (cats)BP ≥180 mmHg; K low (hyperaldosteronism); T4 high (hyperthyroidism)
Retinal hemorrhageHypertension, coagulopathy (thrombocytopenia, DIC, rodenticide), hyperviscosity syndromes (polycythemia, dysproteinemia)BP; platelet count; PT/aPTT; PCV; protein electrophoresis
Hyphema (blood in anterior chamber)Systemic hypertension, coagulopathy, trauma, neoplasia, uveal vessel fragilityBP; coagulation panel; CBC
UveitisFIV, FeLV, FIP, Toxoplasma, Blastomyces, Leishmania, Leptospira, IMHA, lymphomaInfectious titers; FeLV/FIV snap; Coombs; cytology of aqueous
Corneal lipid depositsHyperlipidemia (hypothyroidism, DM, nephrotic syndrome, dietary)Cholesterol, triglycerides, T4, UPC
Anterior lens luxationInherited (Terriers), uveitis, trauma. Not a specific clinpath finding but associated conditions may have lab changesEvaluate underlying uveitis cause
PapilledemaHypertension, intracranial hypertension, lead toxicosisBP; lead levels; CSF if indicated
Pale fundus / gray retinaAnemia, retinal degeneration (SARD — sudden acquired retinal degeneration)PCV; ERG for SARD diagnosis

Hypertensive Retinopathy — Step by Step

Pathophysiology

1
Sustained high BP → retinal arteriole wall stress → arteriole wall thickening and vasoconstriction
2
Breakdown of blood-retina barrier → retinal edema, flame-shaped hemorrhages, exudates
3
Subretinal fluid accumulation → bullous or complete retinal detachment → sudden blindness
4
Vitreal hemorrhage, hyphema, secondary glaucoma in severe cases

Species Notes

  • Cats: Most dramatic retinal changes. Primary hyperaldosteronism → hypertensive blindness is a classic exam question. Hyperthyroidism is the second most common cause of feline hypertension.
  • Dogs: Adrenal tumors (pheo, HAC), CKD. Glomerulonephritis with protein loss can paradoxically lower BP (loss of RAAS substrate) — but nephropathy from any cause eventually → hypertension.
  • Prognosis for vision: Early treatment may restore vision if detachment is <24–48 hr and bullous (fluid between layers, not atrophic). Chronic detachment → permanent retinal atrophy.
Any cat presenting with ACUTE BLINDNESS must have blood pressure measured IMMEDIATELY — hypertensive retinal detachment is an ophthalmic emergency.

Aqueous Humor & Ocular Fluid Analysis

TestIndicationKey finding
Aqueous humor cytologySuspected intraocular neoplasia, fungal uveitis, lymphomaNeoplastic cells (lymphoblasts for lymphoma); fungal organisms; inflammatory cells
Aqueous/vitreous cultureSuspected endophthalmitis (bacterial or fungal)Positive culture confirms infection
Aqueous proteinUveitis (protein leaks from inflamed uvea)Elevated vs normal aqueous protein — confirms active uveitis
Tonometry (IOP)Glaucoma vs uveitisGlaucoma: IOP >25 mmHg. Uveitis: IOP low/normal. Both cause buphthalmos if chronic.
Schirmer Tear TestKeratoconjunctivitis sicca (dry eye)<10 mm/min = KCS; often secondary to distemper, sulfonamides, hypothyroidism
🔬 Urinalysis Deep Dive: Reference tables · SediVue® image atlas · Conventional microscopy image atlas.
🔬 Urinalysis Deep Dive: Complete physical, chemical, and sediment exam — collection methods, USG thresholds, dipstick parameters, urine sediment (casts, crystals, cells), and interpretation framework.

Collection Methods & Sample Handling

MethodBest UseKey Limitation
Free-catch (midstream)USG; large animals; home collectionContaminated — NOT for culture. Contaminated cells/bacteria from distal urogenital tract.
CatheterizationQuantitative culture if cystocentesis not possible20% UTI risk in female dogs (single catheterization). Trauma → ↑RBCs/epithelial cells.
CystocentesisGold standard — culture & sedimentMust avoid friable bladder. Mild iatrogenic hematuria. Sample of choice.
Manual expressionNoneNOT recommended — retrograde bacterial reflux into ureters/kidneys.

USG Thresholds by Species

Dogs>1.030 = adequate concentration
Cats>1.040 = adequate concentration
Horses>1.025 = adequate
Cattle>1.025 = adequate
Isosthenuria1.008–1.012 = tubules NOT modifying filtrate
Hyposthenuria<1.008 = tubules actively DILUTING (ADH absent/ignored; tubules DO work)
Morning samples: best for concentrating ability + highest cast/crystal concentration. Process within 30 min or refrigerate (warm to room temp before analysis).

Dipstick — Parameter by Parameter

ParameterPrincipleKey Interpretation Points
ProteinAlbumin-specific colorimetricFalse + in alkaline urine (horses, ruminants — normal alkaline diet). Interpret with USG: 1+ at SG 1.008 = significant; trace at SG 1.040 = minor. Males dogs: trace-1+ in concentrated urine = normal (renal tubular conjugation of bilirubin). Confirm with UPC ratio (>0.5 dog, >0.4 cat = significant).
GlucoseGlucose oxidase reactionSpecies renal thresholds: dog 180 mg/dL; cat 280 mg/dL; horse 160 mg/dL; cow 100 mg/dL; bird 600 mg/dL. Glucosuria WITHOUT hyperglycemia = renal tubular disease (Fanconi syndrome, Basenji primary glucosuria).
KetonesNitroprusside reactionDetects acetoacetate and acetone — NOT beta-hydroxybutyrate (most abundant in DKA but others present). Positive in DKA, starvation, ketosis. A negative ketone does NOT rule out DKA in early stages.
BilirubinDiazonium salt reactionONLY conjugated bilirubin passes glomerulus. ANY bilirubinuria in CATS = significant (cats rarely produce CB). Male dogs: trace–1+ in concentrated urine = normal (hepatic bilirubin conjugation and tubular excretion). Bilirubinuria appears BEFORE serum bilirubin rises — early cholestasis marker.
Occult bloodPeroxidase-like activity of hemePositive = hematuria OR hemoglobinuria OR myoglobinuria. Differentiate: Hematuria — urine clears on centrifuge, RBCs visible in sediment, plasma clear. Hemoglobinuria — urine does NOT clear, no RBCs, PINK plasma. Myoglobinuria — no RBCs, CLEAR plasma, CK markedly elevated.
pHMethyl red + bromthymol blueMeat diet → acid urine. Vegetable/grain diet → alkaline urine (horses, ruminants: normally alkaline). UTI with urease-positive bacteria → alkaline. Paradoxical aciduria with metabolic alkalosis (pyloric obstruction).
Leukocyte esteraseEsterase hydrolysisUNRELIABLE in animals — many false negatives in dogs, false positives in cats. Do NOT use for UTI diagnosis in veterinary medicine. Use sediment (WBC count) instead.
NitriteBacterial nitrate reductionUNRELIABLE in animals — many false negatives. Useful in humans only. Do not use in veterinary practice.
USG by dipstickIonic concentrationUNRELIABLE — do NOT use in animals. Use refractometer (temperature-corrected).

Urine Sediment — Casts

Casts form in tubular lumens. They are cylindrical molds of tubular contents. Their presence and type indicate the severity and chronicity of renal disease. Best seen with low light (reduce condenser).
Cast TypeCompositionSignificance
HyalinePure protein (Tamm-Horsfall mucoprotein)Occasional in normal concentrated urine. Non-specific. Increased with fever, dehydration, exercise, proteinuria.
GranularDegenerated cell debris from tubular epithelial cellsAcute tubular injury/necrosis (ATN). Indicates active renal parenchymal damage. Always pathologic.
Cellular — RBCRBCs embedded in protein matrixGlomerular disease with bleeding into tubular lumen. Rare but significant — indicates glomerulonephritis.
Cellular — WBCNeutrophils in protein matrixPyelonephritis (ascending bacterial infection). Inflammatory tubular disease.
Cellular — EpithelialShed renal tubular epithelial cellsAcute tubular necrosis, nephrotoxicosis.
WaxyHyaline cast degeneration in stagnant tubulesAdvanced chronic kidney disease. Very stagnant urine flow = severe tubular atrophy.
FattyLipid droplets in matrixCats (normally high lipid in urine); hypothyroidism, DM, nephrotic syndrome.

Urine Sediment — Crystals

CrystalpHMorphologySignificance
Struvite (Mg ammonium phosphate)AlkalineCoffin-lid shaped prisms, colorlessCommon; urease-positive UTI (Staph, Proteus); struvite uroliths
Calcium oxalate monohydrateAcid to neutralDumbbell or "picket fence" shapeEthylene glycol toxicity (dogs 6 hr, cats 3 hr post-ingestion). Also idiopathic.
Calcium oxalate dihydrateAcid to neutralEnvelope/Maltese cross shapeCommon incidental finding; also seen in EG tox; hypercalciuria
Urate (ammonium biurate)Alkaline/neutralThorn-apple or spiky sphere, yellow-brownPortosystemic shunt (cannot convert uric acid → allantoin without liver); also normal Dalmatians
CystineAcidFlat hexagonal platesHereditary cystinuria (tubular reabsorption defect); cystine uroliths; Labrador Retrievers, Newfoundlands
BilirubinAcidOrange-amber needles, stackedBilirubinuria; normal in concentrated male dog urine (trace)
Calcium carbonateAlkalineLarge yellow spheres or dumbbellsNormal in horse and rabbit urine (dietary)
Calcium phosphateAlkalineAmorphous, colorlessAlkaline urine; less clinically significant

Urine Sediment — Cells & Other Findings

Cells

FindingSignificance
RBCs >5/HPFHematuria — trauma, UTI, urolithiasis, neoplasia, coagulopathy
WBCs >5/HPFPyuria — UTI, pyelonephritis, urethritis, prostatitis
Bacteria + WBCsActive UTI (culture to confirm)
Bacteria without WBCsContamination vs subclinical bacteriuria vs early infection
Transitional epithelial cellsNormal shedding vs catheter trauma vs TCC (transitional cell carcinoma — clusters + atypia)
Squamous epithelial cellsContamination from distal tract (free-catch samples)
Waxy castsChronic severe renal disease

Differentiate Red Urine

FeatureHematuriaHemoglobinuriaMyoglobinuria
Urine colorRed, clears on spinRed-brown, does NOT clearRed-brown, does NOT clear
RBCs in sedimentYes (>5/HPF)NoNo
Plasma/serum colorNormal (clear)Pink/redClear (normal)
CKNormalNormal/mild upMarkedly elevated (>10x)
CauseUrinary tract bleedingIV hemolysisRhabdomyolysis

Fig 16. Struvite

Fig 17. Amorphous (NMB wet prep on right)

Fig 18. Bilirubin

Fig 19. Ammonium biurate

Fig 20. Left, calcium oxalate monohydrate; right, calcium oxalate dihydrate

Fig 21. Drug (Tribrissen®) crystals, 10× objective

🔬 Miscellaneous

Fig 22. Left, fat droplets (red arrows, RBC); right, sperm

Fig 23. Pearsonema plica

Fig 24. Contaminant fragmented fiber

🧫 CP Cytology 1 · Dr. Sydney Hastain · VMP 941  |  5 topics: Intro & Collection → Approach → Inflammation → Neoplasia → Lymph Node

🧫 What Is Cytology?

Examination of cells under a microscope from organ/tissue, nodule/mass, or fluid. Cells classified as normal · hyperplastic · inflammatory ± infectious · neoplastic.

CytologyHistopath
InvasivenessMinimalMay be invasive
CostLowerHigher
Turnaround<1 hr possibleDays
Cellular detail✅ ExcellentLimited
Architecture❌ None✅ Intact
Definitive DxSometimesOften
💡 Cytology's biggest advantage: speed and minimal invasiveness.

Liver cytology — excellent cellular detail

Liver histopathology — architecture intact

🩺 Collection Techniques

TechniqueBest for
FNA — aspiration (22G + 5cc syringe)Poorly exfoliative: mesenchymal neoplasms, granulomas
FNA — non-aspiration / woodpecker (22G, no syringe)Easily exfoliative, vascular, or fragile/delicate cells
Impression smear / touch imprintExudative skin lesions; biopsy or necropsy
Tissue scrape, swab, tapeSurface, ear canal, skin lesions
Wash (prostate, urinary bladder, respiratory tract)Luminal or lavage samples
Centesis (abdominal, pleural, pericardial)Body cavity effusions — EDTA tube first!
💧 Fluid handling: (1) EDTA/purple top → cytology; (2) red top → biochemistry (triglycerides, creatinine); (3) culture. Make fresh smears immediately — delayed prep causes cell degeneration and bacterial overgrowth.
⚠️ Impression smear: blot excess blood with gauze FIRST — blood obscures cells.

FNA preparation setup

🔀 Slide Preparation

MethodResult
Wedge — same as blood smear, feathered edgeGood for free cells
Squash prep ✅ — drop → press gently → pull horizontallyWell-spread, even distribution — diagnostic
Splat smear ❌ — press and immediately liftThick clumps, poor staining — non-diagnostic
🔴 Splat smear is the single most common reason cytology is non-diagnostic.

Squash ✅ vs splat ❌ — macro

Squash vs splat — microscopy

🎨 Slide Staining

StainAdvantageDisadvantage
DiffQuik (aqueous Romanowsky)In-practice; better canine distemper inclusionsMast cell granules stain POORLY
Wright-Giemsa (methanolic Romanowsky)Granules stain wellSlower; lab-based
🔴 Suspect MCT but no granules on DiffQuik? → Send for Wright-Giemsa.

Mast cells: WG (purple granules) vs DiffQuik (pale)

Canine distemper inclusions: WG vs DiffQuik

📋 Tips for Successful Submission

TipDetail
Spread all specimensEven small samples — use a light touch
Make multiple slidesSubmit them all; stain one or two to check quality but still submit those
Label all slidesPatient name, site, patient ID, date
Provide signalment + historyDescription of lesion, when first noticed, progression (e.g., dermal, subcutaneous, intraabdominal)
Package appropriatelyAdequate padding; ice pack for fluid samples; separate from formalin biopsy jars

🔭 Approach to Cytologic Evaluation

1. Is sample adequate?
→
2. Low power (4×,10×)
Cell types, arrangement, background, large organisms
→
3. High power (50×,100×)
Nuclear detail, small organisms
→
4. Normal or abnormal?
→
5. Classify
✅ 40× objective requires COVERSLIP — NO OIL. Oil immersion: 50× and 100× objectives only.

Sample Adequacy

✅ AdequateWell-spread, well-stained, intact cells, no artifacts
❌ Too thickCells squished; staining too dark
❌ LysedCells ruptured; non-diagnostic
❌ ArtifactFormalin, US gel, keratin scrolls, stain precipitate
⚠️ Inflammation can cause epithelial/mesenchymal dysplastic change — always look for concurrent inflammation before calling malignancy!

❌ Too thick / dark staining

✅ Well-spread, intact, stained

🎭 Artifacts

Formalin artifact

Ultrasound gel

Stain precipitate + bacteria

Keratin scrolls (fingerprint)

ArtifactAppearance / Key Clue
FormalinWashed-out, pale, nuclear detail lost — from formalin exposure before staining
Ultrasound gelAmorphous pink/purple material obscuring cells
Stain precipitateDark granular deposits — look like organisms! Use oil to confirm
Keratin scrollsCurled translucent sheets from fingerprint on slide

🗂️ Cytologic Pattern Decision Tree

Inflammatory Patterns

PatternThreshold
Suppurative/neutrophilic>70% neutrophils
Granulomatous/macrophagic>50% macrophages
Pyogranulomatous50–70% neuts + 30–50% mononuclear
Eosinophilic>10% eosinophils
Lymphocytic/lymphoplasmacyticSmall lymphs ± plasma cells
MixedNo single type dominates

Non-inflammatory Categories

CategoryKey clue
EpithelialCohesive clusters, distinct boundaries, polygonal
Round cellIndividual, round, discrete — CoM unreliable!
MesenchymalSpindle/stellate, indistinct, poorly exfoliative
NeuroendocrineNaked nuclei in sea of cytoplasm — CoM unreliable!

🔎 Clues to Etiology by Inflammation Pattern

PatternKey Differentials to Consider
Suppurative / neutrophilicBacteria (especially if degenerate neuts); sterile irritants, auto-immune, trauma, ruptured cysts, neoplasia (if non-degenerate)
Macrophagic / pyogranulomatousFungal; foreign body; higher-order bacteria (Mycobacterium, Nocardia, Actinomyces); algal/oomycetes (Prototheca, Pythium)
EosinophilicHypersensitivity/allergic (atopy, food allergy, flea bite, arthropod); parasites; algal/oomycetes (Prototheca, Pythium)
Lymphocytic / lymphoplasmacyticAllergic or immune reaction (e.g., recent vaccination); early viral infection; chronic inflammation
🔴 Always check for organisms in any inflammatory sample — especially when neutrophils are degenerate or macrophages dominate.

🔥 Neutrophilic / Suppurative (>70% neutrophils)

TypeNucleusCauses
Non-degenerateNormal segmentationSterile irritants, auto-immune, trauma, ruptured cysts, neoplasia
DegenerateSwollen, loses segmentation — sometimes unrecognizableBacterial infection → "septic suppurative" · Other infectious
🐛 Degenerate neutrophils = BUG HUNT! Tissue finding ONLY — not in blood.
Toxic ≠ Degenerate:
Toxic = BLOOD = cytoplasmic changes (basophilia, vacuoles, Döhle bodies)
Degenerate = TISSUE = nuclear swelling from local toxic environment

Non-degenerate neutrophils

Degenerate neutrophils + intracellular bacteria

Toxic change (blood smear) vs degenerate change (tissue aspirate) — different locations, different change types. Do not confuse them.

Toxic change — blood smear

Degenerate change — tissue aspirate

🔸 Mixed Inflammation

Mixed — neutrophilic · eosinophilic · macrophagic · lymphocytic

No single inflammatory cell type predominates. Always describe all cell types present.

🟠 Macrophagic / Granulomatous (>50% macrophages)

Infectious: Protozoal (Leishmania, Toxoplasma), higher-order bacteria (Mycobacterium, Nocardia, Actinomyces), fungal (Histoplasma, Blastomyces, Cryptococcus, Coccidioides)

Non-infectious: Foreign body (grass awns, vaccine adjuvant), acral lick dermatitis, late/resolving inflammation

Granulomatous — macrophages, vacuolated cytoplasm

Multinucleated giant cell (macrophage fusing)

🟤 Pyogranulomatous (50–70% neuts, 30–50% mononuclear)

Causes: foreign body or fungal infection; chronic or resolving inflammation. Mononuclear cells = macrophages, giant cells, lymphocytes, plasma cells, mast cells.

Pyogranulomatous inflammation

Pyogranulomatous close-up

🟢 Eosinophilic (>10%) & 🔵 Lymphocytic / Lymphoplasmacytic

Eosinophilic

Infectious: parasites, algae/oomycetes (Pythium, Prototheca)
Non-infectious: eosinophilic plaque/granuloma, flea/food/atopy, arthropod bite

Lymphocytic / Lymphoplasmacytic

Primarily small lymphocytes ± plasma cells. Causes: allergic/immune reaction, early viral, chronic inflammation.

Eosinophilic inflammation

Lymphocytic/lymphoplasmacytic inflammation

Plasma cells in lymphoplasmacytic inflammation — note perinuclear clear zone (Golgi) and deep blue cytoplasm.

Lymphoplasmacytic — plasma cells close-up

🍄 Infectious Organisms — ID Guide (FYI)

OrganismSize / LookKey Feature
MycobacteriumFilamentous rods — negative-stainingAcid-fast positive (pink)
Histoplasma2–5 µm, thin non-staining wallIntracellular; budding
Blastomyces6–15 µm, double wallBroad-based budding
Cryptococcus2–20 µm, non-staining capsuleNarrow-based budding
Coccidioides20–200 µmDoes NOT bud; spherules
Fungal hyphaeVariableSeptate, right-angle branching

Fungal hyphae

Cryptococcus — narrow budding

Blastomyces — broad budding

Histoplasma + Coccidioides

🔬 Criteria of Malignancy (CoM)

🔴 CoM are reliable for epithelial and mesenchymal ONLY — NOT round cell or neuroendocrine tumors. Inflammation causes dysplastic change — always check for concurrent inflammation!
CriterionDefinition
AnisocytosisVariation in cell size
AnisokaryosisVariation in nuclear size
Multinucleation>1 nucleus per cell
Irregular nuclear shapeAbnormal contour
Nuclear moldingNucleus hugs adjacent nucleus
Prominent nucleoliLarge, visible, irregular
Multiple / Anisonucleoliosis>1 nucleolus; variable sizes
Abnormal mitotic figuresAsymmetric, tripolar
Cytoplasmic vacuolationFrothy cytoplasm
Foreign cells / metastasise.g., epithelial cells in LN

Anisocytosis · anisokaryosis · binucleation · prominent nucleoli · bizarre mitotic figure — Dx: Osteosarcoma

🗂️ 4 Cytomorphologic Categories

CategoryKey featuresCoM?
EpithelialCohesive clusters; distinct boundaries; polygonal; well exfoliative✅
Round CellIndividual discrete; round; distinct borders; well exfoliative❌
MesenchymalSpindle/stellate; indistinct; pink matrix; poorly exfoliative✅
Neuroendocrine"Free nuclei in sea of cytoplasm"; very fragile; acinar-like❌

Raskin Table 2.2 — 4 cytomorphologic categories

🔷 Epithelial & Mesenchymal Examples

Epithelial

Trichoblastoma (benign)

Sebaceous adenoma (benign)

Carcinoma (malignant)

Carcinoma (malignant)

Mesenchymal

Lipoma (benign)

Liposarcoma

Sarcoma (malignant)

⭕ Round Cell Tumors — L Y M P H

CoM unreliable for all round cell tumors — diagnose by morphology, not malignancy criteria.
L

Lymphoma

Monomorphic intermediate-to-large lymphocytes; high N:C; scant cytoplasm. Homogeneity is key. CoM unreliable.

Lymphoma

Y

TVT

Pale blue cytoplasm; round, clear, distinct cytoplasmic vacuoles — KEY feature. Responds to vincristine.

TVT

M

Mast Cell Tumor

Small dark purple granules; dogs: eosinophils + fibroblasts. DiffQuik stains granules poorly!

Mast Cell Tumor

Mast Cell Tumor

P

Plasmacytoma

Deep blue cytoplasm + perinuclear clear zone; 'soccer ball' chromatin; binucleated/multinucleated.

Plasmacytoma

Plasmacytoma

H

Histiocytoma

'Fried egg' — darker blue near nucleus fading to periphery; eccentric oval nucleus; spontaneously regresses.

Histiocytoma

Histiocytoma

🧠 Neuroendocrine Tumors

"Free nuclei in a sea of cytoplasm" — cells are very fragile and mostly rupture. Naked nuclei in cohesive-like or acinar-like groups. CoM unreliable — malignant tumors can look bland!

Examples: insulinoma, chemodectoma, pheochromocytoma, C-cell (thyroid) tumor.

Neuroendocrine — naked nuclei

Neuroendocrine — acinar-like

🫘 Lymph Node Evaluation

Normal

Population: Small lymphs predominate (>80–90%); occasional intermediate/large; rare plasma cells

Other: Low numbers non-degen neuts, macrophages, eosinophils

→ Normal

Normal

Reactive / Hyperplastic

Population: Small lymphs still predominate (>50%); increased intermediate/large (>10–20%); ± increased plasma cells

Other: Antigenic stimulation

→ Investigate: infectious · inflammatory · immune · neoplastic

Reactive / Hyperplastic

Lymphoma

Population: Uniform intermediate-to-large lymphs (>50%); fine chromatin; prominent nucleoli; deeper blue cytoplasm

Other: CoM unreliable; monomorphic is key

→ Neoplastic — lymphoma

Lymphoma

Reactive LN — Plasma Cells

Reactive nodes show increased plasma cells. Key features: perinuclear clear zone (Golgi apparatus) + deep blue cytoplasm + eccentric nucleus + "soccer ball" chromatin.

Plasma cells — perinuclear clear zone

⚠️ Lymphadenitis Patterns

TypeKey Cell / ThresholdDDx
Suppurative>5% neutrophilsBacterial, fungal
Eosinophilic>3% eosinophilsParasites, allergy
Macrophagic/mixedMacrophages > occasionalFungal, foreign body
Metastatic neoplasiaForeign cell population (e.g., epithelial cells)Identify primary tumor type
📏 Lymphocyte sizing: Small ≤ 1.5× RBC (~7 µm dog). Intermediate = 1.5–2×. Large >2×.
⚠️ Key rule: Normal → Reactive = small lymphs still dominate. Reactive → Lymphoma = large lymphs take over (>50%) and are monomorphic.
🫁 Cavitary Effusions (CP-2): Fluid collection & submission, gross evaluation, protein/cell count, classification framework, 10 effusion types, large animal normals, and confirmatory testing.

Fluid Collection & Submission

Submission Steps

#StepRationale
1EDTA tube (purple top) firstPrevents clotting; for cytology
2Excess → red top (no additive)For biochem (TG, Cr, bili) and culture
3Make fresh smears immediatelyAvoids cell degeneration & bacterial overgrowth
4Refrigerate fluid only (not smears)Preserves cells in fluid; smears air-dry
5Label slides: name, MR#, date, siteChain of custody, accurate reporting
6Submit signalment + history + imagingEssential clinical context for interpretation

4-Part Fluid Analysis

ComponentMethod / Details
Gross evaluationColor + turbidity — first clue to etiology
Total proteinRefractometer (g/dL). CSF: biochemical analysis (mg/dL) — too dilute for refractometer
Cell countsTNCC + RBC via hematology analyzer. Viscous (joints) or very low-cellularity (CSF) → hemacytometer. WBC diff: count 100 leukocytes on cytology slide.
Cytologic evalDifferential + cell morphology + organism ID

Gross Color Key

ColorSuggests
Clear / pale yellowNormal
Bright yellowUrine (uroabdomen)
RedIatrogenic or true hemorrhage
Yellow-green to dark greenBile
White / "strawberry milk"Chyle

Gross appearance: hemorrhagic (red, left) vs chylous (white, right)

⚠️ Turbidity clues: Clear = normal. Turbid/flocculated = ↑cellularity, bacteria, fibrin, or ingesta (GI rupture/accidental enterocentesis).

Key Cell Types in Effusions

CellNotes
MacrophagesMost common in low-grade effusions; perform erythrophagocytosis in hemorrhagic
Non-degenerate neutrophilsIntact nuclear segmentation; non-septic inflammation
Degenerate neutrophilsNuclear swelling/karyolysis → septic effusion (bacteria)
Small lymphocytesPredominant in chylous effusions
Mesothelial cellsLine serosal cavities; reactive forms = bizarre, can mimic neoplasia!
Eosinophils / mast cellsUncommon; parasites, hypersensitivity

Cell types: macrophage (top), reactive mesothelial cells, non-degenerate neutrophils

Effusion Classification Framework

4 mechanisms of abnormal fluid accumulation: (1) ↓ plasma oncotic pressure → pure transudate, (2) ↑ hydrostatic pressure / venous congestion → modified transudate, (3) ↑ vascular permeability (inflammation) → exudate, (4) lymphatic obstruction/rupture → chylous.
TypeGrossTP (g/dL)TNCC (/µL)Dominant CellsHallmark / Confirm
Pure transudateClear, pale yellow<2.5<1,500Macrophages + sparse lymphs + few non-degen neuts↓albumin or ↑hydrostatic pressure
Modified transudatePale yellow, slightly cloudy>2.5<5,000Mixed: macros + non-degen neuts + lymphs ± mesothelialOngoing pure transudate → ↑permeability
Exudate (non-septic)Yellow/white/tan/orange, cloudy>2.5>5,000Predominantly non-degen neutrophils, fewer macrosRule out infection; FIP, bile, urine, neoplasia
Exudate (septic)Yellow/tan, turbid>2.5>5,000Degenerate neutrophils + intracellular bacteriaDegenerate neuts + bacteria = diagnostic
FIPYellow-green, viscous, fibrinous>4.0<5,000Mix neuts + macros; pink stippled backgroundDisproportionally HIGH protein : LOW cells
HemorrhagicRed to red-brown, opaque>2.5>5,000Blood + erythrophagocytic macros + hemosiderin + hematoidin crystalsPCV >5–10%; erythrophagocytosis = true heme
ChylousWhite/pink ("strawberry milk"), hazy>2.5 (often unreadable)<10,000Small lymphocytes predominateFluid TG > serum TG (usually >3:1 or >100 mg/dL)
GI ruptureVariable: brown/green/tan, turbid>2.5>5,000Degen neuts + macros + mixed bacteria + plant materialFeed material + mixed flora = diagnostic
BiliousGreen-brown to red-brown, hazy-opaque>3.0>5,000Non-degen neutrophils + bile pigmentFluid bili > serum bili (2:1)
UroperitoneumPale yellow → progresses; hazyInitially low, progresses >2.5Initially low, progresses >5,000Non-degen neutrophils; rarely urine crystalsFluid Cr > serum Cr (2:1)
NeoplasticVariable all parametersVariableVariableVariable; lymphoma/carcinoma exfoliate bestIdentify neoplastic cells on cytology
🔑 FIP key rule: HIGH protein + LOW cell count = FIP until proven otherwise. This "protein:cell dissociation" is the single most useful cytologic pattern for FIP.

Transudates

Pure Transudate (Protein-Poor)

FeatureValue
GrossColorless or pale yellow, clear
Total protein<2.5 g/dL
TNCC<1,500 cells/µL
CytologyMostly macrophages, some small lymphocytes, low non-degen neuts

Mechanisms

MechanismExamples
↓ oncotic pressure (albumin <1 g/dL)Liver failure, protein-losing nephropathy, protein-losing enteropathy
↑ hydrostatic pressure (systemic HTN)Congestive heart failure, renal failure, excessive fluids
Venous congestionL or R-sided heart failure, hepatitis/hepatopathy, venous thrombus, compressive mass, organ torsion/volvulus

Pure transudate cytology: macrophages, low cellularity

Modified Transudate (Protein-Rich)

FeatureValue
GrossPale yellow, clear to slightly cloudy
Total protein>2.5 g/dL
TNCC<5,000 cells/µL
CytologyMix of macrophages, non-degen neuts, lymphs ± mesothelial cells
Mechanism: Chronic/ongoing pure transudate → ↑cavitary pressure → mesothelial irritation → ↑vascular permeability → higher TP & TNCC. If persistent → can progress to exudate.

Modified transudate: mixed cellularity with mesothelial cells

Exudates

FeatureValue
GrossYellow, white, tan, orange; slightly cloudy to flocculent
Total protein>2.5 g/dL
TNCC>5,000 cells/µL
CytologyPredominantly neutrophils, fewer macrophages, small lymphs ± mesothelial cells

Mechanism: Increased vascular permeability

CategoryCausesCytology clue
SepticBacterial, viral, fungalDegenerate neutrophils + intracellular bacteria → must rule out sepsis
Non-septicNecrosis, foreign body, organ rupture (GB, UB), neoplasiaNon-degen to mildly degen neuts
ProgressionLong-standing modified transudateProgressive ↑ TNCC over time
🔑 Septic exudate: Degenerate neutrophils (karyolytic/swollen nuclei) + intracellular bacteria = septic. Always look for bacteria inside neutrophils — extracellular alone is insufficient.

Left: non-septic exudate (non-degen neuts + mesothelial cells). Right: septic exudate (degen neuts + intracellular bacteria)

Special Fluid Types

FIP Effusion

FeatureValue
GrossYellow to green; viscous, fibrinous, slightly cloudy
TP>2.5 g/dL, often >4.0 g/dL
TNCCUsually <5,000/µL
CytologyMix neuts + macros; pink stippled proteinaceous background
Mechanism↑ vascular permeability + hyperglobulinemia
🔑 FIP hallmark = protein:cell dissociation. Disproportionately HIGH protein relative to LOW cell count. If TP >4 with TNCC <5,000 → strongly suspect FIP.

FIP fluid: yellow-green, viscous

FIP cytology: pink stippled proteinaceous background

Hemorrhagic Effusion

FeatureValue
GrossRed to red-brown, opaque
TP>2.5 g/dL
TNCC>5,000/µL
RBCMillions/µL; PCV usually >5–10%
CytologyLarge amounts of blood; erythrophagocytic macros; hemosiderin-laden macros; hematoidin crystals
Cause
Ruptured hemangiosarcoma
Coagulopathy
Trauma / hematoma
True hemorrhage vs iatrogenic: True = erythrophagocytosis + hemosiderin + hematoidin crystals (subacute/chronic breakdown products). Iatrogenic tap = no erythrophagocytosis, clears on serial centesis.

Hemorrhagic effusion: erythrophagocytic macrophages, hemosiderin (dark blue), hematoidin crystals (yellow)

Chylous Effusion

FeatureValue
GrossWhite to white-pink, hazy to opaque ("strawberry milk")
TP>2.5 g/dL; often unreadable (chylomicrons interfere)
TNCC<10,000/µL
CytologySmall lymphocytes predominate; neuts/macros may approach 50% with chronicity
MechanismObstruction or rupture of lymphatics → chylomicrons (triglyceride-rich) leak

Common causes:

  • Cardiomyopathy (cats)
  • Mediastinal masses (neoplasia, abscess, granuloma)
  • Lung lobe torsion
  • Diaphragmatic hernia
  • Trauma (thoracic duct rupture)
  • Idiopathic
🔑 Confirm: Fluid TG > serum TG (usually >3:1 or fluid TG >100 mg/dL)

Chylous effusion: white-pink tubes (left); cytology shows small lymphocytes predominating with erythrophagocytosis from concurrent hemorrhage (right)

GI Rupture

FeatureValue
GrossVariable: yellow/tan/brown/green, turbid to opaque
TP>2.5 g/dL
TNCC>5,000/µL
CytologyDegen neuts, macros, mixed intracellular and extracellular bacteria, plant/feed material
CausesTrauma, obstruction/strangulation, foreign body, neoplasia
🔑 Feed material (plant cells) + mixed flora + degen neuts = pathognomonic for GI rupture. Yeast organisms may also be present.

GI rupture: degenerate neutrophils, plant material (blue rod, inset), intracellular bacteria, budding yeast

Bilious Effusion (Biliary Rupture)

FeatureValue
GrossGreen-brown to red-brown, hazy to opaque
TP>3.0 g/dL
TNCC>5,000/µL
CytologyPredominantly non-degenerate neutrophils + fewer macros + bile pigment
CausesHepatic/biliary neoplasia, trauma, foreign body
🔑 Confirm: Fluid bilirubin > serum bilirubin (usually 2:1). Bile is sterile → non-degenerate neuts (vs GI rupture = degenerate).

Bilious effusion: bile pigment (brown granular material) in macrophages and free; green-brown fluid in syringe

Uroperitoneum

FeatureValue
GrossVariable: yellow / yellow-brown / red-brown; hazy to opaque
TP (early)Initially LOW (urine-like) → progresses to >2.5 g/dL
TNCC (early)Initially LOW → progresses to >5,000/µL as inflammation develops
CytologyNon-degen neutrophils + fewer macros; rarely urine crystals
CausesTrauma, neoplasia, urolithiasis
🔑 Confirm: Fluid creatinine > serum creatinine (2:1). Creatinine equilibrates across peritoneum slowly (vs urea, which equilibrates rapidly). Early cases look like dilute urine — progression to exudate is the classic evolution.
Electrolyte changes in uroabdomen: Body absorbs urine electrolytes across peritoneum → serum K↑, Na↓, Cl↓. Use these serum changes as supporting evidence alongside fluid Cr >2× serum Cr.

Neoplastic Effusion

FeatureValue
Gross / TP / TNCC / RBCAll variable — depends on mechanism
CytologyMay or may not see neoplastic cells. Lymphoma & carcinoma most common (exfoliate best).

Mechanisms (multiple possible):

MechanismEffusion type produced
Venous compression → ↑ hydrostatic pressureModified transudate
Vessel erosion/invasionHemorrhagic
Tumor necrosis → inflammationExudate
Cytokines → ↑ vascular permeabilityExudate
Lymphatic compressionChylous

Neoplastic effusion: lymphoma (left — large, round lymphoblasts) vs carcinoma (right — cohesive epithelial clusters)

Large Animal Peritoneal Fluid

Normal large animal fluid is indistinguishable from a transudative effusion on lab values alone — clinical assessment of total volume and fluid character (physical exam, imaging) determines if the volume is abnormal.
ParameterHorsesCattleCamelids
ColorPale yellowPale yellowPale yellow
TurbidityClearClearClear
TP (g/dL)<2.5<2.5<2.5
TNCC (/µL)<5,000<5,000<3,000
CytologyMacrophages + neutrophilsMacrophages + neutrophils ± eosinophilsPredominantly neutrophils + fewer macrophages

Equine Colic — Progressive Changes

Lesion severityFluid change
Early / venous congestionModified transudate (↑TP, mildly ↑TNCC)
Ischemia / inflammationExudate (↑↑TP, ↑↑TNCC, degen neuts)
GI ruptureExudate + bacteria + feed material

Cattle Peritonitis

Variable fluid findings — may see typical ↑TP & ↑TNCC; if compartmentalized (walled-off abscess), may only see ↑TP without significant ↑TNCC. Always correlate with clinical signs.

🃏 Cytology Image Flashcards

Tap any card to flip. 18 cards total.

What type of neutrophils? What does this finding mean?

tap to reveal

Non-degenerate Neutrophils

  • Normal nuclear segmentation
  • Sterile irritants, auto-immune, trauma, neoplasia
  • NOT bacterial

Identify the cell type. What must you do next?

tap to reveal

Degenerate Neutrophils + Intracellular Bacteria

  • Nuclear swelling + loss of segmentation = degenerate
  • Intracellular bacteria = SEPTIC
  • BUG HUNT!
  • Tissue finding only (NOT blood)

Blood smear or tissue aspirate? What change is shown?

tap to reveal

Toxic Change — Blood

  • Cytoplasmic changes: basophilia, vacuolization, Döhle bodies
  • In BLOOD
  • Toxic = cytoplasmic, degenerate = nuclear

Name this inflammation pattern. Top causes?

tap to reveal

Granulomatous / Macrophagic

  • >50% macrophages
  • Fungal (Histoplasma, Blastomyces, Cryptococcus, Coccidioides)
  • Mycobacterium
  • Protozoal
  • Foreign body

Name this pattern and list causes.

tap to reveal

Pyogranulomatous Inflammation

  • 50–70% neuts + 30–50% mononuclear
  • Foreign body, fungal infection, chronic/resolving inflammation

What inflammation type? Common causes?

tap to reveal

Eosinophilic Inflammation

  • >10% eosinophils
  • Parasites, oomycetes
  • Eosinophilic plaque/granuloma, atopy, arthropod bite

Name the criteria of malignancy visible. Diagnosis?

tap to reveal

Criteria of Malignancy — Osteosarcoma

  • Anisocytosis
  • Anisokaryosis
  • Binucleation
  • Prominent nucleoli
  • Bizarre mitotic figure
  • CoM for epithelial/mesenchymal only

Identify this round cell tumor. Key feature?

tap to reveal

Lymphoma

  • Monomorphic intermediate-to-large lymphocytes
  • High N:C
  • Fine chromatin
  • Homogeneity is key
  • CoM unreliable

Identify this round cell tumor. Pathognomonic feature?

tap to reveal

TVT

  • Round, clear, distinct cytoplasmic vacuoles — KEY
  • Pale blue cytoplasm
  • Responds to vincristine

Identify this round cell tumor. Staining pearl?

tap to reveal

Mast Cell Tumor (MCT)

  • Dark purple cytoplasmic granules
  • DiffQuik stains granules POORLY — send for Wright-Giemsa
  • CoM unreliable

Identify this round cell tumor. Describe the cytoplasm.

tap to reveal

Plasmacytoma

  • Deep blue cytoplasm + perinuclear clear zone
  • 'Soccer ball' chromatin
  • Binucleated/multinucleated
  • CoM unreliable

Identify this tumor. Unique behavior?

tap to reveal

Histiocytoma

  • 'Fried egg' appearance — blue near nucleus, fading outward
  • Eccentric oval nucleus
  • Spontaneously regresses

Tumor type? Key cytologic pattern?

tap to reveal

Neuroendocrine Tumor

  • 'Free nuclei in a sea of cytoplasm'
  • Very fragile
  • Acinar-like groups
  • CoM unreliable — malignant can look bland

Describe this lymph node. % small lymphocytes?

tap to reveal

Normal Lymph Node

  • Small lymphs predominate (>80–90%)
  • Occasional intermediate/large
  • Rare plasma cells
  • Normal size

Normal, reactive, or lymphoma? Key distinction?

tap to reveal

Reactive / Hyperplastic Lymph Node

  • Enlarged
  • Small lymphs still >50%
  • Increased intermediate/large (>10–20%)
  • ± Plasma cells

Normal, reactive, or lymphoma? Key feature?

tap to reveal

Lymphoma

  • Uniform intermediate-to-large lymphs (>50%) — MONOMORPHIC
  • Prominent nucleoli
  • CoM unreliable

Identify this organism. Key feature?

tap to reveal

Cryptococcus neoformans

  • 2–20 µm
  • Non-staining capsule (clear halo)
  • NARROW-based budding
  • Granulomatous inflammation

Identify this organism. Distinguishing feature?

tap to reveal

Blastomyces dermatitidis

  • 6–15 µm
  • Double cell wall
  • BROAD-based budding
  • Pyogranulomatous inflammation
